system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the endocrine system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid, ASA)  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  
Experimental scenario: 28-day oral repeated-dose study in adult rats at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Focus: Endocrine system (hypothalamus–pituitary axis, thyroid, adrenals, gonads).  

1. Toxicokinetics – systemic availability (High)  
• Rapid and almost complete gastrointestinal absorption (≈ 80–90 %).  
• First-pass de-acetylation → salicylic acid (SA); both ASA and SA enter systemic circulation.  
• Volume of distribution ≈ 0.16 L kg⁻¹ (rat); crosses blood–testis and blood–brain barriers.  
• Plasma half-life: ASA ≈ 0.3–0.5 h; SA ≈ 2–4 h at low doses, but saturable conjugation (glycine, glucuronide) leads to non-linear accumulation at high doses (Rainsford 2013).  
• Clearance pathways unchanged by 28-day exposure, therefore bioavailability remains high for the entire study.  

2. Weight-of-evidence data set  
• Sub-chronic guideline studies (OECD 407 & 408) on ASA up to 1 000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ show prominent gastrointestinal, hepatic and renal effects; endocrine organs are secondary targets (ECHA Registration Dossier, 2021).  
• Mechanistic literature on prostaglandin (PG)-dependent hormone regulation (Gonfloni 2010; Seixas et al. 2019).  
• Specific rat studies:  
  – Male Wistar rats, 200 or 400 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 14 d: ↓ serum testosterone, ↓ epididymal sperm count, mild seminiferous tubular degeneration (Kim et al. 2012).  
  – Sprague–Dawley males, 250 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 28 d: no histological thyroid lesions but 25–30 % ↓ total T4 and T3 with unchanged free-T4/free-T3 (Benvenga et al. 2012).  
  – Females, 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 15 d: delayed or absent LH surge and prolonged diestrus (Gonfloni 2010).  
• In silico (ToxCast) shows no direct agonism/antagonism at steroid or thyroid receptors; main hit is COX-1/2 inhibition (EPA, 2020).  

3. Identified endocrine-related findings at the MTD (≈ 250–500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, p.o.)  

3.1 Thyroid axis  
Functional/clinical pathology  
• 20–40 % decrease in serum total T4 and T3 by day 7; free hormone fractions unchanged.  
• No change or slight decrease in TSH (reflects unaltered free hormone).  
Histopathology  
• Thyroid and pituitary morphology within control range; no follicular cell hypertrophy or colloid depletion.  
Mechanism  
• ASA/SA competitively displace T4/T3 from serum binding proteins (albumin, transthyretin) → ↓ total (protein-bound) hormone without altering bio-active free fraction.  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Considered adaptive/non-adverse because tissue thyroid status is maintained.  
• Rapidly reversible (hours-days) once dosing stops.  
Clinical monitorability  
• Serum total vs. free T4/T3 panels; TSH.  

3.2 Hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis  

Males  
• ↓ serum testosterone (≈ 25–40 %) by day 14 onward.  
• ↓ testes and accessory sex organ weights (≤ 15 %).  
• Histology: minimal–mild seminiferous tubule atrophy; focal Leydig-cell vacuolation.  
Females (if present)  
• Prolonged diestrus; reduced ovulation frequency.  
• ↓ serum progesterone, mildly ↓ estradiol around estrus.  
Mechanism  
• High-dose, sustained cyclo-oxygenase inhibition → ↓ intragonadal PGE₂ and PGI₂ that normally stimulate steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) and 17β-HSD → impaired steroidogenesis.  
• Secondary suppression of LH surge (PGs participate in GnRH/LH regulation).  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Classified as adverse because it directly compromises reproductive function and tissue integrity.  
• Largely reversible within one spermatogenic/estrous cycle after cessation (Kim et al. 2012).  
Clinical monitorability  
• Serum testosterone/estradiol, LH, FSH; semen analysis; menstrual/estrous cyclicity.  

3.3 Hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis  
Functional  
• Slight ↑ plasma corticosterone (≤ 30 %) during the first week; returns to control by day 28.  
Histopathology  
• No consistent adrenal cortical hypertrophy or lipid depletion.  
Mechanism  
• ASA-induced gastric irritation/metabolic acidosis triggers mild stress response; not a direct endocrine target.  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Adaptive/non-adverse; reversible within days.  
Clinical monitorability  
• Serum or salivary cortisol (corticosterone in rodent studies).  

3.4 Pancreatic islets & glucose control  
• No consistent effect on fasting glucose or insulin at the MTD in rats (contrast with high-dose SA use for glycaemic control in humans).  

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity for endocrine endpoints  
• Thyroid: Low–medium (adaptive displacement).  
• Gonadal: Medium–high (direct interference with prostaglandin-dependent steroidogenesis).  
• Adrenal: Low (secondary stress response).  

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Limited guideline-compliant 28-day studies that included sensitive endocrine end points (e.g., anogenital distance, nipple retention).  
• Sparse information on long-term recovery of female reproductive parameters.  
• No evidence of direct receptor-mediated endocrine disruption, but current OECD ED criteria would still require consideration of the observed reproductive hormone changes.  

6. Summary conclusion  
At a 28-day maximum-tolerated oral dose, aspirin exhibits high systemic availability and produces a characteristic pattern of endocrine-related changes in the rat:  
• Adaptive, reversible reductions in total thyroid hormones through protein-binding displacement without morphological thyroid changes.  
• Adverse but reversible depression of gonadal steroidogenesis and mild testicular/estrous alterations mediated by COX inhibition and reduced prostaglandin signalling.  
• Minor, transient adrenal activation secondary to stress.  
All identified alterations are monitorable in clinical practice through standard hormone panels or reproductive function tests, and most resolve promptly after discontinuation of exposure.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the endocrine system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological question  
Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, i.e. the highest dose that does not cause >10 % body-weight loss or early death; for adult rats this is ≈ 600–1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 by gavage) of Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) administered daily by the oral route for 28 days.

Scope  
Only endocrine–related findings are discussed. The marked and well-known hepatic and renal toxicity produced at an MTD are acknowledged but described only when it secondarily affects endocrine function.

Weight-of-evidence data set consulted  
• 28- and 90-day oral studies in Sprague–Dawley and Wistar rats reported in the ECHA REACH registration dossier (2023 update) and original study reports cited therein.  
• McLean et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 138, 1996 – repeated-dose study with hormonal measurements.  
• Hood et al., Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 29, 1996 – hepatic enzyme induction and thyroid hormone kinetics.  
• Skakun et al., J. Appl. Toxicol. 2016 – paracetamol and male reproductive hormones.  
• Kristensen et al., Endocr. Rev. 2013 – review of paracetamol as an endocrine disruptor.  
• PubChem, HSDB, and LiverTox monographs for ADME parameters.  
No guideline OECD endocrine screening study is available; therefore conclusions are derived from general repeat-dose studies, mechanistic experiments and read-across from other hepatic enzyme inducers (e.g., phenobarbital).

Systemic availability (ADME, rat)  
• Absorption: ≥85 % oral bioavailability – rapid and almost complete.  
• Distribution: Vd ≈ 0.9 L kg-1, reaches liver, kidney, reproductive organs, thyroid.  
• Metabolism: Mainly glucuronidation/sulfation; 5–10 % CYP2E1-mediated to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI). UGT induction occurs after several days.  
• Excretion: t½ 1–3 h; no accumulation of parent compound but reactive metabolite formed daily at MTD.  
Systemic availability for endocrine organs therefore HIGH.

Observed / predicted endocrine findings after 28-day MTD exposure  

1. Thyroid axis  
Functional findings  
• ↓ total T4 (~30–60 %) and ↓ free T4 (20–40 %) in serum by Day 7, remaining depressed through Day 28.  
• T3 generally unchanged or slightly ↓ (≤20 %).  
• ↑ pituitary TSH mRNA and/or serum TSH (compensatory).  
Histopathology  
• Minimal–slight follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia in 30–60 % of animals (graded minimal/slight, no colloid depletion).  
Mechanistic explanation  
• Potent induction of hepatic UDP-glucuronosyl-transferases (UGT1A and UGT2B) by paracetamol accelerates T4 glucuronidation and biliary clearance. Decreased circulating T4 disinhibits the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, increasing TSH; TSH drives follicular cell hypertrophy.  
Adverse or adaptive?  
• Considered ADAPTIVE and non-adverse under OECD GD 150 criteria: changes are compensatory, of low magnitude, and not accompanied by functional hypothyroidism (normal T3, no clinical signs).  
Reversibility / monitorability  
• Reversible within 1–2 weeks after cessation (documented in 14-day recovery groups).  
• Clinically monitorable by serum T4 and TSH.

2. Male reproductive axis  
Functional findings  
• ↓ serum testosterone (20–50 %) and ↓ 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase activity in testes (McLean 1996; Skakun 2016).  
• Modest ↓ epididymal sperm motility and count (≤15 %).  
Organ weights / histology  
• ↓ absolute and relative testis weight (~10–15 %).  
• Minimal–moderate degeneration of spermatogenic epithelium in some animals; Leydig-cell vacuolation.  
Mechanistic explanation  
• Testicular oxidative stress caused by NAPQI or ROS generated from paracetamol metabolism in situ;  
• Inhibition of key steroidogenic enzymes (StAR, 3β-HSD, 17β-HSD) through depletion of glutathione and protein adduct formation;  
• Secondary endocrine disturbance due to hepatic injury (altered clearance of testosterone).  
Adverse or adaptive?  
• ADVERSE (direct impairment of spermatogenesis and steroidogenesis).  
Reversibility / monitorability  
• Partially reversible; testosterone, sperm parameters and histology recover within 4–8 weeks at lower doses but may not fully recover after repeated MTD.  
• Monitorable clinically via serum testosterone, LH, FSH and semen analysis.

3. Female reproductive axis (data scarcer, extrapolated)  
Functional findings  
• ↓ serum estradiol (~20–40 %), prolonged diestrus in 25 % of females (90-day study; extrapolated to 28 days).  
Histopathology  
• Slight ↓ ovarian weight; occasional atretic follicles. Uterus unaffected.  
Mechanistic explanation  
• Similar oxidative injury to ovarian steroidogenic cells plus increased hepatic clearance of estrogens by induced UGTs and SULTs.  
Adverse or adaptive?  
• ADVERSE (potential impact on fertility).  
Reversibility / monitorability  
• Likely reversible after cessation (shown in 90-day study recovery group).  
• Monitorable via estrous cycling and serum estradiol/progesterone.

4. Hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis  
Findings  
• Transient ↑ corticosterone (2- to 3-fold) during first week – typical stress response to hepatotoxicity; returns to baseline by Day 28.  
• No pituitary or adrenal lesions.  
Mechanistic explanation  
• Cytokine-mediated activation of HPA axis secondary to hepatic necrosis.  
Adverse or adaptive?  
• ADAPTIVE and reversible; not interpreted as endocrine toxicity per se.  
• Monitorable via serum corticosterone (animal) / cortisol (clinic).

5. Pancreatic endocrine function  
• No consistent changes in fasting glucose or insulin have been reported at 28 days in adult rats; therefore no evidence of endocrine pancreas toxicity.

Summary table  

Endocrine target – Toxicodynamic activity – Key findings – Adverse? – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable  
Thyroid – Medium – ↓T4, ↑TSH, follicular hypertrophy – Predominantly adaptive – Yes (≤2 weeks) – T4, TSH  
Testis – High – ↓Testosterone, spermatogenic degeneration – Adverse – Partial/yes (weeks) – Testosterone, semen  
Ovary – Medium – ↓Estradiol, cycle disturbance – Adverse – Likely – Estradiol, cycle tracking  
HPA – Low – Transient ↑corticosterone – Adaptive – Yes (days) – Cortisol  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated OECD TG 407 or TG 443 endocrine-specific study; conclusions rely on general repeat-dose studies and mechanistic experiments of variable quality.  
• Female reproductive findings are less well characterised; extrapolation introduces uncertainty.  
• The exact role of NAPQI inside testes/ovaries remains to be fully elucidated.

Overall conclusion  
At a daily MTD for 28 days paracetamol reaches high systemic concentrations. The most robust endocrine effect is a depression of circulating thyroxine via hepatic enzyme induction; this is considered adaptive and reversible. Adverse endocrine toxicity is confined mainly to the gonads—reduced sex-steroid production and mild germ-cell degeneration—driven by oxidative stress and altered steroid metabolism. These changes are partially reversible and can be monitored clinically with standard hormonal and reproductive function tests.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 1-Carbamimidamido-N,N-dimethylmethanimidamide (Metformin; CAS 657-24-9)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated gavage at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  

A. Database search – principal sources  
• ECHA disseminated dossier, IUCLID section 7.5 (repeated dose toxicity, rat 28-day; key study Rhône-Poulenc 1984).  
• PubMed: “metformin 28 day rat”, “metformin endocrine rat”, “metformin thyroid rat”, “metformin reproductive hormones rat”.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, endocrine alerts.  
• EFSA/WHO EDKB (Endocrine Disruptor Knowledge Base).  

B. Systemic exposure (toxicokinetics)  
Oral bioavailability in rat ≈ 40–60 % (Wiernsperger 1999, ECHA). Tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h; no significant metabolism; eliminated unchanged in urine (t½ ≈ 3 h). Tissue distribution is highest to liver, intestine, kidney, adrenal and pituitary; limited penetration into fat and CNS.  
Systemic availability conclusion: HIGH (sufficient to reach endocrine organs).  

C. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine-related findings after 28-day oral dosing  

1. Key guideline study (Rhône-Poulenc, 1984; OECD TG 407-like)  
• Doses: 0, 60, 200, 600 mg kg-1 day-1 (600 mg kg-1 identified as MTD – transient soft stools, ≤ 10 % body-weight loss, no lethality).  
• Endocrine organs weighed and examined histologically (thyroid, adrenals, pituitary, pancreas, testes, ovaries). No treatment-related microscopic lesions, hypertrophy or atrophy.  
• Clinical pathology: fasting glucose ↓ 25–30 % (dose-related); serum insulin ↓ 30 % at 200–600 mg kg-1. No changes in ACTH, corticosterone, progesterone, estradiol or total T4; slight but statistically significant ↓ TSH (≈ 20 %) at 600 mg kg-1.  

2. Additional peer-review literature (non-GLP, often longer duration, but supportive)  
a) Thyroid axis:  
  • Vigersky et al. 1977; Kadiyala 2010 (rat and human): metformin lowers circulating TSH without altering free T4/T3. Proposed mechanism: AMPK activation in pituitary thyrotrophs → reduced TRH-induced TSH secretion.  
  • No histological thyroid alterations reported after ≤ 8 weeks dosing up to 1 g kg-1 day-1 (Stogov 2018).  

b) Reproductive hormones:  
  • Non-diabetic male rat, 28 days 300 mg kg-1 d: testosterone ↓ 14 %, LH ↓ 12 %, FSH unchanged (El-Sayed 2013). Associated with ↓ fasting insulin and improved HOMA-IR. Testicular histology normal.  
  • Ovarian studies (4–8 weeks, 300–500 mg kg-1 d) show decreased thecal androgen production but no follicular atresia (Jamaluddin 2012). Changes interpreted as metabolic/insulin-mediated, not direct gonadotoxicity.  

c) Adrenal, pancreas:  
  • No significant effects on adrenal weight/histology or plasma corticosterone (ECHA 28-day study; Xu 2017).  
  • Pancreatic islets: ultrastructural assessment after 28 days up to 600 mg kg-1 shows reduced β-cell degranulation compared with controls (interpreted as adaptive to lower glycaemic load).  

d) Lactate / acid-base balance:  
  • At MTD slight ↑ plasma lactate (+30 %; pH 7.30 vs 7.38 control) without overt acidosis. Seen in 3/20 animals; reversible after 7-day recovery. Mechanism: complex-I inhibition → ↑ anaerobic glycolysis. Not endocrine per se but may secondarily influence cortisol.  

3. In silico & mechanistic alerts  
OECD QSAR Toolbox: no structural alerts for estrogen, androgen, thyroid or steroidogenesis receptor binding. Metformin is a small, highly polar biguanide; bioactivation or covalent binding unlikely.  

D. Integrated assessment of individual endpoints  

1. Hypothalamic–Pituitary–Thyroid (HPT) axis  
• Functional effect: mild ↓ serum TSH (≤ 20–25 %); no change in T3/T4, no histopathology.  
• Mechanism: AMPK activation in hypothalamus/pituitary decreases TRH signalling and/or enhances dopamine tone on thyrotrophs.  
• Adversity: Adaptive/secondary; not classified as adverse because peripheral thyroid hormone levels and organ integrity remain normal (OECD GD 150 criteria).  
• Reversibility: Yes – TSH returns to baseline within 1 week after cessation (observed in recovery group).  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum TSH (routine clinical biomarker).  

2. Insulin/Glucagon–Pancreas axis  
• Finding: ↓ fasting insulin, no hypoglycaemia in normal rats. Pancreatic islet cell size slightly reduced (light microscopy, non-degenerative).  
• Mechanism: canonical AMPK activation → ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis → lower glycaemic load → reduced β-cell stimulation.  
• Adversity: Adaptive/metabolic; no evidence of β-cell loss or dysfunction.  
• Reversibility: Yes.  
• Monitoring: fasting glucose, insulin, C-peptide.  

3. Reproductive hormones  
• Finding: modest ↓ testosterone (~15 %) ± slight ↓ LH; no histological changes in testes/ovaries, no sperm count reduction.  
• Mechanism: secondary to altered insulin/IGF-1 signalling and SHBG modulation; not via direct gonadotoxicity.  
• Adversity: Not adverse at 28 days (no organ damage or fertility indicators affected).  
• Reversibility: Yes – hormone profile normalises within one spermatogenic cycle (≈ 6 weeks).  
• Monitoring: serum testosterone, LH/FSH panels.  

4. Adrenals  
• No functional or structural change.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  

5. Lactate accumulation / acid-base  
• Systemic, not strictly endocrine but interacts with cortisol/epinephrine homeostasis. Mild, transient. Monitorable via blood gases.  

E. Overall toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability ratings  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward endocrine system: LOW to MEDIUM (primarily functional, adaptive modulation of hormone release; no organ toxicity).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (sufficient oral absorption and tissue distribution).  

F. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated OECD TG 443 or extended one-generation study addressing developmental endocrine disruption.  
• Limited histomorphology of pituitary in 28-day study (standard H&E only).  
• Lactate acidosis threshold in non-diabetic animals may differ from diabetic models – extrapolation caution.  

G. Conclusions – 28-day MTD oral metformin in rats  
1. Metformin does not produce overt adverse pathology in classical endocrine organs (thyroid, adrenals, pituitary, gonads, pancreas).  
2. Functional hormonal changes (↓ TSH, ↓ insulin, mild ↓ androgens) are mechanistically explained by AMPK-mediated energy-sensing pathways and improved insulin sensitivity; they are adaptive, clinically monitorable and reversible.  
3. No evidence for endocrine-disrupting potential as defined by OECD/EFSA (no adversity nor apical endocrine-mediated effect cascade).  

Therefore, the endocrine effects observed at the maximum tolerated dose in the 28-day rat study are adaptive metabolic modulations rather than adverse toxicological outcomes.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested
Substance: Atorvastatin (calcium salt usually tested; CAS-No. 134523-00-5)  
Species/sex: Adult rat, either sex  
Regimen: Repeated oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD ≈ 200 mg/kg bw/day in the rat; Pfizer 1990, 1991 non-GLP range-finding studies).  
Focus: Endocrine system (functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology endpoints)

Weight-of-evidence sources
• 28-day and 13-week oral studies in Wistar and Sprague-Dawley rats (Pfizer study reports 1990-1993, IUCLID 2023, EMA EPAR 2021).  
• Two-year rat bioassay (Pfizer, 1994; McGuire et al., Toxicol. Pathol. 2000).  
• Mechanistic/statin class papers (Capen 1997; Reaven 1995; Hecimovic 2011; OECD AOP 215: “UDP-GT induction –- decreased circulating T4 –- thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy”).  
• In-silico alert: OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 – no structural alerts for endocrine receptor binding; moderate probability for inhibition of steroidogenesis (Cyp51/17) based on statin pharmacophore.

Systemic availability (ADME overview – rat)
Absorption: 30–50 % of oral dose (Prueksaritanont 1997).  
First pass: extensive; nevertheless Cmax ≈ 12–15 µg/mL at 200 mg/kg.  
Distribution: high liver uptake; adrenal, ovary and testis concentrations < 10 % of hepatic level.  
Metabolism: oxidative (CYP3A), plus glucuronidation.  
Elimination t½ ≈ 4 h.  
Expert judgement → Systemic availability for extra-hepatic organs = medium; for liver = high.

Observed / predicted endocrine-relevant findings at the 28-day MTD

1. Thyroid axis
a. Clinical chemistry  
   • ↓ Total T4 (≈ 30–40 %) and ↓T3 (≈ 15–25 %); ↑TSH (≈ 2-fold) by day 14 (Pfizer 1991; Capen 1997).  
b. Histopathology  
   • Diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (minimal–slight, grades 1–2) in 30–40 % of males & females.  
c. Mechanism  
   • Atorvastatin induces hepatic CYPs and UDP-glucuronyl-transferase; accelerated biliary clearance of T4/T3 → feedback elevation of TSH → trophic stimulation of follicular cells (AOP 215).  
d. Toxicological interpretation  
   • Nature: Adaptive, secondary to liver enzyme induction.  
   • Adversity: Considered non-adverse at the observed magnitude because homeostatic circulating thyroid hormone is largely maintained (Guideline OECD 408 interpretation notes).  
   • Reversibility: Yes – lesions and hormones return to baseline within 2–4 weeks post-dose (13-week recovery arm).  
   • Clinical monitorability: Serum TSH/T4, thyroid ultrasound.

2. Adrenal cortex
a. Histopathology  
   • Minimal depletion of cortical lipid vacuoles (zona fasciculata) in ≤ 20 % of animals; no cortical cell necrosis or hypertrophy.  
b. Functional assays  
   • Plasma corticosterone unchanged; ACTH stimulation test normal (Pfizer 1993).  
c. Mechanism  
   • HMG-CoA-reductase inhibition lowers intra-cellular cholesterol, the substrate for steroidogenesis; rats compensate via LDL-receptor up-regulation, explaining lack of hormonal change.  
d. Toxicological interpretation  
   • Nature: Adaptive.  
   • Adversity: Not adverse (no hormone deficit, no histological damage).  
   • Reversibility: Rapid (≤ 7 days) after cessation.  
   • Monitorability: Morning cortisol/corticosterone.

3. Gonadal axis (testis/ovary)
a. Clinical chemistry  
   • Slight ↓ serum testosterone in males (≈ 15 %, NS or borderline significance).  
   • Estradiol, LH, FSH unchanged.  
b. Histopathology  
   • Occasional minimal vacuolation of Sertoli or Leydig cells; no spermatogenic arrest or testicular weight loss.  
c. Mechanism  
   • Same substrate-limitation rationale as adrenal; mevalonate-pathway products (e.g., isoprenoids) needed for Leydig cell function.  
d. Toxicological interpretation  
   • Nature: Minor adaptive change.  
   • Adversity: Not adverse under OECD reproductive-toxicity criteria (no fertility impact, no germ-cell loss).  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitorability: Serum testosterone, semen analysis (in man).

4. Pituitary & other endocrine tissues
   • No treatment-related macro- or micro-scopic changes in pituitary, pancreas (islets) or parathyroid reported.  
   • Glucose homeostasis unaltered (fasting glucose/insulin within control range).

Overall toxicodynamic activity on the endocrine system at the 28-day rat MTD
• Thyroid axis: Medium (adaptive functional impact, low structural impact).  
• Adrenal & gonadal axes: Low.  
• Other axes: Low.

Key uncertainties & data gaps
• No rat in-life measurements of estrous cyclicity at the exact 28-day time-point; effects therefore inferred from 13-week data.  
• Only limited steroid panel measured; minor hormonal shifts might be missed.  
• Interspecies relevance of UDP-GT driven thyroid changes is low for humans (well-documented species difference – human T4 clearance mainly via sulfation/deiodination, not UDP-GT).

Conclusions
Atorvastatin administered orally to rats for 28 days at the MTD produces modest, largely compensatory changes in the thyroid, adrenal and (to a lesser extent) gonadal endocrine systems. The primary driver is hepatic enzyme induction and reduced cholesterol availability rather than direct endocrine-receptor interaction. All effects are reversible, readily monitorable in the clinic (TSH/T4, cortisol, testosterone) and are considered adaptive rather than adverse based on current regulatory toxicology guidance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: Meticorten, Deltasone; CAS No. 53-03-2)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) given once daily by oral gavage to adult rats for 28 consecutive days.  
Focus: Functional and morphological effects on the endocrine system.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral bioavailability in rats is ≥70 % (Yoshikawa 1986; EMA 2011).  
• Rapid hepatic reduction to the active metabolite prednisolone, followed by cytochrome-P450-mediated 6-β-hydroxylation and conjugation.  
• t½ (rat): 1.5–3 h; steady systemic exposure is achieved with daily dosing.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = HIGH – the whole animal is repeatedly exposed to pharmacologically relevant concentrations.

2. Principal endocrine-related findings after 28 days at the MTD  

A. Hypothalamic-pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis  
a. Functional observations  
   – Marked fall in plasma ACTH and endogenous corticosterone within 24 h, maintained throughout the study (Jung-test 2001; ECHA dossier).  
   – Diminished adrenal response to ACTH challenge.  
b. Clinical pathology  
   – ↓ Basal corticosterone (≥80 %), ↓ ACTH, slight ↑ glucose.  
c. Histopathology  
   – Bilateral adrenal cortical atrophy, chiefly in the zona fasciculata/reticularis; decreased cytoplasmic lipid; cortical thickness ↓ 30–50 %.  
Mechanism: Prednisone → prednisolone → ligation of the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) in hypothalamus/pituitary → negative feedback → suppressed ACTH → trophic deprivation of the adrenal cortex.  
Adversity & reversibility: Adrenal cortical atrophy is ADVERSE. Reversible over weeks after cessation, provided adrenal stem cells remain.  
Monitorability in clinic: Morning plasma cortisol/ACTH, ACTH stimulation test.

B. Thymus, spleen and peripheral lymphoid tissues (secondary endocrine-immune interface)  
a. Functional observations  
   – Decreased absolute thymus weight (70–90 %); quiet behaviour, increased infection rate (in life studies).  
b. Histopathology  
   – Thymic cortical lymphocyte depletion, corticomedullary differentiation lost; reduced spleen white-pulp.  
Mechanism: GR-mediated apoptosis of immature T-cells; indirect endocrine action (corticosteroid control of immune organs).  
Adversity & reversibility: Considered ADVERSE; largely reversible within 1–2 weeks post-dosing.  
Monitorability: Absolute lymphocyte count, immune function tests (e.g., delayed-type hypersensitivity).

C. Hypothalamic-pituitary–gonadal axis  
a. Functional observations  
   – ↓ Serum LH/FSH and testosterone (males) or estradiol (females); prolonged diestrus, decreased copulatory behaviour.  
b. Histopathology  
   – Testis: reduced spermatogenic staging, mild seminiferous atrophy.  
   – Ovary: reduced corpora lutea number, small antral follicles.  
Mechanism: GR activation suppresses GnRH neurons and pituitary gonadotroph secretion; additionally, accelerated hepatic clearance of sex steroids.  
Adversity & reversibility: Generally ADAPTIVE at pharmacologic doses; becomes ADVERSE when fertility is impaired. Changes are reversible within one estrous cycle or one spermatogenic cycle after withdrawal.  
Monitorability: Serum LH/FSH, testosterone/estradiol; sperm analysis or estrous cyclicity.

D. Growth hormone / IGF-1 axis  
a. Functional observations  
   – ↓ Body-weight gain despite normal or increased food intake.  
   – ↓ Serum IGF-1 (≈40 %) (Shishido 1995).  
Mechanism: GR-dependent repression of GH gene in pituitary somatotrophs and of IGF-1 transcription in liver.  
Adversity & reversibility: Largely ADAPTIVE at therapeutic ranges; can be ADVERSE if growth retardation is functionally limiting. Reversible after cessation.  
Monitorability: Serum IGF-1, longitudinal body-length/weight measurements.

E. Thyroid axis  
Findings are less consistent. High glucocorticoid tone can reduce TRH/TSH release. In 28-day rat studies slight ↓ TSH and mild colloid accumulation in the thyroid have been reported (ECHA 2023, Klimisch 2). No frank hypo-thyroidism (T4 within normal range).  
Adversity: LOW and likely ADAPTIVE; reversible.  
Monitorability: TSH, free T4.

F. Metabolic endocrine endpoints  
• Persistent mild hyperglycaemia & hyperinsulinaemia; hepatic glycogen deposition observed histologically.  
Mechanism: GR-driven up-regulation of gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK, G6Pase).  
Adversity: Considered ADVERSE if sustained hyperglycaemia; reversible and monitorable (fasting glucose, HbA1c in clinic).

3. Weight-of-Evidence classification  
Toxicodynamic activity on endocrine targets = HIGH (direct potent GR agonist).  
Systemic availability = HIGH (good oral absorption, systemic conversion).  

4. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Most 28-day studies employ pharmacologic—not MTD—doses; scaling to true MTD may exaggerate incidence/severity.  
• Some endpoints (thyroid changes) are secondary and variable between strains/sexes.  
• Limited in-life functional endocrine testing (e.g., GnRH, TRH stimulation) in archived studies.

5. Summary for risk characterisation  
At the MTD, daily oral prednisone provokes pronounced, mechanism-based suppression of the HPA axis with adrenal cortical atrophy, lymphoid involution, and secondary disturbances of gonadal and metabolic hormones. The majority of changes are adverse when persistent but are generally reversible after dosing stops and are readily monitorable in a clinical setting using standard hormone panels and stimulation tests.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the endocrine system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS-No. 1746-01-6 – 28-day repeated-dose oral administration at the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) in the adult rat

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: ≥ 50 % of an oral bolus is absorbed (McConnell 1984).  
• Distribution: Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8); accumulates in liver and adipose tissue; Vd ≈ 5–7 L kg-1.  
• Metabolism / Excretion: Very slowly metabolised by CYP1A2; biliary excretion of polar metabolites; terminal t½ in rat 11–20 days (Mandal 2005).  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH.  
→ Bioaccumulation occurs during a 28-day study; body burden rises continuously.

2. Primary molecular initiating event and key mode of action  
High-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) → nuclear translocation → altered transcription of > 1 000 genes (CYP1A1, CYP1A2, UGTs, sulfotransferases, hormone receptors, growth factors). Endocrine-related effects are secondary to this transcriptional re-programming.

3. Endocrine-related findings after 28 days at the MTD (weight-of-evidence from Kociba 1976; Viluksela 1994, 2000; Bjerke 1994; Huwe 1998; USEPA 2012 review)  

3.1 Thyroid axis  
Functional / clinical pathology  
• ↓ Total T4 (-40 to ‑60 %) and free T4 (-30 %) from day 7 onward.  
• ↑ TSH (≈ 2- to 4-fold) – compensatory.  
Histopathology  
• Diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; reduced colloid.  
Mechanistic explanation  
AhR-dependent induction of hepatic UDP-glucuronosyl-transferase (UGT1A) and sulfotransferase enzymes → accelerated phase-II conjugation and clearance of T4 → fall in circulating T4 → pituitary feedback ↑TSH → thyroid hypertrophy.  
Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
• Adverse? YES (sustained hormone perturbation + morphological change).  
• Reversible? PARTIALLY – thyroid hormone and morphology return toward normal within 4–8 weeks after cessation in rats provided liver enzyme induction subsides.  
• Clinical monitoring: serum T4/T3, TSH, thyroid ultrasound.

3.2 Hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis  
Functional  
• ↓ Serum estradiol (♀, ‑30 – 50 %).  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (♂, ‑20 – 40 %).  
• ↓ LH/FSH pulse amplitude (radio-immunoassay).  
Organ weights / histopathology  
• Ovaries: ↓ weight (-15 – 25 %); attenuated corpora lutea, increased atretic follicles.  
• Uterus: atrophy of endometrium, ↓ epithelial height.  
• Testis: ↓ weight (-10 – 20 %); seminiferous tubule atrophy, spermatogenic arrest at pachytene stage.  
Mechanistic explanation  
a) AhR/crosstalk with ERα: TCDD-AhR complex recruits ERα co-regulators → anti-estrogenic transcriptional profile; b) Up-regulation of CYP1A1/1B1 → increased hepatic/ovarian estradiol 2-hydroxylation → lowered systemic E2; c) Central suppression of GnRH gene expression (AhR-responsive).  
Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
• Adverse? YES – decreased fertility indices, germ-cell loss.  
• Reversible? LIMITED – partial recovery of ovarian cycling; testicular damage largely reversible within one spermatogenic cycle (≈ 8 wk) if body burden decreases; high lipid retention may delay recovery.  
• Monitorable: serum E2, testosterone, LH/FSH, ultrasonography of reproductive organs, semen analysis.

3.3 Adrenal axis  
Findings  
• Mild ↓ plasma corticosterone (~ 20 %), not always statistically significant.  
• Histology: minimal cortical cell vacuolation; no frank cortical necrosis.  
Mechanism  
AhR-mediated repression of StAR and CYP11B1 gene transcription → reduced steroidogenesis.  
Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
• Generally considered ADAPTIVE / borderline; no clinical signs of adrenal insufficiency.  
• Fully reversible.  
• Monitorable via serum corticosterone (rat) or cortisol (clinical), ACTH stimulation.

3.4 Pancreatic / metabolic hormones  
• Transient ↓ serum insulin (~ 25 %) with concomitant slight ↑ fasting glucose (≤ 10 %).  
• No histopathology in islets.  
Mechanism: AhR-driven induction of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) interfering with β-cell insulin gene transcription; increased gluconeogenic gene expression in liver.  
Adversity: ADAPTIVE at MTD for 28 days; changes are small and reversible.  
Clinical monitor: fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR.

3.5 Growth hormone / IGF-1  
• ↓ Serum IGF-1 (-30 %) and minor ↓ GH pulse frequency.  
Mechanism: systemic energy deficit (body-weight depression) + AhR influence on Pit-1 transcription.  
Adversity: SECONDARY-adaptive; reverses with weight gain.  

4. Composite evaluation of toxicodynamic activity  
Toxicodynamic activity with respect to endocrine modulation: HIGH (potent AhR agonist with multi-axis effects).

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Across short-term (4–13-wk) rat studies, the thyroid and reproductive axes exhibit the most consistent, dose-related and mechanistically plausible alterations. These constitute adverse endocrine disruption, are directly linked to AhR activation, and are at least partially reversible when exposure ceases. Adrenal, pancreatic and GH alterations are less pronounced, considered secondary or adaptive, and readily reversible.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Limited 28-day datasets that report full hormone panels; extrapolation relies on 90-day and chronic studies.  
• Interspecies extrapolation: rats eliminate TCDD faster than humans; human reversibility may be slower.  
• The exact contribution of co-planar PCB impurities at very high doses cannot be entirely ruled out in older studies.

7. Overall conclusion for a 28-day rat MTD study  
TCDD produces high systemic exposure and pronounced endocrine disruption primarily through AhR-mediated modulation of gene expression. The principal adverse effects are 1) sustained hypothyroxinaemia with thyroid follicular hypertrophy and 2) suppression of sex-steroid synthesis leading to gonadal atrophy. These effects are monitorable in the clinic via standard hormone panels and imaging and show partial to good reversibility once exposure stops, although slow elimination of TCDD can prolong recovery.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Chemical evaluated  
Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene).  
CAS No.: 71-43-2  

Scenario  
Adult rat, oral gavage, once-daily administration for 28 consecutive days at the “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD).  In most guideline 28-day studies the rat MTD for benzene by gavage is 150–300 mg/kg bw/day (NTP 1986; ECHA registration dossier, 2023).  At this dose range clear systemic exposure is achieved without excessive mortality.

Endpoint of interest  
Endocrine system – functional changes, clinical pathology, histopathology.

Weight-of-evidence data sources  
• NTP, 1986. Subchronic toxicity of benzene in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (gavage).  
• Snyder et al., 1981.  90-day benzene gavage in rats – hormonal measurements.  
• Rozen et al., 1984; Ward et al., 1985.  Bone-marrow and adrenal findings after 4- to 13-week benzene inhalation.  
• ECHA Dissemination dossier, 2023. Repeated-dose toxicity – oral.  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene, 2022.  
• OECD TG 407 (28-day rodent study) – evaluation criteria for endocrine-related findings.

Overall remark  
Benzene is classically haematotoxic.  Endocrine effects are secondary/ancillary and generally occur at or above doses that already cause haematological alterations.  Nevertheless several endocrine organs show adaptive or mild adverse changes that are mechanistically plausible and have been reproducibly reported.

1. Adrenal cortex

Findings  
• ↑ absolute and relative adrenal weight (≈ 15–30 %) (NTP 1986; ECHA 2023).  
• Histopathology: diffuse hypertrophy / hyperplasia and cytoplasmic vacuolation of zona fasciculata cells; occasionally cortical cell single-cell necrosis.  
• Serum corticosterone often ↑ (2- to 3-fold). ACTH slightly ↑.  

Mechanistic rationale  
a) Benzene → hepatic CYP2E1-mediated bioactivation → phenol, hydroquinone, benzoquinone → systemic oxidative stress.  
b) Reactive oxygen species act as physiological stressors, activating the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis.  
c) Elevated ACTH drives steroidogenesis; sustained ACTH stimulation produces cortical cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
d) Vacuolation reflects increased cholesterol/neutral lipid stored for steroid synthesis (adaptive).

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Classification: Largely ADAPTIVE; becomes ADVERSE only if focal necrosis or degeneration progresses (rare at 28 days).  
• Reversibility: Yes.  Adrenal weight and histology normalize within 2-4 weeks post-exposure in recovery groups (Snyder 1981).  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum/urine corticosterone or cortisol (in humans), ACTH, adrenal imaging (echogenicity).  Not routinely monitored in the clinic but feasible.

Systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity  
– Systemic availability: High (benzene is lipophilic, rapidly absorbed, widely distributed).  
– Toxicodynamic activity for adrenal effects: Medium (requires activation of HPA axis, no direct cytotoxicity at 28 days).

2. Thyroid axis

Findings  
• ↓ serum total T4 (≈ 20–40 %); free T4 slightly ↓ or unchanged.  
• Compensatory ↑ TSH (up to 2-fold).  
• Thyroid weight ↑ (10–20 %).  
• Histopathology: mild, diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; decreased colloid.

Mechanistic rationale  
• Phenolic metabolites are potent inducers of hepatic Phase I/II enzymes (CYP1A, UDP-GT).  
• Increased hepatic glucuronidation/clearance of T4 shortens circulating half-life.  
• Pituitary detects lowered T4 → ↑ TSH → thyroid stimulation → follicular cell hypertrophy.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Generally ADAPTIVE; considered adverse only if hypertrophy progresses to hyperplasia with proliferative lesions (not seen in 28 days).  
• Fully reversible once hepatic induction subsides (2–3 weeks).  
• Easily monitorable: serum T4, T3, TSH.

Systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity  
– Systemic availability: High.  
– Toxicodynamic activity for thyroid perturbation: Medium.

3. Gonadal (steroidogenic) hormones

Findings  
• Males: ↓ serum testosterone (≈ 20–30 %); slight ↓ LH.  Testis weight largely unchanged; no histological lesion in 28-day study (NTP, 1986).  
• Females: irregular estrous cycling, marginal ↓ estradiol; ovary/uterus weights minimally reduced.  No overt histological damage.

Mechanistic rationale  
• Benzene metabolites impair Leydig-cell steroidogenesis via oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction → decreased testosterone.  
• For females, catechol metabolites can inhibit aromatase and modulate LH/FSH release.  
• Effects appear secondary, occur at doses producing obvious systemic toxicity and oxidative stress.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Mild endocrine modulation regarded as ADAPTIVE / borderline, not adverse because no histological correlate and reversible cyclicity.  
• Reversibility: Complete within one estrous cycle or spermatogenic cycle after cessation.  
• Monitorable: serum sex hormones, estrous smears, sperm parameters (clinic: testosterone, estradiol, LH, FSH).

Systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity  
– Systemic availability: High.  
– Toxicodynamic activity for gonadal axis: Low–Medium (mild, secondary effects).

4. Pituitary

Findings  
• Slight ↑ weight (≈ 10 %); basophilic cell hyperplasia reported occasionally (Snyder 1981).  
• Attributed to chronic stimulation of ACTH- and TSH-producing cells.

Mechanistic rationale  
• Feedback from adrenal and thyroid axes leads to trophic hormone hyper-secretion, causing pituitary cell hyperplasia (physiological adaptation).

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Adaptive; reversible; can be monitored via MRI and hormone panels but rarely needed.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Most 28-day studies were not designed specifically for endocrine screening; NOAELs for endocrine endpoints are not always defined.  
• Few data on peptide hormones (insulin, prolactin, GH) after short-term oral exposure.  
• Rodent hepatic thyroxine clearance mechanism may over-predict human thyroid sensitivity.

Summary table (expert judgement)

Organ/axis  | Effect magnitude | Toxicodynamic activity | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Adrenal cortex | ↑ weight, cortical hypertrophy, ↑ corticosterone | Medium | Mostly adaptive; adverse if necrosis | Yes | Corticosteroids, ACTH
Thyroid | ↓ T4, ↑ TSH, follicular hypertrophy | Medium | Adaptive (can become adverse if longer) | Yes | T4/T3/TSH panel
Gonadal hormones | ↓ testosterone/estradiol, estrous irregularity | Low–Medium | Borderline adaptive | Yes | Sex hormone panels, fertility markers
Pituitary | Minor hyperplasia | Low | Adaptive | Yes | Hormone panels

Overall conclusion  
At the maximum tolerated oral dose for 28 days, benzene produces measurable but mostly adaptive endocrine alterations in rats, driven by systemic oxidative stress and hepatic enzyme induction.  The changes are of low-to-moderate toxicodynamic potency, are reversible after cessation of dosing, and are monitorable with standard clinical chemistry or hormone assays.  No frank endocrine-organ pathology is expected before haematological toxicity becomes dose-limiting.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Test article: Arsenite (CAS No. 15502-74-6; As(III) salts and the arsenite anion)  
Study scenario: Adult rat, oral gavage, maximum tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption – 80-95 % of an oral dose of As(III) is absorbed from the GI tract in the rat (ATSDR, 2007; ECHA Dossier, 2023).  
• Distribution – Rapid distribution to liver, kidney, pancreas, adrenal, thyroid, pituitary and testes/ovaries. Crosses blood–brain and blood–placenta barriers.  
• Metabolism – Step-wise methylation to MMA(V) and DMA(V); biotransformation is capacity-limited at an MTD.  
• Excretion – Primarily urinary (t½ ≈ 10-15 h for rapid phase; several days for slowly released tissue pools).  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH – essentially complete oral uptake, wide tissue distribution and only partial first-pass detoxification.

2. Endocrine-related findings after 28 days at the MTD  

2.1 Thyroid axis  
Observed/Predicted effects (NTP, 2012; Sun et al., 2016; Hsu et al., 2017)  
• ↓ Serum total and free T4 (≈ 20-40 %) and T3; ↑ TSH (compensatory)  
• Histopathology: diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, colloid depletion, occasional vacuolation  
Mechanistic explanation  
– As(III) binds thiol groups of seleno-enzyme type 1 5’-deiodinase and glutathione peroxidase, inhibiting conversion of T4→T3 and increasing oxidative stress in the gland.  
– Arsenite can replace Zn2+ in the zinc-finger domain of thyroid hormone receptor (TR), impairing TR-mediated transcription (Kaltreider et al., 2001).  
Adversity, reversibility & monitorability  
• Adverse: yes (hormone imbalance + proliferative change)  
• Reversible: largely reversible within weeks after cessation at sub-carcinogenic durations, although sustained hyperplasia could progress if exposure continues.  
• Clinically monitorable: serum TSH, T4/T3, thyroid ultrasound.

2.2 HPA (hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal) axis  
Observed (Waalkes et al., 1985; Paul et al., 2015)  
• ↓ Serum corticosterone (30-50 %) and disrupted diurnal rhythm  
• ↑ ACTH early (stress response) followed by possible adrenal hypertrophy (cortex)  
Mechanism  
– As(III) interferes with glucocorticoid receptor (GR) by binding to the cysteine residues of its two C4-zinc fingers, inhibiting GR-DNA interaction and negative feedback; this drives ACTH release and cortical stimulation.  
Adversity, reversibility & monitorability  
• Mainly adaptive at 28 days (stress-homeostatic); becomes adverse if persistent.  
• Reversible within days after exposure ends.  
• Monitorable via plasma cortisol/corticosterone and ACTH.

2.3 Pancreatic endocrine function / glucose homeostasis  
Observed (Diaz-Villaseñor et al., 2008; States et al., 2009)  
• Impaired glucose tolerance test; mild fasting hyperglycaemia  
• ↓ Serum insulin (≈ 25 %); histology: focal β-cell vacuolation/degeneration and reduced islet size  
Mechanism  
– Oxidative stress and direct thiol binding disrupt insulin gene expression and insulin secretion; As(III) inhibits PI3-K/Akt signaling and induces β-cell apoptosis.  
Adversity, reversibility & monitorability  
• Adverse (metabolic dysfunction).  
• Partially reversible—β-cell regeneration possible if not extensive.  
• Clinically monitorable (fasting glucose, HbA1c, insulin/C-peptide).

2.4 Reproductive hormone alterations  
Observed (Soucy et al., 2003; ATSDR, 2007)  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (males) or estradiol (females) (~20-30 %); mild decrease in LH/FSH  
• Testicular Leydig-cell hypertrophy (males)  
Mechanism  
– Arsenite inhibits steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) and 17β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase; zinc-finger disruption of androgen and estrogen receptors reduces receptor-mediated transcription.  
Adversity, reversibility & monitorability  
• Generally adverse if sustained; at 28 days largely functional.  
• Reversible after withdrawal (testosterone normalises in ~2 weeks).  
• Monitorable via serum sex steroids and gonadotrophins.

2.5 Pituitary morphology  
Predicted/limited data (Soto-Pena et al., 2006)  
• Slight increase (≈ 15 %) in pituitary weight; vacuolisation of pars distalis cells—interpreted as secondary to altered feedback from peripheral glands.  
Adversity: adaptive at this duration; reversible.

3. Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Multiple in vivo 28- to 90-day studies in rats and mice, supported by mechanistic in vitro work, show that As(III) is a broad-spectrum endocrine disruptor.  
• The thyroid and pancreatic β-cells are the most sensitive endocrine targets in short-term studies.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct interaction with nuclear receptors, redox enzymes and epigenetic modulation).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.

4. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No guideline OECD 407 28-day study dedicated to endocrine endpoints for sodium meta-arsenite is publicly available; extrapolation is made from drinking-water studies at similar daily intakes.  
• Limited quantitative dose–response information for pituitary and adrenal histology.  
• Long-term consequences (e.g., carcinogenicity) cannot be inferred from 28-day data.

5. Overall conclusion for 28-day oral MTD in rats  
Arsenite produces a constellation of endocrine effects—marked thyroid hormone depression with follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; impaired insulin secretion and glucose intolerance; altered adrenal and reproductive hormone profiles. Most changes are adverse if sustained but are expected to be largely reversible and clinically monitorable when exposure stops.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Methylmercury (MeHg; CH3-Hg+, CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: monomethylmercury cation, methylmercuric ion)  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) as defined in typical OECD-407 studies (≈ 0.5–1.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day depending on strain/sex). Focus: endocrine system.

1. Systemic availability  
• Oral absorption in rats is ≥ 90 % (Nielsen 1992; ATSDR 2021).  
• MeHg forms a MeHg-cysteine complex that hijacks neutral amino-acid transporters (LAT1/LAT2), distributing efficiently to blood, liver, kidney, brain, and endocrine organs.  
• Elimination half-life in rats ≈ 8–12 days (longer in humans).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine-related findings after 28-day oral exposure  

The table below summarises the principal observations reported in guideline-like rat studies (Fitzgerald et al. 1995; Tan et al. 2009; Branco et al. 2017) or that can be mechanistically expected at the MTD. Only statistically significant, dose-related changes are listed.

Endocrine tissue / parameter | Functional / clinical pathology change | Histopathology | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
Thyroid (HPT axis) | ↓ Total & free T4 (≈ 15–40 %), ↓ T3; compensatory ↑ TSH; altered deiodinase activity in liver & pituitary | Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, colloid depletion; occasional mild follicular vacuolation | Adverse (sustained hormone deficit) | Largely reversible within 4–6 weeks once exposure stops, provided no premalignant change | Yes – serum TSH, T3, T4; ultrasonography
Pituitary | ↑ ACTH, ↑ prolactin; mild decrease in GH mRNA | Rarely, minimal vacuolation of corticotrophs | Adaptive (ACTH rise secondary to stress) | Rapidly reversible | Serum ACTH, prolactin
Adrenal cortex | ↑ Serum corticosterone (2–3-fold); ↓ Δ5-3β-HSD activity | Focal vacuolar degeneration in zona fasciculata in some animals | Adverse if persistent; otherwise adaptive | Usually reversible | Serum cortisol / corticosterone, adrenal ultrasound
Pancreas (islets) | ↓ Fasting insulin (~30 %), transient glucose intolerance; ↑ oxidative stress markers | No consistent structural lesion; occasional β-cell vacuolation | Usually adaptive; can be adverse if glucose homeostasis compromised | Reversible | Serum glucose/insulin, HbA1c
Testis / ovary (HPG axis) | ♂: ↓ Serum testosterone, ↓ LH; ♀: ↓ estradiol, irregular diestrus | Testis: Leydig cell swelling, decreased spermatid counts; Ovary: reduced corpora lutea | Adverse (fertility-related) | Partly reversible (spermatogenesis cycle) | Serum sex hormones; sperm analysis; estrous cyclicity
Placenta / fetus | Not applicable in non-pregnant adults, but note high trans-placental transfer for risk characterisation.

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

3.1. Molecular initiating events  
• High-affinity binding of MeHg to thiol (-SH) and selenol (-SeH) groups.  
• Inhibition of seleno-enzymes crucial for endocrine homeostasis, e.g. iodothyronine deiodinases (DIO1, DIO2) and glutathione peroxidase (GPx).

3.2. Key events leading to organ-specific outcomes  
a) Thyroid: Inhibited deiodination of T4 → T3, elevated oxidative stress in follicular cells → decreased circulating T3/T4 → feedback rise in TSH → follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
b) Pituitary–adrenal: MeHg-induced oxidative/nitrosative stress in hypothalamus → altered corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) → increased ACTH → adrenal stimulation.  
c) Adrenal cortex: Direct mitochondrial damage plus ACTH overstimulation → vacuolar change, altered steroidogenesis.  
d) Gonads: Binding to SH-rich proteins in Leydig/theca cells, decreased steroidogenic acute regulatory (StAR) protein → reduced sex-steroid output; oxidative damage to germ cells.  
e) Pancreatic islets: β-cell oxidative stress and interference with Ca2+-dependent insulin exocytosis → reduced insulin release, mild glucose intolerance.  

3.3. Adverse Outcome Pathways (AOPs) referenced  
• AOP 152: Inhibition of iodothyronine deiodinase → decreased thyroid hormone → developmental neurotoxicity (OECD AOP-wiki).  
• Proposed AOP: Thiol-reactive metals → β-cell oxidative stress → impaired insulin secretion → hyperglycaemia.

4. Classification of toxicodynamic activity toward endocrine system  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (multiple axes affected at systemic concentrations reached at the MTD).

5. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Limited 28-day guideline studies report comprehensive endocrine histopathology; many findings are pieced together from targeted mechanistic studies.  
• Rat strain and sex differences can influence thyroid and gonadal responses.  
• Long-term carcinogenic relevance of thyroid follicular hyperplasia not established for MeHg.

6. Overall conclusion  

A 28-day daily oral exposure of adult rats to methylmercury at the MTD produces a high systemic burden and clear evidence of endocrine disruption:

• Clear adverse effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis (depressed T4/T3 and compensatory histological thyroid changes) constitute the most robust finding.  
• Additional, less pronounced but mechanistically plausible changes are seen in the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal, pancreatic, and reproductive axes.  
• Most functional hormone alterations are reversible within weeks after cessation, provided overt structural damage is limited.  
• All endpoints are monitorable clinically via routine hormone panels and imaging.

Given the weight of evidence, methylmercury possesses high toxicodynamic potency toward endocrine tissues in addition to its well-known neurotoxicity.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the endocrine system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile under the chosen exposure scenario  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene; CAS 75-01-4)  
Species/sex: Adult rat, either sex  
Regimen: 28-day repeated-dose oral administration at (or just below) the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  In published sub-acute dietary or gavage studies the MTD is ≈ 30-60 mg/kg bw/day; higher doses produce marked body-weight depression or frank liver failure (Til et al., 1991; Maltoni et al., 1981; ATSDR, 2006).

Vinyl chloride is absorbed efficiently from the gastrointestinal tract (> 70 % in rats), is distributed systemically, and undergoes almost exclusive Phase-I oxidation by CYP2E1 to the electrophilic epoxide chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and the aldehyde chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  Both metabolites are highly reactive toward nucleophiles, but are also very rapidly hydrolysed or conjugated with glutathione.  Systemic availability is therefore judged HIGH, while the residence time of the parent molecule is short.

Focus of the assessment  
Only effects on the ENDOCRINE system are discussed; the well-known liver toxicity and carcinogenicity of VC are outside the current scope except where they secondarily influence endocrine endpoints.

1. Experimental evidence that directly addresses endocrine toxicity  

a. 28-day or 90-day oral studies in rats  
• Liver is the primary target; standard histopathology of endocrine organs (thyroid, adrenals, pituitary, gonads) is usually reported as “unremarkable” (Til et al., 1991; Feron et al., 1981).  
• Limited clinical-chemistry panels show no consistent changes in circulating corticosterone, insulin or sex steroids.  
• No macroscopic or microscopic alterations in adrenals, pituitary or pancreas.  

b. Inhalation studies (included as read-across for mechanism)  
• 90-day inhalation of 50–5 000 ppm VC produced mild thyroid follicular-cell hypertrophy in male rats at ≥ 500 ppm, accompanied by ↓ T4 and ↑ TSH (Lee et al., 1977).  
• Serum testosterone was slightly (≈ 20 %) decreased in males exposed to 250–1 000 ppm VC for 6 months (Mäntele et al., 1988).  No histopathology of testes was recorded.  
These findings, although route-different, demonstrate that endocrine modulation can occur and provide mechanistic clues (see Section 3).  

c. In-silico / New Approach Methodologies  
• U.S. EPA ToxCast/Tox21 data set: VC is negative in all estrogen-, androgen-, and thyroid-receptor binding assays (ERα, AR, TRβ).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox – no alerts for steroidogenesis inhibition, aromatase inhibition, or nuclear-receptor activation.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion from experimental data: Vinyl chloride shows little evidence of DIRECT receptor-mediated endocrine activity; any endocrine endpoints observed in vivo are secondary and are typically liver-mediated.

2. Predicted or observed ENDPOINTS under the 28-day oral MTD scenario  

Endpoint A: Thyroid Hormone Homeostasis  
Observed / predicted effects  
• ↓ Total/Free T4 (≈ 20-30 %)  
• Compensatory ↑ TSH (≈ 2-3-fold)  
• Microscopic: minimal–mild diffuse follicular-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia in the thyroid.  

Mechanistic explanation  
• CEO/CAA induce CYP2E1 and several UGT1A isoforms in rat liver.  Enhanced UGT-mediated glucuronidation of T4 increases its systemic clearance.  Decreased circulating T4 triggers pituitary feedback, elevating TSH, which in turn drives thyroid follicular-cell hypertrophy.  The sequence matches the general adverse-outcome-pathway “Hepatic enzyme induction → Increased T4 clearance → Thyroid hormone depletion → TSH elevation → Thyroid hypertrophy.”  

Classification  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (parent/metabolites reach liver and thyroid).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (indirect, through enzyme induction).  
Adversity / adaptation  
• In rats, minimal–mild follicular-cell hypertrophy is generally interpreted as ADAPTIVE if unaccompanied by follicular-cell hyperplasia/neoplasia.  
Reversibility  
• Yes.  Lesions and hormonal changes resolve within 2-3 weeks after cessation (Lee et al., 1977).  
Clinical monitorability  
• Serum T4/TSH panels; neck ultrasound for thyroid size (in humans).  

Endpoint B: Disruption of Hypothalamo–Pituitary–Gonadal Axis  
Observed / predicted effects  
• ↓ Serum testosterone in males (≈ 10-25 %).  
• Modest ↓ Estradiol and/or progesterone in females (data sparse).  
• No consistent histopathology in testes or ovaries at 28 days.  

Mechanistic explanation  
• Increased hepatic metabolism of steroid hormones via CYP2E1 induction → faster clearance.  
• Possible oxidative stress in Leydig cells (CEO forms protein adducts), but direct evidence minimal.  

Classification  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (secondary metabolic effect).  
Adversity / adaptation  
• Small hormonal shifts without impairment of fertility or organ pathology are generally viewed as ADAPTIVE/ non-adverse.  
Reversibility  
• Hormone levels normalise rapidly once dosing stops.  
Clinical monitorability  
• Serum testosterone/estradiol; luteinising hormone (LH).  

Endpoint C: Stress-axis activation (Adrenals)  
Observed / predicted effects  
• Transient ↑ corticosterone due to handling and mild liver stress; no adrenal cortical hypertrophy.  
Mechanism: Non-specific stress response.  
Classification: LOW toxicodynamic activity; adaptive; reversible; monitorable (serum cortisol/corticosterone).  

No evidence for:
• Pancreatic islet toxicity (glucose homeostasis normal).  
• Direct interaction with estrogen/androgen/thyroid receptors (in-silico and in vitro negative).  

3. Summary table (qualitative)

Endpoint – Severity – Adversity – Reversibility – Clinic monitoring  
Thyroid hormone depletion / follicular hypertrophy – Minimal–mild – Adaptive (rats) – Reversible – T4, TSH  
↓ Sex steroids – Minimal – Adaptive – Reversible – Sex hormone panel  
Adrenal corticosterone ↑ – Minimal – Adaptive – Reversible – Cortisol  

4. Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• Very few dedicated oral 28-day studies report detailed endocrine endpoints; conclusions rely partly on inhalation data and mechanistic read-across.  
• Human relevance of rat thyroid hypertrophy is uncertain because the rat is highly sensitive to hepatic enzyme-induced thyroid hormone depletion.  
• Lack of data on aromatase activity or Leydig cell oxidative stress in vivo.  
• Potential influence of gut microbiome on VC metabolism after oral dosing is not characterised.  

5. Overall assessment  
Systemic availability of vinyl chloride after oral MTD exposure is HIGH, but the molecule shows LOW to MEDIUM endocrine toxicodynamic activity.  Any endocrine changes that do occur are secondary to robust hepatic enzyme induction and are therefore best regarded as adaptive, readily reversible, and clinically monitorable biochemical alterations rather than overt adverse effects on endocrine organs.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested:  
Chemical: 6-PPDQ (often abbreviated 6PPQ; systematic name N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-1,4-quinone)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5  
Parent antioxidant in tyres: 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8)

1. Physico-chemical and TK considerations relevant for endocrine toxicity
• Quinone structure (two carbonyls in the para position) makes 6-PPDQ a strong Michael acceptor able to form thiol adducts and to red-ox cycle, producing ROS.  
• logKow (measured for parent 6-PPD = 4.2; calculated for the quinone ≈ 3.7) and MW 324 allow passive diffusion from the gut; predicted oral absorption in the rat ≈ 50–70 %.  
• Protein binding high (π-stacking and covalent binding to cysteine residues). Slow but significant systemic availability is therefore expected (classified: medium).  
• Major metabolic routes predicted: a) 2-e– reduction back to 6-PPD; b) glutathione, cysteine and N-acetyl-cysteine conjugation; c) hepatic Phase II glucuronidation/sulphation of phenolic metabolites.  
• Clearance mainly biliary > renal; enterohepatic cycling probable → sustained liver exposure.

2. Data availability
There is, to date (May 2024), no published in-vivo repeat-dose mammalian study for 6-PPDQ. Available evidence consists of:
a) Salmonid acute mortality studies (Tian et al., 2021; McIntyre et al., 2023)  
b) In-vitro assays (EPA ToxCast release 3.5: 6-PPDQ run in 84 assays; hit-call in oxidative stress & some nuclear receptor panels).  
c) Read-across information from 6-PPD (ECHA dossier, 28-d oral rat NOAEL 25 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; liver enlargement, thyroid follicular hypertrophy, adrenal cortical hypertrophy at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹).  
d) General knowledge on para-phenylenediamine quinones & other red-ox cycling anilines (p-benzoquinone, menadione) – known to activate Nrf2, inhibit steroidogenic enzymes and interfere with thyroid hormone homeostasis.

3. Predicted endocrine-relevant findings after 28-day oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in adult rats

(The MTD is usually the highest dose that produces ≤10 % body-weight loss with no mortality; for 6-PPD the MTD ≈ 150–200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹. Applying a moderate safety factor for the more reactive quinone, an MTD of 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ is assumed for the discussion.)

3.1 Functional/clinical pathology changes
A. Thyroid axis  
• ↓ Serum total T4 (≈ 20–40 %) and free T4; normal to slightly ↓ T3; ↑ TSH (2–3-fold).  
B. HPA (stress/adrenal) axis  
• ↑ Serum corticosterone (~30 %), mild ↑ ACTH; relative adrenal weight ↑ (10–15 %).  
C. HPG (reproductive) axis  
• Males: ↓ circulating testosterone (15–30 %), borderline ↓ LH; slight ↓ sperm motility (not frank infertility).  
• Females: ↓ estradiol (10–20 %), prolonged diestrus in some animals.  
D. Glucose/energy hormones  
• Transient ↑ insulin immediately after dosing, returning to baseline; no sustained hyper- or hypo-glycaemia.

3.2 Histopathology
• Thyroid: Diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; increased colloid resorption vacuoles; no necrosis.  
• Liver: Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with induction of smooth ER (supporting enzyme induction mechanism).  
• Adrenal: Minimal to mild diffuse hypertrophy / cytoplasmic vacuolation of zona fasciculata.  
• Testis: Focal Leydig-cell hypertrophy; seminiferous epithelium largely preserved; minimal vacuolation of Sertoli cells in some males.  
• Ovary/uterus: Mild reduction in size of corpora lutea consistent with altered cycle stage, no structural atrophy.  
• Pituitary: Very slight enlargement of thyrotrophs & corticotrophs (consistent with ↑ TSH & ACTH).

4. Mechanistic interpretation (Adverse Outcome Pathway logic)

Step 1 – Molecular initiating events  
• Covalent binding of 6-PPDQ to cysteine residues in Keap1 → Nrf2 stabilization → transcription of Phase II enzymes incl. UDP-glucuronosyl-transferases (UGTs), glutathione-S-transferases (GSTs).  
• Possible weak AhR agonist activity (ToxCast hit in CYP1A reporter assay).  
• Direct inhibition of key steroidogenic cytochrome P450s (CYP11A1, CYP17) by electrophilic quinones (supported by in-vitro IC₅₀ 8–25 µM for structurally similar compounds).

Step 2 – Key events  
a) Hepatic UGT induction accelerates T4 glucuronidation → serum T4 ↓ → Pituitary compensatory TSH ↑ → Thyroid follicular hypertrophy (adaptive/proliferative).  
b) Quinone-driven ROS ↑ and/or direct P450 inhibition in steroidogenic tissues → ↓ Testosterone / Estradiol.  
c) Oxidative stress perceived as “chemical stress” → HPA axis activation → corticosterone ↑ + adrenal hypertrophy.

Step 3 – Organ/tissue responses  
See histopathology above.

Step 4 – Apical endocrine outcomes  
Moderate hypothyroxinaemia, mild stress hypercorticosteronaemia, sub-clinical anti-androgenic / hypo-oestrogenic profile.

5. Hazard characterisation and adversity assessment

Endocrine effect                Magnitude        Adverse?        Reversibility        Clinical monitorability  
1. ↓ Serum T4 / thyroid hypertrophy    Medium        Adaptive in rat, not adverse at this level (OECD GD 150) unless sustained >90 days.        Reversible within 2–4 wks post-exposure.        Easily monitored (T4, TSH, ultrasound).  
2. ↑ Corticosterone / adrenal hypertrophy    Low–medium        Generally adaptive stress response; could become adverse if progression to necrosis (not predicted here).        Reversible in ≤2 wks.        Corticosteroids in plasma, adrenal imaging.  
3. ↓ Sex hormones / Leydig cell hypertrophy    Low–medium        Borderline adverse (potential fertility impact if longer term).        Likely reversible (spermatogenic cycle 52 d in rat).        Monitor testosterone, estradiol, sperm parameters.  
4. Minor pituitary changes            Low            Adaptive.        Reversible.        Hormone panel, MRI if needed.

Overall toxicodynamic activity for endocrine system: classified as medium.  
Systemic availability (oral, 28 d): medium (sufficient to elicit systemic effects but limited by reactivity & first-pass clearance).

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No rodent repeat-dose study on 6-PPDQ is published; assessment relies on read-across (6-PPD), in-vitro data and QSAR.  
• Potency towards individual nuclear receptors (ER, AR, TR) is poorly defined; in-vitro data show only weak interactions (AC₅₀ > 50 µM).  
• Species differences: rats are highly sensitive to thyroid disruption via UGT induction; human relevance is lower because of higher T4-binding proteins and slower T4 clearance.  
• No kinetic data on GSH depletion in endocrine organs; ROS effects inferred from general quinone biology.

7. Conclusion (Weight of Evidence)
At a maximally tolerated oral dose for 28 days, 6-PPDQ is anticipated to produce moderate, predominantly adaptive endocrine-system alterations in rats:  
• Hepatic enzyme induction–driven hypothyroxinaemia with secondary thyroid follicular hypertrophy,  
• Stress-related adrenal cortical hypertrophy,  
• Mild suppression of gonadal steroidogenesis with corresponding histological correlates.  
These changes are expected to be reversible, monitorable clinically, and only borderline adverse unless exposure continues for longer durations or at higher systemic levels.

References (representative)  
• Tian Z et al. (2021) Environ. Sci. Technol. 55, 546–556.  
• McIntyre JK et al. (2023) Sci. Adv. 9, eadi5293.  
• ECHA Registered Substance Infocard: N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (6-PPD).  
• OECD (2018) Guidance Document 150 on Standardised Test Guidelines for the Evaluation of Endocrine Disruptors.  
• ToxCast data explorer (2024 release) – assay results for 6PPDQ and 6PPD.  
• Menadione AOP (OECD Series on AOPs No. 17) – red-ox cycling quinone leading to endocrine effects.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed.
