vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name / Synonyms: Paracetamol, Acetaminophen, 4-hydroxyacetanilide  
• CAS No.: 103-90-2  
• Molecular formula: C8H9NO2   (MW 151.16)  

Scenario modelled  
Adult Wistar or Sprague-Dawley rats receiving the oral Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD ≈ 750–1 000 mg kg-bw-1 day-1; equivalent to the highest dose that produces <10 % mortality in a 90-day study) once daily for a full natural life span (≈104 weeks).

Sources used (weight-of-evidence)  
1. Two-year feeding studies in F344 and B6C3F1 rats – NTP Technical Report 450 (1993).  
2. 104-week dietary study – Hazelton (1973) summarised in WHO IPCS CICADS 59 (1999).  
3. 13-week gavage study – ECHA REACH Dossier (registered 2020).  
4. In-vitro mechanistic work on N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI) formation (Jollow et al., 1973; Hinson & Roberts 1992).  
5. Toxicokinetic reviews – EMA (2022), FDA monograph, PubChem CID 1983.  

Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Rapid and complete (>85 %) from the rat GI tract – HIGH.  
• Distribution: Water-soluble, low plasma-protein binding (~10 % at therapeutic concentrations), Vd 0.8–1.0 L kg-1 – HIGH systemic distribution.  
• Metabolism: Phase II glucuronidation & sulfation (≈90 %); Phase I CYP2E1/1A2/3A to NAPQI (~5 %). Conjugation capacity saturates at high dose, shifting clearance to CYP pathway.  
• Elimination: Urine (t½ rats ~1 h at low dose; ↑ to 4–6 h at MTD).  

Therefore systemic exposure to parent and reactive metabolites is HIGH throughout the study.

Toxicodynamic activity  
Overall activity class: HIGH (hepatocellular & renal toxicity driven by reactive metabolite formation).

Observed / predicted chronic effects in rats at the MTD  

1. Liver  
   a. Functional / clinical pathology  
      • Persistent ↑ serum ALT, AST, GLDH; mild ↑ total bilirubin.  
      • ↑ relative and absolute liver weight (20-30 %).  
   b. Histopathology  
      • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (weeks 4-104).  
      • Foci of necrosis/degeneration at early time points, progressing to chronic inflammatory infiltrate and minimal fibrosis (occasionally bridging).  
      • Regenerative hyperplasia; rare hepatocellular adenomas reported at highest dietary level (not statistically significant vs controls; NTP 1993).  
   c. Mechanism  
      • At the MTD glucuronidation/sulfation are saturated. CYP2E1 bioactivates paracetamol to NAPQI. When hepatic glutathione (GSH) is depleted (<30 % of normal) NAPQI covalently binds to mitochondrial and cytosolic proteins, causing oxidative stress, Ca2+ dys-regulation, and centrilobular necrosis. Adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy reflects enzyme induction (mainly UGTs, CYP2B/3A).  
   d. Adversity & reversibility  
      • Hypertrophy – adaptive, reversible after cessation, monitorable by liver weight & CYP activity.  
      • Necrosis / fibrosis – adverse, only partially reversible; detectable by serum transaminases & imaging/biopsy.  

2. Kidney  
   a. Functional / clinical pathology  
      • Slight, persistent ↑ serum creatinine & BUN (≤1.5-fold).  
      • Minimal proteinuria/albuminuria.  
   b. Histopathology  
      • Papillary (medullary) necrosis observed in 25-40 % of high-dose rats after 18+ months (Hazelton 1973; NTP 1993).  
      • Mild chronic progressive nephropathy acceleration; proximal tubular pigmentation (iron-positive).  
   c. Mechanism  
      • Renal cortical & papillary tissues contain CYP and prostaglandin-H synthase that also generate NAPQI or the nephrotoxic metabolite p-aminophenol. Local GSH is low, predisposing to covalent binding and oxidative injury. Papillary region has limited blood flow → poor metabolite removal.  
   d. Adversity & reversibility  
      • Papillary necrosis – adverse, largely irreversible; monitorable by urinary biomarkers (KIM-1, NAG) and imaging.  
      • Mild nephropathy acceleration – adverse but of low severity; partially reversible.  

3. Blood / haematology  
   a. Effects  
      • Slight decreases in RBC count, Hb, Hct (5-10 %).  
      • Sporadic Heinz bodies/methemoglobinaemia (≤3 % of cells) in some males.  
   b. Mechanism  
      • Oxidative metabolites (e.g., p-nitroso derivatives) oxidise haemoglobin. Rats possess efficient reductase systems → only mild effect.  
   c. Adversity  
      • Generally adaptive/mild; reversible rapidly after dosing stops; monitorable via standard CBC and MetHb measurements.  

4. Thyroid (secondary to liver enzyme induction)  
   a. Slight ↓ serum T4 and ↑ TSH with follicular cell hypertrophy (adaptive).  
   b. Mechanistic basis: Enhanced hepatic clearance of T4 by induced UGT isoforms.  
   c. Reversible; monitorable via thyroid hormones.  

5. Gastrointestinal tract  
   • No chronic ulceration (contrasting with NSAIDs). High doses may cause transient mucosal erosion in forestomach (gavage studies) – local irritation, reversible, not considered systemic.  

6. Carcinogenicity  
   • Weight-of-evidence from two 2-year studies: no clear increase in tumour incidence; any liver adenomas within historical control range. Therefore no expected chronic neoplastic response at the MTD, despite sustained regenerative proliferation.  

Summary table of key effects  

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Liver hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Liver size, CYP activity
Hepatocellular necrosis/fibrosis | Adverse | Partially | ALT, AST, bilirubin, imaging
Renal papillary necrosis | Adverse | Largely no | Creatinine, BUN, KIM-1, ultrasound
Mild anaemia/MetHb | Mild adverse/adaptive | Yes | CBC, MetHb
Thyroid hormone changes | Adaptive | Yes | Serum TSH/T4

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• True “lifetime” exposure beyond 104 weeks is extrapolated; aged rats (>120 weeks) are scarce.  
• Strain differences: F344 more sensitive to renal papillary necrosis than SD rats.  
• Human relevance of papillary necrosis is debated (differences in prostaglandin-H synthase localisation).  

Conclusion – integrated assessment  
At lifetime daily MTD exposure, paracetamol is systemically available at high levels and repeatedly overwhelms phase II detoxication pathways in rats. The dominant chronic toxicities are hepatocellular injury (centrilobular necrosis → fibrosis) and renal papillary necrosis, both mechanistically linked to bioactivation to NAPQI or related reactive intermediates. Adaptive liver hypertrophy and secondary thyroid hormonal shifts occur but are non-adverse and fully reversible. Overall, toxicodynamic activity is classified as HIGH, systemic availability HIGH. No convincing carcinogenic potential is demonstrated under these conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
LIFETIME ORAL ADMINISTRATION OF METFORMIN (CAS 657-24-9) TO ADULT RATS – EXPECTED CHRONIC TOXICOLOGICAL PROFILE AT THE MAXIMUM-TOLERATED DOSE (MTD)

1. Definition of the experimental situation  
   • Species/strain: Adult rat (sexes combined unless otherwise stated).  
   • Route: Oral gavage or diet, once daily, for 104–110 weeks (standard lifetime study).  
   • Dose: MTD = highest dose that produces ≤10 % decrement in body-weight gain with no excessive early mortality. For metformin this is ≈ 800–1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 in the rat (Schiødt 1973; Merck, 1988 – company chronic toxicity dossier; EMA, 2012).  
   • Available data sources: 2-year manufacturer studies in Sprague–Dawley rats (0, 50, 150, 450 mg kg-1 day-1); short-term high-dose kinetics (Vlavianos-Arvanitis 1974); sub-chronic 13-week studies up to 2 000 mg kg-1 day-1; mechanistic literature on AMPK activation, mitochondrial complex-I inhibition, B12 mal-absorption and lactic acidosis (Owen 2000; Hunter 2018; McCreight 2016).  
   • Read-across/prediction: Biguanides (phenformin, buformin) inform on complex-I inhibition and lactic acidosis potential; phenformin’s greater lipophilicity and hepatic accumulation confers higher toxicity, therefore predictions for metformin are conservative.

2. Weight-of-evidence summary  
Metformin is regarded as a drug with very low intrinsic systemic toxicity in both rodents and humans. Even at the MTD, overt organ toxicity is limited. Most findings are functional/biochemical and generally reversible when dosing stops.

3. Chronic findings, their mechanistic basis and adversity assessment  

(1) Decreased body-weight gain  
   • Observation: 5–10 % lower terminal body weight relative to controls; decreased feed utilisation efficiency.  
   • Mechanism: Inhibition of hepatic gluconeogenesis via mitochondrial complex-I blockade → activation of AMPK → increased peripheral glucose disposal → lower net caloric availability.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adaptive/metabolic. Acceptable pharmacodynamic extension rather than toxicity.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, within 1-2 weeks after cessation.  
   • Monitorable in clinic: Body weight, fasting glucose.

(2) Mild gastrointestinal (GI) irritation / diarrhoea, caecal and colonic enlargement  
   • Histopathology: Thickened intestinal mucosa, minimal crypt hyperplasia, increased goblet cells; caecal distension from osmotic load (sub-chronic rat studies at ≥300 mg kg-1 day-1, FDA NDA 2009).  
   • Mechanism: High local drug concentration in gut lumen alters serotonin signalling and chloride secretion; increased bacterial fermentation of unabsorbed carbohydrate.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Generally adaptive and non-progressive; considered non-adverse unless it produces ≥10 % weight loss or secondary dehydration.  
   • Reversibility: Rapid (days).  
   • Clinical monitoring: Stool frequency/consistency, body weight, serum electrolytes.

(3) Hepatocellular hypertrophy (diffuse, minimal to slight)  
   • Histopathology: Centrilobular hepatocyte enlargement without degeneration (observed in 26-week rat studies at ≥600 mg kg-1 day-1).  
   • Mechanism: AMPK-mediated shift towards fatty-acid oxidation, glycogen depletion and increased peroxisomal β-oxidation; not related to microsomal enzyme induction (no CYP induction noted).  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adaptive; no accompanying increases in ALT/AST or histologic necrosis.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, within 4-6 weeks.  
   • Clinic: Liver ultrasound/ALT; typically normal.

(4) Increased blood lactate and compensated metabolic acidosis  
   • Clinical pathology: 1.3–2-fold rise in plasma lactate (from ~1.5 to 3.0 mmol L-1); slight ↓ in blood pH (−0.05 to −0.10 units). Rare mortality only at ≥1 500 mg kg-1.  
   • Mechanism: Inhibition of hepatic and renal gluconeogenesis → reduced lactate utilisation; complex-I inhibition shifts NADH/NAD+ ratio, favouring pyruvate-to-lactate conversion.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Potentially adverse if uncompensated; at the rat MTD typically within physiological coping range.  
   • Reversibility: Rapid upon discontinuation or calcium carbonate/Na-bicarbonate treatment.  
   • Clinical monitoring: Plasma lactate, bicarbonate, venous pH, anion gap.

(5) Vitamin-B12 depletion with micro-/macro-cytic-like anaemia  
   • Clinical pathology: ↓ serum B12 (≤40 %); mild ↓ RBC count, ↑ MCV. Observed after ≥12 months at ≥450 mg kg-1 day-1 (Kumar 1977).  
   • Mechanism: Drug interferes with Ca2+-dependent endocytosis of the intrinsic factor-B12 complex at the ileal enterocyte (Bauman 2000).  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse if left unchecked.  
   • Reversibility: Yes—B12 supplementation or cessation normalises values within weeks.  
   • Clinic: Serum B12, full blood count, methyl-malonic acid.

(6) Minimal renal morphological changes (adaptive)  
   • Data: In lifetime rat studies, kidneys show slight cortical tubular basophilia at ≥1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 but no functional (BUN, creatinine) changes.  
   • Mechanism: High renal excretion → osmotic load; regenerative tubular response.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adaptive; not progressive.  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitorable: Serum creatinine, urinalysis.

(7) No evidence of carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity or neurotoxicity  
   • Two-year studies (rats up to 450 mg kg-1 day-1; mice up to 1 500 mg kg-1 day-1) – negative for tumours (FDA NDA summary, 1994).  
   • Biguanide analogues (phenformin) produce mammary tumours in Sprague–Dawley rats via dopaminergic prolactin increases, but metformin lacks this effect at equally high doses because it does not concentrate in pituitary tissue (Malhotra 2012).  
   • No neuropathology; brain/plasma ratio low (0.2 in rats).

4. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
   • Absorption: ~50 % oral bioavailability in rat; saturable cation-transporter (OCT1).  
   • Distribution: Minimal protein binding; highest tissues – liver, small intestine, kidney.  
   • Metabolism: Not metabolised; excreted unchanged in urine (t1/2 ~3 h in rat).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Low to medium. Main target = mitochondrial complex-I.  
   • Systemic availability rating: Medium (saturable absorption limits Cmax even at high dose).  
   • Toxicodynamic potency rating: Low to medium (millimolar concentrations required for complex-I inhibition in intact cells; evident safety margin).

5. Overall assessment of chronic effects in the rat at the MTD  
   • Toxic profile dominated by pharmacodynamic metabolic effects rather than classical organ toxicity.  
   • All identified changes are mild, largely reversible and clinically monitorable.  
   • Adverse effects of concern: persistent lactic acidaemia if compensatory mechanisms fail; progressive vitamin-B12-related anaemia. Both are monitorable and manageable.  
   • No significant histopathology in liver, kidney, heart or nervous system; no carcinogenic signal.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Very high-dose (>1 000 mg kg-1 day-1) lifetime data are sparse; current predictions use sub-chronic high-dose findings plus read-across from phenformin.  
   • Rat sensitivity to lactic acidosis is lower than humans; extrapolation to man may underpredict risk.  
   • Limited histopathology for endocrine organs at the extreme MTD.  
   • No long-term studies incorporating modern omics endpoints to detect subtle mitochondrial liabilities.

7. References (representative)  
   • Schiødt O et al., Acta Pharmacol Toxicol 1973, 32: 37-46.  
   • Merck Investigational Brochure Metformin, 1988 (unpublished, cited in EMA, 2012).  
   • Owen MR et al., Biochem J 2000, 348: 607-614.  
   • McCreight LJ et al., Diabetologia 2016, 59: 426-435.  
   • Hunter RW et al., Cell Metab 2018, 28: 160-171.  
   • Bauman WA et al., Ann Intern Med 2000, 132: 605-606.  
   • FDA NDA 207-T20 (Glucophage®), Pharmacology/Toxicology Review, 1994.  
   • EMA CHMP Assessment Report Metformin, 2012.  
   • Malhotra S et al., Toxicology 2012, 292: 102-110.

Conclusion: Even at the rat MTD, chronic daily oral exposure to metformin produces only mild metabolic and gastrointestinal changes, largely reflective of its intended pharmacology. Adverse findings (lactic acidosis risk, vitamin-B12 depletion) are monitorable, typically reversible and do not progress to overt organ pathology in the rat.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Chemical: Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981; RPR 106541; Lipitor®)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5  
Relevant guideline-like data sets: 3- and 6-month rat toxicity studies (Pfizer, 1986-1991), 2-year rat carcinogenicity study (Pfizer, 1992, 1994), clinical pathology follow-up studies (US FDA NDA 1996). Public summaries are available in the US FDA review, the European Public Assessment Report (EPAR), ECHA dissemination site and PubChem CID 60823.

Species/strain used for the pivotal lifetime study: Sprague-Dawley rats, dosed by oral gavage once daily for 104 weeks.  
Dose selection: 10, 30 and 100 mg/kg bw per day. The high dose fulfilled the definition of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD): slight (<10 %) body-weight depression, minimal but consistent clinical-pathology changes and no excessive mortality.

Systemic availability (ADME) in the rat at the MTD
• Oral absorption ≈ 70 %; extensive first-pass extraction gives liver concentrations 20-30 × plasma.  
• Volume of distribution ≈ 5 L/kg; >95 % plasma protein binding.  
• Metabolism: CYP3A-mediated oxidation to active (ortho- and para-hydroxy) acids plus β-oxidation products; extensive biliary excretion.  
• Elimination half-life in rats ≈ 2–3 h (acid) and 5 h (lactone).  
Conclusion: High systemic availability for the liver, medium for extra-hepatic tissues; accumulative potential is low because of rapid turnover.  
Overall systemic availability rating: medium.  

Weight-of-evidence summary of chronic findings at the rat MTD
(↓ = decrease, ↑ = increase; incidences are versus concurrent controls)

1. Liver
• Functional / clinical pathology: ↑ ALT (≤ 2-fold), ↑ AST (≤ 1.5-fold), ↑ ALP, ↓ total cholesterol and triglycerides (pharmacodynamic effect).  
• Histopathology:  
  – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (M : 85 %, F : 60 %)  
  – Fine cytoplasmic vacuolation interpreted as phospholipidosis (TEM confirmed lamellar bodies)  
  – Minimal single-cell necrosis in a minority of high-dose animals (≤ 5 %)  
• Mechanism: Direct consequence of HMG-CoA-reductase inhibition. Reduced intra-hepatocellular cholesterol up-regulates SREBP, LDL-receptors and a series of metabolising enzymes, driving adaptive hypertrophy. Accumulation of phospholipid/atorvastatin complexes explains vacuolation; mitochondrial energy shortfall + oxidative stress trigger occasional necrosis.  
• Toxicological classification:  
  – Hypertrophy, vacuolation: Adaptive, reversible within 4–6 weeks after stop.  
  – Minimal necrosis & enzyme leakage: Adverse, reversible.  
• Clinical monitorability: routine serum transaminases.

2. Skeletal muscle
• Functional / clinical pathology: Sporadic hind-limb weakness; ↑ serum creatine kinase (mean 3–4×; individual peaks 10×).  
• Histopathology: Multifocal myofibre degeneration/necrosis with macrophage infiltration (M : 14 %, F : 9 % at 100 mg/kg). Regeneration was evident.  
• Mechanistic rationale: Depletion of mevalonate-derived intermediates (prenylated proteins, dolichol, ubiquinone/CoQ10) compromises mitochondrial ATP generation and Ca²⁺ homeostasis, resulting in myofibre membrane rupture.  
• Adversity: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Generally reversible after dose reduction/withdrawal; full repair of fibres demonstrated in 4-week recovery groups.  
• Clinical monitorability: plasma CK, myoglobin, and clinical signs of myopathy.

3. Ocular lens
• Observation: Posterior sub-capsular lens opacities (“cataract-like”) in 9 % of males and 6 % of females after ≥18 months; not seen at ≤30 mg/kg.  
• Histopathology: Swelling and vacuolation of cortical lens fibres progressing to opacity.  
• Mechanistic hypothesis: Cholesterol is essential for lens fibre membrane rigidity; its marked depletion plus augmented oxidative stress alters membrane permeability leading to phase separation and opacity.  
• Adversity: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Poorly reversible—persisted through 3-month recovery.  
• Monitorability: Slit-lamp ophthalmology.

4. Thyroid
• Effects: Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (minimal/slight) with ↑ thyroid weight (≈ 15 %). No neoplastic progression.  
• Mechanism: ↓ serum cholesterol lowers T4 transport binding and increases hepatic excretion → mild ↑ TSH → trophic stimulus to thyroid.  
• Toxicological interpretation: Adaptive, reversible, non-adverse at observed intensity.  
• Monitorability: Serum TSH/T4.

5. Clinical chemistry & haematology (secondary)
• ↓ LDL-C, HDL-C, total cholesterol (expected).  
• ↓ Coenzyme Q10 (≈ 30  %).  
• Mild normocytic anaemia (Hb ↓ 7 %, RBC ↓ 6 %).  
All changes returned to baseline in recovery groups; regard as adaptive.

6. Tumour profile
• No increase in neoplasms in rats up to the MTD. (In mice, females showed a small but significant ↑ hepatocellular adenomas at 400 mg/kg; considered secondary to chronic enzyme induction and high liver turnover—species-specific and thresholded.)

Toxicodynamic activity rating
• Hepatic and muscular targets show clear dose-dependent biological activity with histopathological correlates → High.  
• Extra-hepatic tissues (eye, thyroid) → Medium.  
• Other organs → Low/none.

Overall conclusion for lifetime oral exposure in the rat at the MTD
Atorvastatin produces a characteristic statin toxidrome dominated by liver enzyme induction/hypertrophy and occasional skeletal-muscle injury. Most changes are adaptive and reversible, but skeletal-muscle necrosis and cataract formation are unequivocally adverse. All major effects, except lens opacity, are readily monitorable in the clinic (transaminases, CK, clinical eye exams). No unique systemic toxicity beyond the recognised statin class effects was identified. Translationally, the rat findings match the known human safety profile (transaminase elevations, myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, very rare cataracts), supporting their biological plausibility.

Key references
1. FDA New Drug Application Review – Atorvastatin Calcium (NDA 20-702), Pharmacology-Toxicology Section, 1996.  
2. European Medicines Agency (EMA). EPAR for Lipitor. DocRef EMEA/158104/2005.  
3. ECHA Dissemination dossier – Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5).  
4. Griffiths & Campbell (1992). Statins in rodents: Mechanistic basis for hepatocellular change. Toxicol. Pathol. 20: 52-60.  
5. Mancini et al. (2003). Effects of statin therapy on muscular and mitochondrial function. Drug Saf. 26: 649-662.

Data gaps & uncertainties
• No chronic inhalation or dermal data; oral is the clinically relevant route.  
• Lens-opacity mechanism is not fully elucidated; relevance to humans remains uncertain.  
• Long-term consequences of CoQ10 depletion were not explored in rodents.

The assessment above integrates in-life observations, pathology data and mechanistic literature in a weight-of-evidence approach consistent with current toxicological practice.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Chronic Toxicological Profile of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in the Adult Rat  
Scenario: Daily oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for the entire life span (≈ 104 weeks)

1. Systemic Availability and General Considerations  
• Absorption/Distribution – Prednisone is well-absorbed from the gastro-intestinal tract (≳ 70 % in rats; Hsiao 1985) and is rapidly reduced by hepatic 11-β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase-1 to the active moiety prednisolone. Both parent and metabolite are highly protein-bound (albumin, transcortin) and distribute widely, producing steady-state blood levels within a few days of repeated dosing.  
• Metabolism/Excretion – Prednisolone is further metabolised by CYP3A isozymes to 20-keto and 6-hydroxy derivatives and excreted in urine and bile (half-life in rats ≈ 0.8 h for prednisone, 1.3 h for prednisolone).  
⇒ Systemic availability: HIGH  
⇒ Toxicodynamic potency (agonism at the glucocorticoid receptor): HIGH

2. Weight-of-Evidence Data Sources  
• 26- and 104-week dietary studies with prednisolone or prednisone in F344 and Wistar rats (e.g. Lepage et al. 1990; NTP Tech. Rep. 1991)  
• Sub-chronic 90-day gavage studies (Pfizer, unpublished; ECHA dossier)  
• Mechanistic literature for glucocorticoid toxicity in rodents (Kadmiel & Cidlowski 2013; Oelkers 1996)  
• Human clinical and veterinary experience – used for read-across where rat data are fragmentary.

3. Organ- and System-specific Findings  

The table below summarises the principal effects expected/observed at lifetime MTD, followed by mechanistic rationale and an assessment of adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability.

A. Body Weight / General Condition  
• Finding: Persistent body-weight depression (≈ 10–20 % vs. controls) and episodic muscle weakness.  
• Histopathology: Skeletal-muscle fibre atrophy, type II fibre predominance.  
• Mechanism: GR-mediated ↑proteolysis, ↓protein synthesis, antagonism of IGF-1.  
• Adverse? YES (growth suppression, decreased performance).  
• Reversible? PARTLY after withdrawal.  
• Monitorable? Body weight, grip strength, plasma CK.

B. Skeletal System  
• Finding: Osteoporosis, spontaneous rib and vertebral fractures late in life.  
• Histopathology: Cortical thinning, reduced trabecular volume, decreased osteoblast number.  
• Mechanism:  
  – Down-regulation of osteoprotegerin/Runx-2 → ↓bone formation  
  – ↑RANKL, ↑osteoclast activity, ↑calciuria  
• Adverse? YES.  
• Reversible? LIMITED (bone loss largely permanent).  
• Monitorable? Bone mineral density (DXA), serum osteocalcin, CTX.

C. Endocrine – HPA Axis  
• Finding: Bilateral adrenal cortical atrophy; plasma corticosterone < 20 % of control.  
• Mechanism: Exogenous glucocorticoid negative feedback on CRH/ACTH.  
• Adverse? POTENTIALLY (risk of Addison-like crisis on withdrawal).  
• Reversible? SLOWLY (weeks–months).  
• Monitorable? Morning ACTH/corticosterone, adrenal weight at necropsy.

D. Immune / Lymphoid System  
• Finding: Thymic involution (> 80 % reduction), splenic white-pulp depletion, lymphopenia, decreased IgG titres, higher septicemia incidence late in study.  
• Mechanism: Suppression of NF-κB, AP-1 transcription; induction of lymphocyte apoptosis.  
• Adverse? YES (immunosuppression).  
• Reversible? YES after withdrawal but takes weeks.  
• Monitorable? CBC (lymphocytes), serum immunoglobulins, lymph-organ size by imaging (in translational context).

E. Hepatobiliary System  
• Finding: Hepatomegaly with vacuolar (glycogen) hepatopathy; sporadic hepatocellular adenomas in males (↑trend, not always statistically significant).  
• Histopathology: Centrilobular hepatocyte enlargement, cytoplasmic glycogen accumulation, minimal necrosis.  
• Mechanism:  
  – GR-driven gluconeogenic enzyme induction (PEPCK, G6Pase)  
  – Secondary oxidative stress may contribute to adenoma promotion.  
• Adverse?  
  – Vacuolar change: ADAPTIVE/Borderline (reversible).  
  – Tumours: POTENTIALLY ADVERSE but species-specific and only at high doses.  
• Reversible? Vacuolar change YES; tumours NO.  
• Monitorable? Serum ALT/AST, liver ultrasound.

F. Metabolism (Carbohydrate/Lipid)  
• Finding: Persistent fasting hyperglycaemia (+ 30–60 %), hyperinsulinaemia early, eventual β-cell exhaustion. ↑Triglycerides and VLDL.  
• Mechanism: Enhanced hepatic gluconeogenesis, peripheral insulin resistance (down-regulation of GLUT-4).  
• Adverse? YES (diabetogenic).  
• Reversible? Generally YES.  
• Monitorable? Fasting glucose, HbA1c, serum lipids.

G. Gastro-intestinal Tract  
• Finding: Focal gastric fundic ulcers (incidence 15–25 %); thinning of intestinal mucosa.  
• Mechanism: Reduced prostaglandin synthesis, impaired epithelial turnover, reduced gastric mucus.  
• Adverse? YES.  
• Reversible? YES with healing; risk of perforation if severe.  
• Monitorable? Occult blood, endoscopy.

H. Cardiovascular / Renal  
• Finding: Mild systolic hypertension (+ 10-15 mm Hg), left-ventricular concentric hypertrophy, renal calcium deposits (nephrocalcinosis).  
• Mechanism:  
  – Prednisone has weak mineralocorticoid activity → Na⁺ retention  
  – Bone resorption → hypercalciuria → nephrocalcinosis  
• Adverse? BORDERLINE (functional changes, low pathology severity).  
• Reversible? YES after cessation.  
• Monitorable? Blood pressure, serum electrolytes, renal ultrasound.

I. Skin / Hair  
• Finding: Dermal thinning, alopecia patches, delayed wound healing.  
• Histopathology: Reduced dermal collagen, attenuated epidermis, fewer hair follicles in anagen phase.  
• Mechanism: Inhibition of collagen gene expression, keratinocyte proliferation.  
• Adverse? USUALLY MINOR; cosmetic.  
• Reversible? PARTLY.  
• Monitorable? Clinical observation, histology if needed.

J. Eye  
• Finding: Posterior sub-capsular cataracts in 10–20 % of animals > 18 months.  
• Mechanism: Oxidative damage and osmotic stress in lens fibres secondary to glucocorticoid-induced dysregulation of lens proteins.  
• Adverse? YES (visual impairment).  
• Reversible? NO (requires surgery).  
• Monitorable? Slit-lamp examination.

K. Reproductive Organs  
• Finding: Testicular atrophy (↓weight 30–40 %), reduced spermatogenesis, irregular oestrous cycling in females.  
• Mechanism: Suppression of GnRH/LH/FSH via HPA axis crosstalk; direct inhibitory effects on Leydig & Sertoli cells.  
• Adverse? YES (fertility impact).  
• Reversible? PARTLY after withdrawal.  
• Monitorable? Organ weights, sperm counts, hormone panels.

4. Overall Assessment  

Toxicodynamic Activity: HIGH – broad genomic actions via glucocorticoid receptor.  
Systemic Availability: HIGH – good oral absorption and metabolic activation.  
Principal Target Organs: Bone, immune/lymphoid, endocrine (adrenal, pituitary), metabolism (liver/pancreas), eye.  
Pattern of Toxicity: Predominantly systemic, multi-organ, dose-related, largely class-specific for glucocorticoids.  
Reversibility: Many functional changes are reversible, but structural lesions in bone and eye are largely permanent once established.  
Clinical Monitorability: Most endpoints have clear translational markers (body weight, CBC, cortisol, BMD, glucose, eye exam, etc.).  

5. Data Gaps & Uncertainties  
• Limited lifetime studies with prednisone per se; much of the long-term rat data rely on prednisolone read-across (active metabolite) – justified because systemic exposure in vivo is largely prednisolone.  
• Tumour findings are inconsistent and may be secondary to hormonal perturbation rather than direct genotoxicity; no evidence of mutagenicity in standard assays.  
• Extrapolation to humans must account for species differences in mineralocorticoid potency and bone turnover.

6. Conclusion  
Lifetime daily MTD dosing of prednisone in the rat produces a well-defined spectrum of chronic glucocorticoid toxicities. The most consequential adverse outcomes are skeletal fragility, immunosuppression, endocrine (HPA) suppression, metabolic dysregulation, and ocular cataracts. Most functional effects can be tracked by routine clinical parameters, and many are at least partially reversible; however, structural bone loss and cataracts are generally irreversible, highlighting the importance of dose optimisation and therapeutic monitoring in clinical settings.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Chronic‐toxicity profile of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6) in adult rats exposed daily at the maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD; 0.001–0.01 µg kg-1 day-1 for a lifetime in most guideline studies; Kociba et al., 1978; NTP, 2006)  

1. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) and toxicodynamic activity  
• Systemic availability: HIGH  
  – Oral absorption ≥ 50 % (Haws et al., 2006).  
  – Extremely lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8), slowly eliminated (t½rat ≈ 3–4 weeks).  
  – Extensive bioaccumulation in liver and adipose tissue.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
  – Potent, persistent agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
  – Down-stream signalling alters > 1 000 AhR-responsive genes (CYP1A1/1A2, COX-2, IL-1β, etc.).  
  – Generates oxidative stress, endocrine disruption, and profound immunomodulation.  

2. Key chronic findings in lifetime rat studies at or near the MTD  

A. Body-weight and general condition  
• “Wasting syndrome”: progressive body-weight depression (≈ 15–20 %) despite normal or slightly reduced feed intake.  
  Mechanism: AhR-driven hypermetabolism, altered energy balance, and cytokine changes (IL-6 ↑, TNF-α ↑).  
  Adversity: Adverse. Reversibility: Partially reversible if dosing stops early; generally sustained during continuous exposure. Clinical monitoring: Body weight, food consumption.

B. Liver  
Functional/clinical pathology  
 – Serum ALT, AST, ALP, GGT ↑; triglycerides ↑, cholesterol ↑; total T4 ↓.  
Histopathology  
 – Early: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction).  
 – Mid-term: steatosis, periportal inflammation, bile-duct hyperplasia, mild fibrosis.  
 – Late: focal nodular hyperplasia, cholangiofibrosis, and increased incidence of hepatocellular adenomas/carcinomas (Kociba et al., 1978; NTP, 2006).  
Mechanism: Persistent AhR activation → CYP1A1/1A2 over-expression, oxidative stress, dysregulated cell proliferation/apoptosis, altered bile acid homeostasis.  
Classification  
 • Hypertrophy: Adaptive, reversible, monitorable (serum enzymes, ultrasound).  
 • Steatosis/fibrosis: Adverse, partially reversible (steatosis) or largely irreversible (fibrosis).  
 • Neoplasia: Adverse, irreversible.

C. Immune and lymphoid organs  
Findings  
 – Thymic atrophy (up to 60 % weight reduction).  
 – Splenic lymphoid depletion; reduced peripheral T-cell counts; impaired antibody responses (IgM plaque ↓).  
Mechanism: AhR-mediated suppression of thymocyte maturation (CD4⁺CD8⁺ → single positive arrest) and altered cytokine milieu.  
Classification  
 – Adverse. Reversible when exposure ceases (thymus repopulates within weeks).  
Monitoring: Immunophenotyping, serum immunoglobulins.

D. Endocrine system  
• Thyroid: Serum total/free T4 ↓, TSH ↑; follicular-cell hypertrophy and hyperplasia; increased follicular adenomas in lifetime studies.  
  Mechanism: Hepatic UGT induction → acceleration of T4 clearance.  
  Adverse vs adaptive: Hormone changes alone adaptive; sustained hypertrophy and tumors adverse. Largely reversible for functional changes; tumors irreversible.  
• Reproductive axis: Decreased circulating estradiol, delayed puberty, altered estrous cycling. Testicular Leydig-cell adenomas reported at high lifetime doses. Mechanism: AhR cross-talk with steroidogenic pathways.  

E. Dermatologic/ocular  
• Follicular hyperkeratosis and sebaceous-gland atrophy (“chloracne-like” lesions in rats).  
  Mechanism: AhR-induced epidermal differentiation and sebocyte apoptosis.  
  Adverse (cosmetic/quality-of-life). Reversible over months post-exposure. Monitorable by skin exam.  

F. Cardiovascular and metabolic  
• Mild bradycardia and hypotension reported in telemetry studies; mechanism involves autonomic imbalance (AhR in cardiac myocytes). Generally adaptive/reversible.  
• Dyslipidemia (↑ VLDL, LDL) secondary to hepatic effects—adverse but monitorable (serum lipids).  

G. Bone and tooth  
• Decreased bone mineral density and delayed incisor eruption linked to altered vitamin D and collagen expression; usually adverse and partially reversible.  

H. Carcinogenicity (weight of evidence)  
Tumor spectrum in chronic rat studies (Kociba et al., 1978; NTP, 2006):  
 – Liver (hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma)  
 – Thyroid follicular-cell adenoma/carcinoma  
 – Lung bronchiolo-alveolar adenoma (in some strains)  
 – Cholangiocarcinoma (rare but treatment-related)  
Mechanism: Non-genotoxic promoter via sustained AhR-mediated cell proliferation and epigenetic changes. Irreversible, adverse.  

3. Summary matrix of effects  

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (TEF = 1.0)  
————————————————————————————————————  
Endpoint | Effect | Adverse? | Reversibility | Clinic monitoring  
Body weight | Wasting | Yes | Partial | BW, nutrition  
Liver enzymes | ↑ALT/AST | Marker | Reversible | Clinical chemistry  
Liver hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes* | Reversible | Imaging/enzymes  
Fibrosis/Neoplasia | Yes | No | Histology/α-fetoprotein  
Immune | Thymic atrophy | Yes | Yes | Flow cytometry  
Thyroid hormones | T4 ↓ | Adaptive | Yes | TSH, free T4  
Thyroid tumors | Yes | No | Ultrasound, thyroglob-Ab  
Skin | Chloracne | Yes/quality | Yes | Dermatology exam  
————————————————————————————————————  
*Becomes adverse if progresses to fibrosis or neoplasia.

4. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Rat-to-human quantitative extrapolation complicated by species differences in AhR affinity (human EC50 roughly 10-fold higher).  
• The true MTD can vary with strain, diet, and fat content; chronic studies often used 0.001–0.01 µg kg-1 day-1, but some newer work suggests slightly higher tolerance.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP1A2 binding and adipose sequestration not fully characterized.  

5. Overall evaluation (weight of evidence)  
Daily lifetime oral exposure of rats to the MTD of TCDD produces a high incidence of systemic, predominantly AhR-mediated toxicities. Liver, immune, and endocrine systems are the principal targets, with secondary metabolic and dermal effects. Many early biochemical and hypertrophic responses are adaptive and reversible, but progression to fibrosis, immune suppression, and neoplasia is clearly adverse and largely irreversible. Continuous clinical monitoring of liver function, thyroid status, immunocompetence, body weight, and skin changes would capture the major effects in a translational setting.

Key references  
• Kociba RJ et al. (1978) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 46:279–303.  
• NTP (2006) TR-521: TCDD Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies in Harlan Sprague-Dawley Rats.  
• Birnbaum LS & Tuomisto J (2000) Environ Health Perspect 108:173–201.  
• Haws LC et al. (2006) Crit Rev Toxicol 36:793–853.  
• IARC (2012) Monograph 100F: TCDD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Chemical assessed  
• Substance: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene)  
• CAS-No.: 71-43-2  
• Relevant physico-chemical properties (oral exposure)  
  – MW 78.1 g·mol-1, log Kow 2.1, vp 12.7 kPa (25 °C)  
  – Water solubility 1.8 g·L-1 (20 °C)  
  – Highly lipophilic enough for rapid gastrointestinal absorption; extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism.  

Summary conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – ≥90 % oral absorption in rats; extensive distribution (Vd ≈ 3–4 L·kg-1).  
• Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH for the haematopoietic system; MEDIUM for liver, immune organs; LOW for most other organs at non-narcosis doses.  
• Principal chronic adverse effects at a life-time maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD ≈ 200–400 mg·kg-bw·day-1) are:  
  1. Progressive bone-marrow toxicity (pancytopenia, aplastic anaemia).  
  2. Immunotoxicity (lymphoid depletion, diminished cell-mediated immunity).  
  3. Genotoxicity leading to haematopoietic neoplasms (especially malignant lymphoma/leukaemia).  
  4. Secondary hepatic and splenic lesions related to altered haematopoiesis.  
  5. Mild renal tubular and forestomach irritative changes (adaptive/secondary).  

Detailed evaluation  

1. Key functional changes (clinical observations, functional tests)  
   a. Haematological alterations  
      • Decreased RBC count, Hb, Hct → normocytic or macrocytic anaemia.  
      • Marked leukopenia (granulocytopenia, lymphopenia).  
      • Thrombocytopenia.  
      • Reticulocytosis early, followed by hypocellularity.  
      Mechanistic basis: Myelotoxic metabolites (benzene-oxide, p-benzoquinone, hydroquinone) formed in liver, transported to bone-marrow, undergo further peroxidase-mediated oxidation → ROS, DNA strand breaks, topoisomerase II poisoning, protein adducts → stem/progenitor cell death and differentiation block.  

   b. Immunological/host-defence effects  
      • Diminished T-cell dependent antibody response, depressed NK-cell activity (in vivo plaque assay, cytotoxicity tests).  
      • Enlarged susceptibility to infections (reported in repeat-dose rat studies: Cronkite et al., 1985; Rosenthal & Snyder, 1987).  
      Mechanism: Overlap with bone-marrow toxicity; additional direct effects on splenic and thymic lymphocytes via oxidative stress and DNA adduct formation.  

   c. Clinical chemistry/organ function  
      • Mild ↑ ALT, AST, ALP during early exposure (enzyme-induction/adaptation).  
      • Hypo-globulinaemia (immunoglobulin depression).  
      • No consistent nephrotoxicity markers except slight ↑ urinary glucose/protein if tubular necrosis appears late.  

   d. Neurobehaviour / body weight  
      • Slight, reversible hypoactivity within 1–2 h post-dosing (CNS depression/narcosis), minimal long-term behavioural change at MTD.  
      • Body-weight gain reduced by 5–15 % (compensatory caloric loss and disease burden).  

2. Morphological / histopathological changes  

   a. Bone marrow  
      • Hypocellularity, fat cell replacement, megakaryocyte depletion (weeks–months).  
      • Dysplastic change; micronuclei, chromosome aberrations (polyploidy).  
      • Late-life tumour spectrum: myeloid leukaemia, lymphoblastic leukaemia, malignant lymphoma (significant ↑ in NTP 2-yr gavage study, 0, 50, 100, 200 mg·kg bw; NTP TR-289, 1986).  
      Adversity: clearly adverse, largely irreversible once neoplasia develops; hypocellularity partly reversible if exposure ceases early.  

   b. Spleen  
      • Extramedullary haematopoiesis initially (adaptive), progressing to atrophy with depletion of white pulp (adverse).  
      • Brown pigment deposition (haemosiderin) due to RBC destruction.  

   c. Thymus & lymph nodes  
      • Lymphoid depletion (cortex shrinkage).  

   d. Liver  
      • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive, CYP2E1 induction).  
      • Foci of cell necrosis, increased mitotic figures at prolonged high dose (adverse but usually mild).  
      • No clear evidence of hepatocellular carcinoma in rats by oral route at MTD; mice show increased hepatomas.  

   e. Kidney  
      • Minimal-to-mild proximal tubular basophilia and necrosis in some studies (adverse, reversible).  

   f. Forestomach  
      • Squamous hyperplasia and mild inflammation (local irritation from gavage bolus; adaptive, reversible).  

3. Mechanistic explanation (integrated)  

   • Metabolism: CYP2E1 (and 1A, 2B) convert benzene to benzene-oxide → non-enzymatic rearrangement to phenol or further oxidation to catechol/hydroquinone. Phase-II conjugates (sulfate, glucuronide) reach bone-marrow where myeloperoxidase produces 1,4-benzoquinone/ROS.  
   • Critical events (Adverse Outcome Pathway for benzene-induced marrow toxicity, OECD AOP-ID 153):  
     KE1: formation of reactive metabolites →  
     KE2: oxidative DNA/protein damage, topo-II inhibition →  
     KE3: stem cell death or mutagenesis →  
     AO: aplastic anaemia and haematopoietic cancers.  
   • Genetic toxicity (micronuclei, gene mutations, chromosomal translocations) documented in rat marrow, consistent with human t(8;21), t(9;11), del(5q) leukemias in exposed workers (IARC 2018).  

4. Classification of each effect  

Effect | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible after cessation? | Clinically monitorable?  
1. Bone-marrow hypocellularity, cytopenias | Adverse | Partly reversible if exposure < chronic threshold; not if aplastic | Yes – CBC, reticulocytes, bone-marrow biopsy  
2. Leukaemia/lymphoma | Adverse | Irreversible | Yes – haematology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics  
3. Immunodepression | Adverse | Largely reversible | Yes – immunoglobulins, lymphocyte phenotyping  
4. Liver hypertrophy | Adaptive | Fully reversible | Yes – serum liver enzymes, ultrasound  
5. Mild liver necrosis | Adverse | Reversible at dose reduction | Yes – ALT, AST, histology  
6. Renal tubular changes | Adverse (low severity) | Reversible | Yes – urine analysis, BUN/creatinine  
7. Forestomach hyperplasia | Adaptive (local irritation) | Reversible | Not directly relevant to humans (no forestomach)  

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  

• Oral lifetime studies less numerous than inhalation; inhalation shows similar profile but at lower internal dose due to respiratory metabolism.  
• Sex differences: female rats slightly less sensitive in some studies (lower CYP2E1).  
• Lack of high-resolution omics data for early biomarkers in rats – extrapolation relies on human mechanistic information.  

6. Human relevance  

Mode-of-action is concordant across species (similar metabolic pathways, bone marrow targets) → effects considered qualitatively predictive for humans.  

References (representative)  
• NTP (1986) Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in F344/N Rats and B6C3F1 Mice, Gavage Studies. NTP Technical Report 289.  
• Medinsky MA & Schlosser PM (1993) Critical Rev Toxicol 23: 171-204.  
• IARC Monograph 120 (2018) Benzene.  
• Snyder R (2012) Chem-Biol Interact 196: 30-36.  
• Bus JS & Popp JA (1987) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 87: 329-349.  

Overall, lifetime daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose of benzene to adult rats produces pronounced, adverse, largely irreversible haematopoietic toxicity leading to anaemia, immunosuppression and leukaemia/lymphoma; secondary liver, spleen and kidney lesions occur with lower severity and are mostly reversible or adaptive upon cessation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Chronic Toxicological Profile of Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) in Adult Rats  
Route: Oral (gavage or drinking-water)  
Regimen: Daily administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for ≥24 months (full life-span)  

Key references consulted  
• NTP Technical Report 559 – Sodium Arsenite (drinking-water, 2-yr study in F344 rats & B6C3F1 mice, 2012)  
• NTP Technical Report 447 – Arsenic Trioxide (gavage, 2-yr study in F344 rats & B6C3F1 mice, 2001)  
• JECFA & ATSDR Toxicological Profiles for Inorganic Arsenic (2007, 2019 updates)  
• IARC Monograph 100C (2012) – Arsenic and inorganic arsenic compounds  
• PubChem CID 681 (arsenite ion) and ECHA dossier on “Sodium arsenite”  

Systemic availability & toxicokinetic considerations  
• Oral absorption: 70–95 % in rats; bioavailability therefore classified as HIGH.  
• Distribution: rapidly reaches liver, kidney, spleen, lung, skin, nervous tissue; readily crosses placenta.  
• Metabolism: hepatic reduction (AsV→AsIII) followed by oxidative methylation to MMAsIII/V and DMAsIII/V via AS3MT; methylated species are still biologically active.  
• Excretion: mainly urine (t½ in rat blood ≈ 8–16 h for inorganic As; up to 30 h for methylated forms).  
Because the animal receives the MTD every day, steady-state tissue burdens are achieved and detoxification capacity (methylation, glutathione) is chronically challenged.

Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (strong electrophile that targets thiol-containing proteins, perturbs redox homeostasis, and shows multisystem carcinogenicity).

1. Survival, body weight and general condition  
Observed changes  
• Slight (≤10–15 %) depression in terminal body weight despite normal food availability.  
• Early deaths largely attributable to tumor burden at later time points.  
Interpretation  
• Body-weight depression is adverse but reversible after cessation.  
• Decreased survival is adverse and irreversible.

2. Haematological system  
Findings  
• Normocytic, normochromic anaemia (↓RBC 10–20 %, ↓Hb, ↓Hct).  
• Leukopenia (total WBC ↓15–30 %), occasional thrombocytopenia.  
Mechanistic basis  
• AsIII binds lipoic-acid–dependent enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase)→ mitochondrial dysfunction in bone-marrow stem cells; additionally, interference with heme biosynthesis (inhibition of δ-aminolevulinic-acid dehydratase).  
Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring  
• Adverse. Partial recovery likely within weeks of exposure cessation.  
• Easily monitorable by complete blood count (CBC).

3. Liver  
Functional / clinical chemistry  
• ↑ALT, AST, ALP (2- to 3-fold).  
Histopathology  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy progressing to degeneration/necrosis; regenerative hyperplasia; bile-duct proliferation.  
• Increased incidence of hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma (males > females).  
Mechanistic explanation  
• Oxidative stress and inhibition of the Nrf2-Keap1 defense axis; altered DNA methylation, promotion of cell proliferation.  
Adverse? – Yes.  
Reversibility – Degenerative changes partly reversible; neoplasms irreversible.  
Monitoring – Serum liver enzymes, imaging (ultrasound/CT for tumours).

4. Kidney  
Findings  
• ↑BUN, creatinine; mild proteinuria.  
• Tubular epithelial degeneration/regeneration, interstitial fibrosis, occasional glomerular sclerosis.  
Mechanism  
• Proximal tubular uptake of trivalent arsenicals; high affinity for vicinal thiols in mitochondrial enzymes→ ROS, ATP depletion.  
Adverse, partly reversible (fibrosis and glomerular lesions are not).  
Monitoring – Serum creatinine, urinary β2-microglobulin or N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase.

5. Skin and appendages  
• Epidermal hyperkeratosis-hyperplasia on footpads, occasional papillomas/ squamous-cell carcinomas.  
Mechanism – Direct dermal accumulation plus systemic oxidative/epigenetic effects on keratinocytes.  
Adverse; hyperkeratosis might regress, tumours irreversible.  
Clinical monitoring – Dermatologic examination, histopathologic biopsy.

6. Lung  
• Increased bronchiolo-alveolar adenomas/carcinomas in some studies (sex- and strain-dependent).  
Mechanism – Redistribution of methylated arsenicals to lung, ROS-mediated DNA damage.  
Adverse, irreversible; monitor by imaging (X-ray, CT).

7. Cardiovascular system  
Observations  
• Mild hypertension; ECG alterations; microscopic myocardial degeneration/fibrosis and vascular medial hypertrophy.  
Mechanism  
• Arsenite-induced endothelial dysfunction (↓NO), oxidative stress, and Ca2+-handling disruption in cardiomyocytes.  
Adverse; partly reversible if fibrosis limited.  
Monitorable – Blood pressure, echocardiography, cardiac enzymes.

8. Nervous system  
• Peripheral motor neuropathy (decreased grip strength, nerve-conduction velocity); mild cerebellar Purkinje-cell loss.  
Mechanism – Mitochondrial inhibition and axonal transport disruption; arsenic-induced neuroinflammation.  
Adverse but largely reversible over months with clearance.  
Monitoring – Neurological examination, EMG/nerve-conduction studies.

9. Gastrointestinal tract  
• Persistent but mild diarrhea, reduced nutrient absorption; histology shows mucosal erosion and crypt hyperplasia in duodenum/jejunum.  
Mechanism – Direct irritancy plus inhibition of ATP-dependent ion pumps.  
Adverse but reversible and clinically monitorable (stool consistency, endoscopy).

10. Immune system  
• ↓Spleen weight, reduced lymphocyte proliferation (in vitro), muted antibody responses.  
• Histology: mild lymphoid depletion in spleen and thymus.  
Mechanism – ROS, apoptosis via MAPK activation.  
Adverse, reversible; monitorable by immunophenotyping.

11. Endocrine / metabolic  
• Impaired glucose tolerance and mild insulin resistance.  
Mechanism – Inhibition of protein-tyrosine phosphatases, dysregulation of insulin signaling.  
Adverse, reversible; monitor by fasting glucose/HbA1c.

12. Reproductive organs (incidental in chronic study)  
• Slight testicular atrophy, decreased epididymal sperm motility.  
Mechanistic link – Oxidative stress and interference with steroidogenesis.  
Adverse, may be reversible after wash-out.  
Monitorable – Sperm analysis, serum testosterone.

Weight-of-evidence statement  
Multiple high-quality chronic studies in rats and mice consistently demonstrate multisystem toxicity and carcinogenicity at or near the MTD. The pattern is coherent with human epidemiological data. No reliable evidence of an adaptive, beneficial response at these doses; virtually all effects are adverse. Some functional and biochemical changes (e.g., enzyme elevations, mild anaemia, neuropathy) show potential for reversibility, whereas structural lesions (fibrosis, neoplasia) are not reversible.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Quantitative NOAELs are strain- and matrix-specific; true MTD differs between drinking-water and gavage studies.  
• Limited data on neurobehavioural endpoints in life-time studies.  
• Interaction between dietary methyl-donor status and arsenic methylation capacity not routinely evaluated.

Summary classification  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent multisystem toxicant & known carcinogen).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Methyl-mercury (principally present as methyl-mercury chloride, CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: monomethylmercury, MeHg⁺).  
Scenario: Daily oral administration to adult rats for the whole life span at the “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD, i.e. highest dose producing <10 % body-weight decrement and no excessive early mortality).

Summary conclusion  
1. Systemic availability: HIGH – ≈90–95 % oral absorption; efficient distribution to all tissues, especially CNS.  
2. Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – covalent binding to thiol-containing proteins, mitochondrial impairment, oxidative stress, Ca²⁺-dysregulation, microtubule damage.  
3. Principal chronic target organs/effects (weight-of-evidence from lifetime rat studies and mechanistic concordance with other species):  
   • Central & peripheral nervous system (primary) – irreversible, adverse, clinically monitorable.  
   • Kidney (secondary, after demethylation) – partly reversible, adverse.  
   • Immune modulation & mild hepatotoxicity – usually adaptive at MTD but may become adverse at higher doses.  
   • Body-weight suppression – adaptive/toxicologically non-specific.  
   • No convincing evidence for chronic cardiopulmonary or adult reproductive injury at the MTD in rats (developmental toxicity is sensitive but outside the current scope).  

Detailed evaluation  
A. Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: 90–95 % within 24 h (NTP 1993; Clark & Sterne, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1988).  
• Distribution: Forms a cysteine conjugate that is mistaken for methionine and carried across membranes by L-type amino-acid transporter-1 (LAT-1). Highest steady-state concentrations in brain, liver, kidney; crosses placenta and enters milk.  
• Metabolism: Slow demethylation by intestinal micro-flora and in situ in kidney/liver to inorganic Hg²⁺.  
• Elimination half-life in rat: 15–30 d for MeHg⁺; 40–60 d for resultant inorganic Hg in kidney. Fecal excretion dominates; minor urinary clearance.  
→ Lifelong daily dosing produces progressive accumulation, especially in brain and kidney, until steady state (∼6–10 half-lives).

B. Functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology findings

1. Central nervous system  
   a. Functional manifestations  
      • Progressive hind-limb weakness, ataxia, intention tremor, hypo-/hyper-reflexia, visual impairment, auditory startle loss, altered operant performance (Suter 1983; Newland & Reile 1999).  
   b. Histopathology  
      • Neuronal loss/necrosis in cerebellar granule cell layer, deeper layers of cerebral cortex (visual, auditory, somatosensory), dorsal root ganglia; Wallerian degeneration in ascending dorsal columns and sciatic nerve (Inoue et al. 1985; Chang 1977).  
      • Gliosis and microglial activation.  
   c. Mechanism  
      • MeHg–cysteinate readily enters neurons→ binds sulfhydryl groups in tubulin, actin, mitochondrial enzymes; inhibits seleno- and thiol-dependent antioxidant systems (GPx, TrxR) leading to ROS accumulation.  
      • Disturbs intracellular Ca²⁺ homeostasis, triggers excitotoxicity via NMDA receptor potentiation and glutamate transporter inhibition.  
      • Impairs microtubule polymerisation, blocking axonal transport.  
   d. Toxicological characterisation  
      • Adverse: YES (produces functional impairment).  
      • Reversible: NO (axonal/neuronal loss is permanent; some functional compensation possible).  
      • Monitorable in clinic: Yes—neurobehavioural tests, nerve-conduction velocity, visual evoked potentials, auditory brainstem response; brain MRI (for humans).  

2. Peripheral nervous system  
   a. Functional: Decreased tail-flick latency, paresthesia, slowed nerve conduction.  
   b. Histology: Axonal degeneration of large myelinated fibres; Schwann cell myelin splitting.  
   c. Mechanism: Same as CNS plus distal axonopathy due to long axonal length and high metabolic demand.  
   d. Adverse & largely irreversible. Monitorable through EMG/NCV, quantitative sensory testing.  

3. Kidney  
   a. Clinical pathology  
      • Mild proteinuria, enzymuria (N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, NAG), elevated urinary micro-globulins; occasional mild BUN/creatinine rise late in life (Bernhoft 1994).  
   b. Histopathology  
      • Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) epithelial swelling, mitochondrial damage, occasional necrosis; regenerative proliferation; chronic low-grade interstitial nephritis.  
   c. Mechanism  
      • Demethylation to inorganic Hg²⁺ within renal tissue; Hg²⁺ binds to thiol groups on PCT membranes & enzymes → mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress. Cellular repair is efficient at MTD, explaining partial reversibility.  
   d. Toxicological characterisation  
      • Adverse but often mild at MTD; partially reversible after cessation.  
      • Monitorable: urine protein/β₂-micro-globulin, urinary Hg, serum creatinine, renal ultrasound.  

4. Liver  
   a. Findings (long-term rat data)  
      • Slight hepatomegaly (relative weight ↑10–15 %); microvesicular steatosis in scattered hepatocytes; minimal increases in ALT/AST (<2× control).  
   b. Mechanism: Induction of metallothionein and phase-II conjugation enzymes—adaptive detoxification response.  
   c. Characterisation  
      • Generally adaptive at MTD; reversible and not associated with overt dysfunction.  
      • Monitorable: Serum transaminases, liver ultrasound.  

5. Immune system  
   a. Observed changes  
      • Enlarged spleen; mild lymphopenia, reduced NK-cell activity; increased auto-antibody titers in susceptible strains (Ilback 1991).  
   b. Mechanistic hypothesis: Mercuric haptenation of self proteins → breaking of tolerance, Th2 shift.  
   c. Characterisation  
      • Borderline adverse; effects regress upon exposure cessation; monitorable via CBC, auto-antibody panels.  

6. Body-weight and feed-efficiency  
   • Persistent ≈8–12 % reduction in body-weight gain at MTD. Non-specific energy cost of detoxification and mild anorexia.  
   • Adaptive/non-adverse (OECD chronic toxicity guidance). Reversible.  

C. Carcinogenicity  
• Lifetime rat bioassays (Takaoka 1966; NTP 1993) show no robust increase in tumour incidence after adjusting for decreased survival; IARC considers MeHg “Group 2B—possibly carcinogenic” based on limited human data, not strongly supported by rat studies. Mutagenic potential is low; genotoxicity arises only at cytotoxic concentrations via oxidative stress.  
→ At the rat MTD, carcinogenicity is not a defining chronic endpoint.

D. Weight-of-evidence statement  
The CNS is the critical target for chronic oral MeHg exposure in adult rats, with renal injury as the secondary endpoint. Both derive from the metal’s high systemic availability, persistence and covalent interaction with cellular thiols leading to oxidative injury. The profile is congruent across species (rat, mouse, primate, human) and is mechanistically well understood through impairment of antioxidant defences, calcium homeostasis and microtubule function.

E. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Very high-quality life-time rat data exist; however, most used MeHg hydroxide rather than chloride—speciation differences are minimal but should be noted.  
• Limited information on subtle endocrine endpoints in aged rats.  
• Strain-specific auto-immunity not fully characterised.

F. Translation to human clinical monitoring (for context)  
• Blood/whole-blood and hair total Hg as exposure biomarkers.  
• Urine inorganic Hg for renal burden.  
• Neuro-functional battery (visual fields, coordination, tremor, hearing thresholds).  
• Renal tubular injury markers (NAG, KIM-1).

References (abbreviated)  
1. National Toxicology Program (NTP). Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Methylmercury Chloride in F344/N Rats and B6C3F1 Mice. TR-408, 1993.  
2. Chang LW. Methylmercury: neuropathology. Acta Neuropathol 1977;41:293-306.  
3. Inoue K et al. Chronic effects of methylmercury on rat peripheral nerve. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1985;78:295-307.  
4. Newland MC, Reile PA. Learning and behavior after chronic, low-level methylmercury exposure. Toxicol Sci 1999;50:179-187.  
5. Ilback NG. Immunomodulation by methylmercury. Toxicology 1991;67:117-132.  
6. Bernhoft RA. Mercury toxicity and treatment: a review. J Environ Public Health 2012:460508.  
7. Clarkson TW, Magos L. The toxicology of mercury and its chemical compounds. Crit Rev Toxicol 2006;36:609-662.  

End of assessment.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Chronic Toxicological Profile of Vinyl Chloride (CAS 75-01-4) in Adult Rats After Lifetime Daily Oral Administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Chemical identity and key toxicokinetic (TK) features
• Synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene, VC  
• Volatile, lipophilic gas (b.p. –13.4 °C) that is readily absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract when administered in solution or corn-oil gavage.  
• First-pass hepatic extraction is high (CYP2E1-mediated), yielding the reactive metabolites chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both form DNA- and protein-reactive etheno-adducts and generate ROS.  
• Systemic availability: High for the liver (portal delivery, rapid metabolism); moderate for extra-hepatic tissues because most parent VC is cleared in the liver.  
→ Expert rating:  
   – Systemic availability: HIGH for liver, MEDIUM for other organs  
   – Intrinsic (toxicodynamic) reactivity: HIGH (direct-acting alkylating metabolites)

2. Experimental database relevant to lifetime oral exposure
Key studies (rats unless stated otherwise)  
• Feron et al., 1979, Toxicology 13: 143–157 – 18-month drinking-water study (0, 0.01, 0.1, 1 mg/L ≈ up to 0.05 mg/kg d).  
• Maltoni et al., 1981, Am. J. Ind. Med. 2: 335–378 – 2-year gavage study (0, 1.7, 5, 50 mg/kg d).  
• Til et al., 1991, Food Chem. Toxicol. 29: 713–718 – 12-month follow-up on Maltoni cohort.  
• NTP, 1998 – gavage cancer bioassay in B6C3F1 mice and F344 rats (0, 0.6, 1.3, 2.5 mg/kg d; study stopped early because of excessive liver tumors).  
No guideline-level chronic oral study at very high doses exists, so the profile below integrates oral data with the far larger inhalation cancer database and mechanistic information (read-across).

3. Expected effects at an oral MTD (≈ 50–100 mg/kg d in the rat, defined as the highest dose producing <10 % reduction in body-weight gain and <10 % excess mortality unrelated to neoplasia)

A. General systemic/clinical observations  
• Slight, persistent reduction (5–10 %) in body-weight gain; decreased food consumption at onset (adaptive, reversible).  
• Sporadic transient hypoactivity after gavage (CNS depressant effect of parent VC; reversible, monitorable by functional observation battery).

B. Liver – the primary target organ  
Functional / clinical chemistry  
• Marked, dose-related increases in serum ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT, total bilirubin.  
• Mild hypoalbuminaemia, elevated cholesterol and triglycerides (altered hepatocellular synthetic & lipid metabolism).  
Morphology / histopathology  
1. Early (≤ 6 months): centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, single-cell necrosis, increased mitotic figures (adaptive/regenerative).  
2. Mid-term (6–18 months):  
   – Oval-cell/ductular proliferation, cholangiofibrosis  
   – Foci of cellular alteration (eosinophilic & basophilic) – preneoplastic  
3. Late (≥18 months):  
   – Hemangiosarcoma (angiosarcoma of liver) – hallmark lesion  
   – Hepatocellular adenoma and carcinoma  
   – Cholangiocarcinoma (occasionally)  
Mechanistic explanation  
• CEO and CAA form N²,3-ethenoguanine and 1,N⁶-ethenoadenine DNA adducts, resulting in point mutations (K-ras, p53).  
• Chronic necrosis/regeneration provides a proliferative environment that fixes mutations.  
• Endothelial cells show high CYP2E1 activity and are therefore vulnerable, explaining angiosarcoma.  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Enzyme induction and hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible after cessation.  
• Fibrosis and tumors: clearly adverse, irreversible.  
Clinical monitoring in humans: serum liver enzymes, ultrasound/ elastography for fibrosis, serum/plasma VC-associated protein adducts, urinary thiodiglycolic acid (exposure biomarker).

C. Hematopoietic system  
Findings  
• Mild, chronic normocytic anemia and thrombocytopenia reported at high inhalation doses and predicted for oral MTD: secondary to hepatic dysfunction and marrow suppression by CAA.  
Mechanism: oxidative stress and direct stem-cell DNA damage.  
Adverse? Yes. Reversible? Partially, after exposure ends.  
Clinical monitoring: CBC, reticulocyte counts.

D. Immune / spleen  
• Splenomegaly with increased extramedullary hematopoiesis; occasional hemangiosarcomas in spleen (adverse, irreversible). Likely driven by circulating CEO entering splenic endothelial cells.

E. Nervous system  
• At high Cmax after gavage transient CNS depression; in lifetime studies no consistent neuropathology. Mechanism: parent VC acts as a narcotic gas; metabolites do not reach brain at toxic levels due to first-pass removal.  
Adverse? Largely adaptive & reversible.

F. Kidney  
• Slight increased relative weight; minimal tubular degeneration in some studies, but not considered a robust lesion. Mechanism: systemic exposure to CAA; low incidence. Adversity: questionable; likely mild and reversible.

G. Reproductive / developmental  
No consistent effects in chronic studies once animals reach adulthood; therefore not anticipated at oral MTD in adult male/female rats.

4. Weight-of-Evidence summary
• Robust evidence (IARC group 1 human carcinogen) that lifetime exposure produces liver angiosarcoma and other tumors in rats; oral route increases first-pass delivery, so hazard is at least as great as for inhalation.  
• Non-neoplastic liver toxicity (fibrosis, enzyme changes) precedes tumorigenesis and is mechanistically linked to reactive metabolites.  
• Extrahepatic effects (hematological, splenic, mild renal) have lower strength of evidence but are plausible and seen sporadically.  
• Overall confidence: high for hepatotoxicity and carcinogenicity; moderate for hematological and splenic lesions; low for kidney and neurotoxicity.

5. Classification of main effects
Adaptive / reversible  
• Early hepatocellular hypertrophy & enzyme induction  
• Transient CNS depression  
Adverse / irreversible  
• Portal fibrosis, cholangiofibrosis  
• Angiosarcoma, hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma  
• Persistent anemia/thrombocytopenia (partially reversible)  
Monitorability in the clinic  
• Serum liver enzymes, platelet count, hemoglobin  
• Urinary thiodiglycolic acid (exposure)  
• Imaging (ultrasound, MRI) for liver lesions  
• Serum etheno-DNA adducts (research biomarkers)

6. Data gaps & uncertainties
• Few guideline-quality oral lifetime studies at high doses; most data extrapolated from inhalation.  
• Limited information on threshold vs. non-threshold dose–response for non-neoplastic liver effects.  
• Species differences in CYP2E1 activity may influence quantitative extrapolation to humans.

7. Overall conclusion
Daily lifetime oral exposure of rats to vinyl chloride at the MTD leads to:
1. High systemic (hepatic) availability of reactive metabolites.  
2. Early adaptive liver hypertrophy followed by progressive, clearly adverse hepatotoxicity, culminating in irreversible liver fibrosis and multiple tumor types, dominantly hepatic angiosarcoma.  
3. Secondary hematological and splenic effects; minor kidney and transient neurofunctional changes.  
The toxicodynamic activity of vinyl chloride is high, the critical effects are adverse and largely irreversible, and the key events (DNA adduct formation, oxidative stress, regenerative proliferation) are well understood mechanistically.
