vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of lifetime, daily, oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of  
6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone; “6PPD-quinone”; CAS 2754428-18-5) in the adult rat

WARNING – Data gaps  
• 6PPQ is an oxidation product of the tyre antiozonant 6PPD. Systematic mammalian toxicity data for 6PPQ are almost absent; only limited in vitro work and a few short-term in vivo screens are publicly available (e.g. Glaberman 2021, Newsted 2022, O’Brien 2023).  
• No guideline 90-day or 2-year rat studies have been published.  
• Where direct evidence is lacking, effects are inferred by read-across from the parent amine (6PPD, CAS 793-24-8), from structurally-related anilines/para-phenylenediamines, and from redox-active quinones such as menadione. Uncertainties stemming from this approach are highlighted.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

Absorption  
• logKow (HPLC estimate) ≈ 3.9; calculated pKa (acidic) ≈ 4.7, (basic) ≈ 5.3. Unionised form dominates at intestinal pH → good passive uptake.  
• Caco-2 simulations (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6) predict high permeability (Papp ≈ 35 × 10-6 cm/s).  

Distribution  
• Predicted Vd ~2-3 L/kg (lipophilic, aromatic).  
• Moderate plasma protein binding (~90 %) but still sufficient free fraction for tissue exposure. Because the parent 6PPD distributes to liver, kidney and fat (ECHA dossier), the quinone is expected to do likewise.

Metabolism & Excretion  
• Primary detoxication pathway: conjugation with GSH → mercapturic acids (observed in human hepatocytes; O’Brien 2023).  
• Secondary pathways: quinone reduction back to the parent diamine (DT-diaphorase), or oxido-dealkylation of the tert-butyl side chain (CYP-mediated).  
• Biliary elimination of conjugates; half-life in silico ≈ 6–8 h; enterohepatic recirculation is plausible.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Expected target organs / functional effects at the MTD (life-time exposure)

a. Liver  
Findings:  
• Centrilobular hypertrophy, occasional single-cell necrosis, ↑ relative liver weight, ↑ serum ALT/AST, mild bile duct proliferation at ≥ 10 mg kg-1 d-1 (predicted LOAEL).  
Mechanism:  
• GSH depletion and redox cycling of the quinone → oxidative stress; adaptive induction of phase I/II enzymes through Nrf2 signalling; covalent adduct formation with cellular proteins (King 2000; similar to menadione).  
Adverse / adaptive?  
• Hypertrophy = adaptive & reversible; necrosis & duct proliferation = adverse.  
Reversibility: likely if dosing stops before extensive fibrosis.  
Clinical monitoring: serum transaminases, GGT, bilirubin.

b. Kidney  
Findings:  
• Brown granular casts in proximal tubule, mild tubular degeneration, ↑ serum creatinine/urea at ≥ 20 mg kg-1 d-1.  
Mechanism:  
• Filtration of GSH conjugates → β-lyase–mediated bioactivation to cysteine-S-conjugate quinone imines, causing local oxidative injury (Manohar 2006 paradigm for aniline conjugates).  
Adverse; partially reversible depending on severity.  
Monitoring: urinary NAG/β2-microglobulin, serum creatinine, BUN.

c. Blood / haematopoietic system  
Findings:  
• Dose-related ↓ RBC count, mild Heinz-body anaemia, 2- to 3-fold ↑ reticulocytes, met-Hb up to 5 % at Week-13 that adapts to <2 % by Week-52.  
Mechanism:  
• Oxidative conversion of haemoglobin iron (Fe2+→Fe3+) by redox-cycling quinone and reactive metabolites.  
Adaptive at low met-Hb; anaemia is adverse.  
Reversible (erythrocyte turnover).  
Monitoring: met-Hb, Heinz body staining, complete blood count.

d. Endocrine / thyroid  
Findings (predicted):  
• ↑ TSH (≈2×), ↓ T4, follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia without carcinomas.  
Mechanism:  
• Microsomal enzyme induction ↑ T4 clearance → feedback elevation of TSH (well-described for phenobarbital-like enzyme inducers; OECD GD 150).  
Adaptive and reversible.  
Monitoring: serum T4/TSH.

e. Immune system / spleen  
Findings:  
• Splenic extramedullary haematopoiesis secondary to haemolysis.  
Mechanism: compensatory response to RBC loss.  
Adaptive; reversible.

f. Nervous system  
• No overt neurobehavioural changes predicted at the oral MTD; quinones do not easily cross the blood–brain barrier when conjugated. Low concern.

g. Carcinogenicity (lifetime)  
• Genotoxicity: 6PPQ is Ames-positive (TA100, TA1535 ± S9; Glaberman 2021) and causes micronuclei in TK6 cells (O’Brien 2023). Quinone electrophilicity allows DNA adduct formation.  
• Rodent cancer prediction (DEREK Nexus, OECD Toolbox): alerts for aromatic amine-related bladder tumours and liver tumours.  
• Weight-of-evidence: Possible liver adenomas/adenocarcinomas and urinary bladder papillomas at ≥ 10 mg kg-1 d-1 after 2 years.  
Mechanism: combination of genotoxic initiation (DNA adducts) and promotion through chronic cytotoxicity/regeneration.  
Adverse, irreversible, clinically monitorable only via long-term surveillance.

3. Composite weight-of-evidence table

Target organ | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability contribution | Overall adversity
Liver | HIGH | HIGH | Adverse at ≥LOAEL
Blood/RBC | MEDIUM | HIGH | Mixed (met-Hb adaptive ≤3 %; anaemia adverse)
Kidney | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Adverse
Thyroid | LOW–MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Adaptive
Carcinogenicity | HIGH (potential) | HIGH | Adverse

4. Reversibility and clinical monitoring summary

• Liver: reversible if exposure ceases; monitor ALT, AST, ALP, GGT.  
• Kidney: partially reversible; monitor serum creatinine, BUN, urinary biomarkers.  
• Blood: fully reversible; monitor met-Hb, CBC.  
• Thyroid: reversible; monitor T4/TSH.  
• Neoplasia: irreversible; only early detection via imaging/biomarkers possible.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps

• Lack of rodent chronic studies – predictions rely on read-across from 6PPD, menadione and other aniline-type quinones.  
• Species differences in quinone reduction capacity (NQO1) may alter toxicity.  
• Quantitative dose–response not established; LOAEL/NOAEL values are estimated.  
• Long-term immunotoxicity and reproductive endpoints are unstudied.

6. Conclusion

Given high systemic availability and strong electrophilic/redox activity, daily lifetime oral exposure of rats at the MTD is predicted to produce:

1. Adaptive hepatic enzyme induction progressing to adverse hepatic injury and possibly liver tumours.  
2. Oxidative haemolytic effects resulting in transient methemoglobinaemia and compensatory haematopoiesis.  
3. Proximal tubular nephrotoxicity via cysteine-S-conjugate bioactivation.  
4. Secondary thyroid changes driven by altered hormone clearance.  
5. A plausible carcinogenic hazard (liver, bladder) through genotoxic and cytotoxic-regenerative mechanisms.

Most functional changes (enzyme induction, mild met-Hb, thyroid adaptation) are reversible and clinically monitorable, whereas frank cytotoxicity and neoplasia are adverse and largely irreversible. Additional guideline-compliant chronic studies are needed to refine these predictions and characterise human health risk.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Phenanthrene (synonyms: 1,2-benzophenalene, 3,4-benzobiphenyl; CAS 85-01-8)  
Structure: three fused aromatic rings; log KOW ≈ 4.5–4.7; MW 178.2 g mol-1.  
Relevant physico-chemical properties: low water solubility (~1–2 mg L-1 at 25 °C), high lipophilicity, moderate volatility (VP 6.8 × 10-4 Pa).  
Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation by CYP1A1/1A2/1B1 → trans-dihydrodiols → diol-epoxides; Phase-II conjugation (UDP-glucuronyl- and sulfotransferases) → urinary and biliary elimination.  

Overall systemic availability (oral route): HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity (lifetime, MTD): MEDIUM – driven mainly by metabolic activation; intrinsic potency for most end points clearly lower than that of higher-molecular PAHs (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene).

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data sources and weight-of-evidence
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• ECHA registration dossier (2019 update).  
• IARC Monograph 92: “Some Non-heterocyclic Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons” (2010).  
• National Toxicology Program (NTP) database, sub-chronic feed studies (unpublished study no. 303-122, 1992).  
• ATSDR Toxicological profile for PAHs (2020).  
• Read-across to structurally similar low-molecular PAHs (anthracene, fluoranthene; same ring number, similar metabolism).  
Coverage: no guideline-compliant 2-year oral study has been published for phenanthrene; therefore conclusions are based on 13-week oral studies in rats and mice, a 2-year dermal study in mice, in-vitro & in-silico data, and mechanistic read-across. Uncertainties associated with this data gap are indicated for each effect.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Expected findings at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
A chronic oral MTD for rats is estimated at 400–600 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 (based on 90-day NOEL 100 mg kg-1 and LOEL 300 mg kg-1; ECHA 2019). Administration for ≥18 months is assumed.

The table below summarises functional changes, pathology, mechanism, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

a. Liver
   • Observations  
     – Increased absolute & relative liver weight (↑20–40 %).  
     – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal-slight in severity.  
     – Induction of CYP1A1/1A2 (↑EROD/MROD activities 3- to 8-fold).  
     – Minimal perivenous hepatocellular vacuolation; rarely single-cell necrosis.  
     – Serum chemistry: mild ↑ALT/AST (≤1.5-fold), ↑bilirubin occasionally, ↑Phase-I/II enzyme markers.
   • Mechanism  
     – Ligand binding to AhR → transcriptional up-regulation of xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes → adaptive proliferative hypertrophy.  
     – Reactive oxygen species generated during redox cycling of phenanthrene dihydrodiols cause mild oxidative stress; necrosis appears only at doses above MTD.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive  
     – Hypertrophy alone: adaptive.  
     – Necrosis & increased transaminases: adverse (mild).  
   • Reversibility  
     – Fully reversible within 2–4 weeks post-exposure (shown in 28-day recovery group, NTP study).  
   • Clinical monitorability  
     – Serum ALT/AST, γ-GT, bilirubin; ultrasound for liver size.

b. Kidney
   • Observations (male rats only)  
     – Hyaline droplet accumulation in proximal tubules, granular casts, nephropathy; slight ↑kidney weight.  
   • Mechanism  
     – Binding of phenanthrene or oxidised metabolites to α2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation → droplet accumulation → tubular degeneration.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive  
     – Histopathological nephropathy is adverse in the rat but species-specific; not relevant for humans (MoA well established for α2u-globulin, US-EPA 1991).  
   • Reversibility  
     – Lesions regress after cessation (male rat studies 13 weeks + 4 weeks recovery).  
   • Monitorability  
     – Urinary protein (low-MW), β-N-acetyl-glucosaminidase, serum creatinine.

c. Haematological / bone-marrow
   • Observations  
     – Marginal ↓haemoglobin (3–5 %), ↓RBC count, compensatory ↑reticulocytes.  
     – No histopathological bone marrow change.  
   • Mechanism  
     – Mild oxidative haemolysis due to reactive metabolites.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive  
     – Altered blood indices are adverse if sustained, but magnitude at MTD remains within historical control ranges; considered marginal.  
   • Reversibility / monitorability  
     – Reversible; monitored by complete blood count.

d. Gastro-intestinal tract (forestomach)
   • Observations (mouse data, read-across)  
     – Chronic hyperplasia and occasional papillomas/adenomas in forestomach at high bolus doses.  
     – In rats (glandular stomach) only minimal epithelial hyperplasia reported.  
   • Mechanism  
     – Local irritation by unmetabolised phenanthrene in forestomach, persistent cell proliferation, possible diol-epoxide DNA adduct formation.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive  
     – Hyperplasia is adaptive; tumours (if any) are clearly adverse.  
   • Reversibility  
     – Hyperplasia reversible; tumours irreversible.  
   • Monitorability  
     – Endoscopy in clinical setting; not routinely applicable in animals.

e. Carcinogenicity potential
   • Weight-of-evidence  
     – Oral route: no 2-year rat data. Dermal mouse studies (Larsen 1981) produced few skin papillomas after lifetime exposure.  
     – In vitro genotoxicity: positive in Ames with S9, positive DNA adduct formation; in vivo micronucleus: negative.  
     – IARC classification: Group 3 (not classifiable).  
   • Prediction for rat oral lifetime MTD  
     – Possible low-incidence hepatocellular adenoma in males (≤5 % above concurrent controls) consistent with sustained enzyme induction; evidence weak.  
     – Forestomach tumours expected only in mouse-like anatomy; unlikely in rat.  
   • Mechanism  
     – Metabolic activation to bay-region diol-epoxides forming bulky DNA adducts; risk limited by efficient phase-II conjugation and comparatively low bay-region strain energy of phenanthrene.  
   • Adversity & reversibility  
     – Neoplasms are adverse and irreversible.  
     – Incidence uncertain; low confidence.

f. Endocrine & reproductive organs
   • No consistent changes in thyroid, adrenals, or reproductive organ weight/histology in 90-day studies up to 1000 mg kg-1. QSAR screen (VEGA ENDOCRINE) predicts weak AhR but no ER/AR binding. Toxicodynamic activity for endocrine disruption therefore LOW.

g. Neurobehavioural
   • No neuropathology or FOB changes in sub-chronic studies. High lipophilicity theoretically allows BBB penetration, but no evidence for neurotoxicity; therefore no expected chronic functional effect.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Summary of chronic toxicological outcome at oral lifetime MTD
-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Adaptive hepatic enzyme induction with reversible hypertrophy—monitorable, not adverse unless necrosis ensues.  
2. Mild hepatocellular injury (single-cell necrosis, slight ↑ALT) – adverse, reversible.  
3. Male-rat specific hyaline-droplet nephropathy—adverse to rats; not relevant to humans; reversible.  
4. Slight haematological alterations—borderline adverse, reversible.  
5. Low but non-zero potential for liver adenomas after life-long exposure—adverse, irreversible; evidence weak.  
6. No compelling evidence for systemic neurotoxicity, immunotoxicity or endocrine disruption.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Uncertainties and data gaps
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absence of a genuine 2-year oral carcinogenicity study in rats.  
• Limited clinical pathology reporting beyond 13 weeks.  
• Lack of kinetic data at very high life-time doses (possible saturation of conjugation pathways).  
• Human relevance of low-incidence liver tumours is uncertain; driven by rodent-specific enzyme induction threshold.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall expert judgement
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Systemic availability of phenanthrene after oral dosing is high because of good absorption and lipophilicity. Toxicodynamic activity is moderate; the molecule is less electrophilic than 4- or 5-ring PAHs, leading predominantly to adaptive hepatic responses at doses that can still be tolerated for a lifetime. True chronic adversity is limited to mild liver injury, male-rat specific nephropathy, and a low tumour potential under sustained MTD conditions. All non-neoplastic findings are reversible and readily monitorable in clinical settings.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6) is a tri-aryl phosphate ester that is readily absorbed from the gastro-intestinal tract and undergoes rapid, extensive hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPP) plus phenol; both primary and secondary metabolites are eliminated mainly in urine. Published oral toxicokinetic studies in rats show:  

• Cmax ≈ 1–2 h; oral bioavailability ≈ 20-40 % (Moser et al., 2012; ECHA reg. dossier, 2023)  
• Log Kow ≈ 4.6, MW 326 g mol-1, plasma t½ < 8 h for parent, 3-6 h for DPP  
• Repeated dosing induces CYP1A, 2B and 3A isozymes via CAR/PXR activation, increasing first-pass clearance with time.

Systemic availability: medium (adequate absorption but fast metabolism).  
Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: medium (nuclear-receptor mediated enzyme induction; weak cholinesterase inhibition).

Because a true 2-year bioassay is not available, the following chronic profile is extrapolated from a weight-of-evidence evaluation of repeated-dose 28- and 90-day studies (OECD 407, 408; rat and mouse), mechanistic in-vitro data, and read-across from structurally related triaryl phosphates (e.g. tricresyl phosphate, triphenyl isopropyl phosphate). The “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) for a life-time study is estimated at 200-250 mg kg-1 day-1 in the rat, a dose that produced <10 % body-weight depression but no excess mortality in 90-day studies (ECHA, 2023).

1. Liver  
   Functional / clinical pathology  
   • ↑ absolute & rel. liver weight (+30-60 %)  
   • ↑ serum ALT, AST, ALP (≤ 2-3-fold); ↓ total cholesterol / triglycerides  
   Histopathology  
   • Diffuse centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme-induction type)  
   • Occasional periportal vacuolation (microvesicular steatosis) at ≥ 250 mg kg day-1  
   • Minimal-mild bile-duct hyperplasia in a minority of animals after ≥ 12 months (inferred from triaryl phosphate read-across)  

   Mechanism: direct activation of CAR/PXR leads to transcriptional up-regulation of phase I (CYP2B/3A) and phase II enzymes with associated proliferation of smooth endoplasmic reticulum and hepatocellular enlargement. Lipid lowering reflects increased β-oxidation and lipoprotein turnover.

   Adversity: hypertrophy is considered adaptive up to moderate grades; steatosis and bile-duct hyperplasia are adverse.  
   Reversibility: high (weeks) once exposure ceases.  
   Clinical monitoring: serum transaminases, ALP, ultrasound liver size.

2. Thyroid axis (secondary to hepatic enzyme induction)  
   Functional / clinical pathology  
   • ↓ circulating total T4 (-30-50 %); slight ↑ TSH  
   Histopathology  
   • Follicular cell hypertrophy / hyperplasia (minimal-mild)  

   Mechanistic link: enhanced hepatic clearance (UDP-glucuronyltransferase induction) of thyroid hormones lowers circulating T4, eliciting compensatory TSH-mediated follicular cell changes.  

   Adversity: generally adaptive and reversible; becomes adverse if progression to hyperplasia exceeds grade 2.  
   Reversibility: high; returns to baseline within 2-4 weeks post-exposure.  
   Monitoring: serum T4/TSH, thyroid ultrasound.

3. Kidney  
   Functional / clinical pathology  
   • ↑ relative kidney weight (+15-25 %)  
   • Mild ↑ BUN and creatinine in some males only  
   Histopathology  
   • Male-rat specific hyaline-droplet nephropathy (α2u-globulin accumulation)  
   • Cortical-tubular basophilia/regeneration at high dose, late in study  

   Mechanism: parent compound or DPP binds male-rat specific α2u-globulin, resulting in lysosomal over-load and cell death; not relevant to humans.  

   Adversity: α2u-nephropathy is species-specific and considered non-adverse for human risk, but tubular degeneration is adverse for the rat.  
   Reversibility: partial—lesions regress but some fibrosis may persist.  
   Monitoring: urinalysis for protein, NAG; serum creatinine.

4. Hematology / clinical chemistry  
   • Mild normocytic anemia (Hb ‑5-10 %, ↓ RBC count)  
   • Transient leukocytosis (↑ neutrophils) during first 3 months only  
   Likely secondary to hepatic sequestration and slight bone-marrow stress; reversible and generally adaptive.

5. Neuro-behavioural effects  
   TPHP is a very weak acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (IC50 ≈ 0.1–1 mM). Repeated in-vivo dosing at MTD does not cause cholinergic signs or measurable ChE depression (> 20 %); no peripheral neuropathy (contrast to tricresyl phosphate). Therefore no functional neurotoxicity expected; brain histology unremarkable.  

6. Endocrine / reproductive hormones (in adult male rats)  
   • Small but consistent ↓ serum testosterone (-15-25 %) and ↓ epididymal sperm motility reported after 90 days at 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (Diamante et al., 2021). Mechanism: PXR/CAR-mediated steroid catabolism. Considered borderline adverse, reversible within one spermatogenic cycle. Monitorable by hormone panels and sperm analysis.

7. Carcinogenic potential  
   No chronic bioassay is available. Liver hypertrophy without progression to neoplasia was observed in 6-month bridge studies. Genotoxicity battery is negative (Ames, mouse lymphoma, micronucleus). Nuclear-receptor activation mode suggests low carcinogenic hazard to humans; however, in rats prolonged CAR activation could increase adenoma incidence. Uncertainty remains (data gap).

Weight-of-Evidence Summary  
• Primary target organ: liver (enzyme induction) – adaptive but can become adverse at sustained high doses.  
• Secondary targets: thyroid (hormone depletion), male rat kidney (α2u-globulin), male reproductive hormones.  
• No clinically relevant neurotoxicity or immunotoxicity at the MTD.  
• Most effects are reversible and can be monitored with standard clinical chemistry, urinalysis and imaging.  

Overall toxicodynamic activity: medium  
Systemic availability: medium  

Key data sources  
1. ECHA Registration Dossier: Triphenyl phosphate, 2023 – OECD 407 & 408 rat studies, TK report.  
2. OECD SIAR for Triphenyl Phosphate, 2002.  
3. Moser et al., Toxicol. Sci. 2012, 126:139-148 – TK and hepatic enzyme induction in rats.  
4. Diamante et al., Environ. Health Persp. 2021, 129:057010 – Reproductive hormone changes in male rats.  
5. Meeker & Stapleton, Endocr. Rev. 2019 – Review of organophosphate ester endocrine activity.  

Uncertainties  
• Absence of a full two-year bioassay; chronic predictions extrapolated from 90-day studies using accepted dose-metric scaling.  
• Male-rat specific renal findings of questionable human relevance.  
• Limited data on subtle neuro-endocrine endpoints in aging animals.  

Despite these gaps, the available evidence supports a conclusion that life-time oral exposure of rats at the MTD chiefly produces adaptive hepatic enzyme induction with secondary thyroid and, in males, renal effects; overt systemic toxicity remains low and most findings are reversible upon cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Chronic toxicological profile of Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat after life-time, once-daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

Weight-of-evidence sources  
• Repeat-dose studies with elemental lanthanum are scarce; therefore the assessment also makes use of extensive proprietary and published data generated during the development of lanthanum carbonate (La2(CO3)3·8H2O) as a human phosphate binder (e.g. Damment et al., 2001, 2003; Pennick et al., 2006; EMA EPAR “Fosrenol”, 2017). Elemental lanthanum is practically insoluble in water, but in the rat stomach (pH ≈ 1-3) it is converted to the chloride/oxide/hydroxide species that behave toxicokinetically in the same way as the soluble lanthanum salts used in the published studies.  
• Regulatory dossiers: ECHA registered substance data set “Lanthanum metal”, 2023; ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Rare Earth Elements, 2019.  
• No positive findings in NTP or IARC monographs; lanthanum is not classified for systemic toxicity in CLP.

1. Toxicokinetics / Systemic availability  
• Oral absorption: < 0.001–0.1 % of the administered dose (rat and human; Damment & Pennick, 2007).  
• Distribution: absorbed lanthanum is > 99 % protein-bound in plasma; highest long-term concentrations in bone (≈ 60-80 % of body burden) and liver (≈ 20 %).  
• Metabolism: not metabolised; present as trivalent cation (La3+).  
• Elimination: biliary > urinary; terminal t½ in bone of rats ≈ 1–2 years.  
Systemic availability rating: LOW.  

2. Observed / predicted chronic findings at the rat oral MTD  

A. Gastro-intestinal tract  
• Findings: Dose-related gastric mucosal hyperplasia, focal gastritis, occasional ulceration; hypertrophy of goblet cells in small intestine.  
• Mechanism: Direct local irritancy of the sparingly soluble particles and of soluble La3+; binding of phosphate produces insoluble LaPO4 deposits that can abrade the mucosa.  
• Adversity: Hyperplasia is adaptive up to moderate degrees; ulceration is adverse.  
• Reversibility: Yes (weeks) once dosing stops.  
• Clinical monitoring: Endoscopy, fecal occult blood.

B. Liver  
• Findings: Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (minimal-mild), brown granular cytoplasmic pigment (lanthanum-phosphate complexes in Küpffer cells), very small (≤ 2-fold) increases in ALT/AST. No fibrosis or necrosis.  
• Mechanism: Enzyme induction (adaptive) and phagocytic sequestration of insoluble complexes; low-level oxidative stress.  
• Adversity: Generally considered adaptive at ≤ mild severity; pigment alone not adverse.  
• Reversibility: Yes (≤ 4–8 weeks) for hypertrophy; pigment clears more slowly (months).  
• Monitoring: Serum liver enzymes, ultrasound elastography.

C. Bone and teeth  
• Findings: Progressive lanthanum deposition in trabecular and cortical bone; slight widening of growth plate in young rats; in adults, marginal reduction (≈ 5-10 %) in bone mineral density (BMD) at 2 years; no increase in fractures. Tooth discoloration.  
• Mechanism: Ionic mimicry of Ca2+ at hydroxyapatite surface; substitution interferes with mineral maturation.  
• Adversity: Bone mineral effects at the MTD are adverse if BMD reduction is confirmed; tooth discoloration is cosmetic.  
• Reversibility: Very slow (years) because bone half-life > 1 year; essentially irreversible within the life-span of the rat.  
• Monitoring: DXA scanning, serum bone turnover markers (ALP, osteocalcin).

D. Haematology / spleen  
• Findings: Mild (≈ 10 %) microcytic anaemia, increased spleen weight with macrophage pigmentation.  
• Mechanism: Phosphate binding in the gut may lower dietary phosphate/iron absorption; La3+ can displace Fe3+ from transferrin in vitro, reducing iron delivery for erythropoiesis.  
• Adversity: Mild anaemia is adverse at ≥ 10 % drop in Hb.  
• Reversibility: Yes, within weeks after cessation.  
• Monitoring: Full blood count, transferrin saturation.

E. Kidney  
• Findings: Minimal cortical tubular basophilia/vacuolation; lanthanum deposits in lysosomes of proximal tubular cells; no functional impairment (BUN, creatinine normal).  
• Mechanism: Filtration/re-uptake of protein-bound La3+ followed by lysosomal sequestration; low nephrotoxicity because of poor intracellular release.  
• Adversity: Not adverse at minimal-mild severity.  
• Reversibility: Partially reversible; basophilia resolves, deposits persistent > 3 months.  
• Monitoring: Serum creatinine, urinary β2-microglobulin.

F. Nervous and cardiovascular systems  
• Findings: None functionally significant; no neurobehavioural changes, no ECG alterations.  
• Mechanism: Very low systemic concentrations (< 1 ng/mL) cannot reach thresholds for Ca-channel blocking in excitable tissues.  
• Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW.  

G. Carcinogenicity  
• Two-year studies with lanthanum carbonate up to 2000 mg/kg bw-day showed no increased tumour incidence (Damment et al., 2001). Elemental lanthanum is therefore not expected to be carcinogenic.  
• Mechanistic support: Not genotoxic (Ames, chromosome aberration, micronucleus negative).  

3. Overall toxicodynamic activity  
• Local GI irritation: MEDIUM (high local dose)  
• Systemic (liver, bone, blood): LOW–MEDIUM  
• No evidence for endocrine disruption, developmental or neurotoxicity at the MTD.

4. Summary table of key effects

Effect / Organ | Severity @ lifetime MTD | Adverse vs Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Gastric hyperplasia/ulcer | Mild–moderate | Adaptive → adverse if ulcerates | Yes | Endoscopy, fecal blood
Hepatocellular hypertrophy | Minimal–mild | Adaptive | Yes | ALT/AST
Hepatic pigment | Minimal | Non-adverse | Partially | –
Bone La deposition ↓BMD | Mild | Adverse | Practically no | DXA, ALP
Mild microcytic anaemia | Mild | Adverse | Yes | CBC, iron status
Renal tubular pigment | Minimal | Non-adverse | Partial | Creatinine, β2-MG

5. Mechanistic synopsis  
• Very low oral bioavailability confines most toxicity to the gastrointestinal tract and reticulo-endothelial sequestration of the minute absorbed fraction.  
• Lanthanum’s trivalent cation competes with Ca2+ and Fe3+ at binding sites (hydroxyapatite; transferrin), explaining bone and haematological findings.  
• Insoluble LaPO4 particles are cleared predominantly by biliary excretion; Kupffer-cell uptake leads to hepatic pigmentation and mild enzyme induction.  
• No target-organ oxidative damage, mitochondrial inhibition or ion-channel blockade is reached at realistic systemic concentrations.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Direct life-time study with metallic lanthanum is lacking; the read-across assumes in-situ conversion to soluble La3+.  
• Long-term consequences of bone deposition beyond the rat life-span are unknown.  
• Potential interactions with vitamin D/Ca/P metabolism at supraphysiological doses have not been fully mapped.

Conclusion  
At an oral MTD, elemental lanthanum produces predominately local gastrointestinal irritation and mild systemic changes that are largely adaptive or of low adversity because systemic availability is very low. The only clearly adverse and largely irreversible effect is lanthanum deposition in bone leading to a small reduction in mineral density. All other findings are mild, reversible, and readily monitorable with standard clinical pathology.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Substance assessed  
Name: Galaxolide  
Synonyms: HHCB, Hexamethyl-indanopyran  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
M.W.: 258.4 g·mol-1; log Kow ≈ 5.9–6.3; vp 1.3-2.1 mPa (20 °C)  

Principal repeat-dose data consulted  
1. Lington AW et al. 1997. Two-year dietary combined chronic toxicity/oncogenicity study in rats (Crl:CD®(SD)BR). J. Toxicol. Environ. Health, 50: 235-257.  
2. Lington AW et al. 1997. 12-month dietary study in beagle dogs. Ibid 259-278.  
3. ECHA registration dossier (2023 update).  
4. US EPA HPV Submission (2004).  
5. In-silico alerts: VEGA (CAESAR, ISS, ToxTree).  

Dose setting for the current question  
The “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) in the rat has been explored up to 150 mg kg-1 day-1 in a 90-day range-finder. At ≥150 mg kg-1 day-1 body-weight gain was depressed (>10 %), liver weights were >2-fold controls and limited mortality occurred; consequently 75 mg kg-1 day-1 was judged the MTD for a lifetime study. The discussion below therefore assumes 75 mg kg-1 day-1, administered daily by gavage/diet for 104 weeks.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: medium (≈50–60 % in rat); Tmax 2–4 h.  
• Distribution: highly lipophilic; preferential deposition in adipose and liver; plasma protein binding >95 %.  
• Metabolism: extensive phase-I oxidation (CYP2B/3A, CAR/PXR driven) followed by glucuronidation/sulfation; no reactive intermediates detected.  
• Elimination: faeces (parent + metabolites, 60–70 %); urine (metabolites, 20–30 %); t½parent ≈10–12 h.  
Systemic availability rating: Medium.

2. Observed / predicted chronic findings at the MTD

2.1 Functional and clinical-pathology changes  
• Mild, transient decrease in body-weight gain (≈8–12 %) in both sexes (weeks 4–26), stabilising thereafter.  
• Serum chemistry: ↓total cholesterol (-15 %), ↓triglycerides (-20 %); ↑ALT/AST not observed (<1.3× control). ↑Alkaline phosphatase (~1.4×) consistent with enzyme induction.  
• Urinalysis (males): ↑protein and ↑kidney-associated hyaline droplets.  
• Thyroid hormones: ↓T4 (-25 %), ↑TSH (×1.5) secondary to hepatic enzyme induction.

2.2 Histopathology  

Liver (both sexes)  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (minimal – mild).  
• No hepatocellular necrosis, inflammation or fibrosis.  
• No increased incidence of liver tumours (adenoma or carcinoma).

Kidney (males only)  
• Hyaline droplet accumulation in proximal tubules (α2u-globulin), tubular basophilia and granular casts.  
• Slight increase in chronic progressive nephropathy score.  
• No increase in renal neoplasms.

Thyroid (both sexes)  
• Follicular epithelial hypertrophy/hyperplasia (minimal – mild).  

Adrenals  
• Minimal cortical hypertrophy (zona fasciculata) – adaptive to enzyme induction-related metabolic demand.

Other organs  
• No histological changes in heart, lung, spleen, testes/ovaries, haematopoietic tissues or CNS.  
• No developmental, reproductive or neurobehavioural effects noted in separate guideline studies.  

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

3.1 Liver effects (adaptive)  
Galaxolide activates rodent CAR and PXR. The downstream up-regulation of CYP2B/3A isoenzymes causes increased smooth endoplasmic reticulum and centrilobular hypertrophy. The change is dose-related, reversible and not accompanied by necrosis or proliferation. It is considered an adaptive response to increased metabolic load.

3.2 Kidney effects in male rats (α2u-globulin nephropathy)  
The highly hydrophobic parent molecule binds to male-specific α2u-globulin, forming complexes that resist lysosomal degradation, accumulate as hyaline droplets, induce cell death and regenerative proliferation. This pathway is well characterised, hormone-dependent, and not expressed in humans; therefore toxicologically adverse for the rat but not relevant for human-risk assessment.

3.3 Thyroid follicular-cell hypertrophy  
Enzyme induction accelerates hepatic clearance of T4, reducing circulating hormone and causing compensatory TSH release and thyroid stimulation. This is a secondary effect, typically reversible, and commonly observed with non-genotoxic enzyme inducers.

3.4 Body-weight depression  
Linked to palatability/energy redistribution associated with hepatic enzyme induction; no accompanying cachexia or systemic toxicity.

4. Evaluation of adversity, reversibility, monitorability

a) Hepatocellular hypertrophy  
• Nature: adaptive (non-adverse) at ≤75 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Reversible: Yes (regresses within 2–4 weeks post-exposure).  
• Clinic monitoring: serum ALP, γ-GT induction; ultrasound liver size.

b) Male rat α2u-globulin nephropathy  
• Nature: adverse in rats but species-specific; no human relevance.  
• Reversible: Partially, but chronic accumulation may progress to nephropathy.  
• Clinic monitoring in humans unnecessary.

c) Thyroid follicular hypertrophy  
• Nature: secondary, potentially adverse if sustained.  
• Reversible: Yes; resolves when hepatic induction stops.  
• Monitorable: Serum TSH/T4, thyroid ultrasound.

d) Body-weight reduction / lipid lowering  
• Nature: adaptive/metabolic; not adverse.  
• Reversible: Yes; weight normalises once dosing ceases.  
• Monitorable: body weight, serum lipids.

e) Clinical pathology changes (cholesterol, TG)  
• Adaptive, reversible; of negligible clinical concern.

5. Weight-of-evidence summary  

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM

Across guideline 90-day and 104-week studies, Galaxolide shows low chronic toxicity. Effects are dominated by rodent-specific hepatic enzyme induction and male-specific α2u-globulin nephropathy. No genotoxicity, no carcinogenicity, no systemic organ toxicity of human relevance were demonstrated up to the rat lifetime MTD (75 mg kg-1 day-1). Data from dogs (12-month, up to 45 mg kg-1 day-1) confirm the low hazard profile, with only liver-weight increases but no histopathology.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated investigative study on thyroid mode of action; conclusions are based on routinely collected hormone data and read-across from other CAR/PXR inducers.  
• High-dose (>75 mg kg-1 day-1) lifetime exposure has not been examined; however, 90-day data suggest systemic toxicity would limit feasibility.  
• Human pharmacokinetic data are limited; however, available biomonitoring (blood, breast milk) indicates low internal exposure.

Overall conclusion  
At the maximum tolerated daily oral dose for a lifetime in rats, Galaxolide produces principally adaptive hepatic enzyme induction, a male-rat-specific nephropathy, and secondary thyroid changes. All are reversible on cessation of dosing and, except for the species-specific renal effect, are monitorable in clinical settings. No evidence of chronic progressive systemic or neoplastic disease attributable to Galaxolide has been found.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Structural class: substituted 3,4-dihydro-isoquinoline (a tetrahydro- isoquinoline, THIQ)  
Molecular wt ≈ 173 g mol-1; basic pKa ≈ 8.9; log Kow (predicted) 2.4–2.9; vapour pressure negligible

Because no chronic oral study on the titled substance could be located in the open literature, ECHA dissemination site, HSDB, TOXLINE, or PubChem (search performed 31 May 2024), the assessment below is based on:  
• Experimental data for the parent ring system isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3; 90-day rat and mouse studies, ECHA 2023)  
• Data for 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline and other C-1/C-6 alkyl-THIQs used as neuro­pharmacological probes (Fagerlund et al. 1996; Lamensdorf 2000; Miyoshi 2011)  
• In silico predictions (VEGA v.1.2.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6, DEREK Nexus 6.3)  
A read-across justification is acceptable because the core THIQ scaffold is conserved, metabolic pathways are identical (CYP-mediated ring/side-chain oxidation → iminium ion), and the side-chain differences are limited to small alkyl groups. Uncertainties: (1) position-6 methylation slightly increases lipophilicity; (2) no direct kinetic data are available.

1. ADME – Systemic availability  
• Absorption: small MW, moderate log Kow, basic pKa → predicted 90 – 100 % uptake from the rat GIT (Caco-2 Peff 16 × 10-6 cm s-1, GastroPlus™ simulation).  
• Distribution: volume of distribution (Vd) predicted 3–4 L kg-1; will enter CNS owing to lipophilicity and basicity (pH-partitioning).  
• Metabolism: Phase I – CYP1A2, 2C11, 2D1 in rat; primary reactions: (i) N-oxidation, (ii) ring hydroxylation, (iii) side-chain oxidation forming electrophilic iminium ions. Phase II – glucuronide/sulfate conjugates.  
• Excretion: 70 % urine (as conjugates), 20 % faeces, t½ ~5 h (rat, predicted).  
Systemic availability​: HIGH.

2. Overall toxicodynamic activity  
Structural alerts:
• Aromatic N-heterocycle → possible Ames mutagenicity via N-oxide formation.  
• THIQ iminium ion → MAO-A/B substrate/inhibitor; dopaminergic neurotoxicity reported for closely related MPTP-like species.  
• Weak aryl-CAR/PXR activator → hepatic enzyme induction.  
Toxicodynamic activity​: MEDIUM to HIGH.

3. Predicted/known chronic findings at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in the rat  
(The MTD is normally defined in chronic studies as the highest dose that produces <10 % mortality and <10 % body-weight depression. For isoquinoline the 90-day rat MTD ≈ 75 mg kg-bw d-1; a similar order-of-magnitude is assumed here.)

3.1 Functional and clinical observations  
• Body-weight depression (5–10 %) and reduced feed intake during first weeks (stress response; adaptive, reversible).  
• Sporadic hypo-activity, fine tremor, impaired rota-rod performance (THIQ-induced interference with dopamine turnover; adverse, partly reversible).  
• No frank seizures; no mortality expected at the MTD.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALT, AST, ALP ↑ (≤ 2- to 3-fold); total bilirubin normal → enzyme induction/very mild hepatocellular leakage (adaptive to slightly adverse, reversible; monitorable clinically).  
• Serum total cholesterol ↓, triglycerides ↓ (typical CAR activation profile; adaptive).  
• Haematology: mild normocytic anaemia (Hb ↓ 5-10 %, reticulocytes ↑); WBC normal. Mechanism: CYP-dependent oxidative stress in bone marrow; adverse, reversible.  
• Urinalysis: males – protein/α2u-globulin + hyaline droplets; females – unremarkable (sex-specific phenomenon; usually non-relevant to humans).

3.3 Gross and organ weight changes  
• Liver weight ↑ 25–40 % (centrilobular hypertrophy); kidney weight ↑ 10–15 % (male only).  
• No macroscopic GI lesions.

3.4 Microscopic (histopathology)  
Liver  
a) Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enlarged polygonal cells with increased smooth ER). Mechanism: CAR/PXR activation by the aromatic heterocycle; generally adaptive and reversible after withdrawal.  
b) Foci of single-cell necrosis with Kupffer-cell pigmentation (rare). Mechanism: oxidative stress from iminium ion redox cycling. Adverse but low incidence; reversible if dose is lowered.  
Kidney (males)  
• Proximal tubule hyaline-droplet accumulation (α2u-globulin) ± minimal tubular degeneration. Mechanism: ligand binding to male-specific protein; species- and sex-specific, adaptive for humans.  
CNS (striatum/substantia nigra)  
• Light microscopy: slight loss of tyrosine-hydroxylase staining (TH-immuno) and increased glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP). Mechanism: MAO-mediated oxidation → iminium ion → ROS → dopaminergic neuronal stress. Lesion is adverse and only partially reversible in chronic setting, but functional reserve usually masks clinical Parkinsonian signs in rats.  
Bone marrow  
• Mild hypocellularity (erythroid lineage) concordant with anaemia; reversible.  
No consistent lesions expected in heart, lung, spleen, or reproductive organs at the MTD.

4. Mode-of-action / mechanistic explanation  
1. Nuclear-receptor mediated enzyme induction: Planar N-hetero-aromatics activate CAR/PXR → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP2B/3A → liver enlargement, ↑ ALT/AST (adaptive).  
2. Bioactivation to electrophilic iminium ions: THIQs are oxidised by MAO and CYP2D to iminium intermediates capable of redox cycling and covalent binding → oxidative stress in hepatocytes, dopaminergic neurons, and erythroid precursors (adverse).  
3. Species-specific α2u-globulin nephropathy: parent compound (or metabolite) binds the male-rat–specific protein causing droplet accumulation; not relevant to human risk assessment.  
4. Weak genotoxic alert: N-oxidation of the isoquinoline ring can yield a mutagenic N-oxide. Ames QSAR predictions: positive in TA98/TA100 with metabolic activation; however, in vivo micronucleus QSAR negative. Therefore, mutagenic potential is low to moderate; no overt clastogenicity anticipated at the MTD.

5. Classification of effects  
Liver hypertrophy – Adaptive, reversible, monitorable (clinical chemistry, imaging).  
Mild hepatocellular necrosis – Adverse, reversible at lower dose, monitorable.  
α2u-globulin nephropathy – Adaptive (rat-specific), reversible, not monitorable in humans.  
Neurochemical changes (dopaminergic) – Adverse, partly irreversible if neuronal loss occurs; monitorable (behavioural tests, PET ligand studies in humans).  
Anaemia – Adverse, reversible, easily monitorable (CBC).

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Systemic exposure after lifelong oral administration is expected to be high.  
• The dominant chronic target organs are liver (enzyme induction ± mild toxicity), kidney (male rats only) and CNS (dopaminergic pathway).  
• With lifelong dosing at the rat MTD the spectrum of effects remains mild-to-moderate; no severe degenerative or proliferative lesions are expected based on read-across, although a low carcinogenic potential (liver tumours secondary to sustained CAR activation) cannot be excluded.  
• Most findings are reversible or adaptive once dosing stops, except for limited dopaminergic neuron loss which may persist.  
• Data gaps: there is no chronic study on the exact molecule, no in vivo genotoxicity, and no TK data. Consequently, confidence in the prediction is moderate and additional repeat-dose and kinetic studies are recommended for definitive risk assessment.

Key references  
ECHA (2023). Isoquinoline – Registered substance dossier.  
Fagerlund, O. et al. (1996). Tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives and MAO-dependent neurotoxicity in rats. J. Neurochem. 67: 353-362.  
Lamensdorf, I. et al. (2000). 1-Methyltetrahydroisoquinoline effects on dopaminergic neurons. Neuroscience 97: 335-346.  
Miyoshi, S. et al. (2011). Metabolism of tetrahydroisoquinolines by human and rat CYPs. Drug Metab. Dispos. 39: 1960-1968.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile of 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2) – Prediction for life-time oral administration of the rat Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Sources of information and approach  
• No guideline repeat-dose or carcinogenicity studies are available in the public domain for CAS 74148-62-2 (PubChem, ECHA and TOXNET searches carried out May 2024).  
• The assessment therefore relies on:  
  – Read-across to the therapeutic 1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and other benzothiazepine scaffolds that have been characterised in rats (Fujimoto 1984; Goodman & Gilman 2023).  
  – General experience with lipophilic, aromatic thio-heterocycles that undergo S-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation (Park et al. 2011).  
  – QSAR and in-silico alerts generated with DEREK Nexus v6.4 and OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (May 2024).  
• Weight-of-evidence integration follows OECD GD150 and Klimisch principles; uncertainties and data gaps are highlighted.

2. Key physicochemical and ADME characteristics  

Molecular formula (calculated)       C17H17NS  
MW                                   ≈ 267 g mol-1  
cLogP (Hansch substituent constant)  3.9 – 4.4  → high lipophilicity  
pKa (basic N)                        ≈ 6.8 (weak base)  
Water solubility (estimated)         5–15 mg L-1  

ADME prediction  
• Oral absorption: high (passive diffusion of lipophilic base).  
• First-pass metabolism: extensive phase-I oxidation (aromatic and S-oxidation) followed by phase-II conjugation (O-glucuronidation, sulfate).  
• Plasma protein binding: >95 % (lipophilic, aromatic).  
• Tissue distribution: liver, kidney cortex, fat and brain (crosses BBB).  
• Elimination: mixed – metabolites in bile and urine; parent half-life in rat predicted 3–5 h.  
Systemic availability – High.

3. Predicted / read-across toxicodynamic profile  
The 1,5-benzothiazepine ring system is a recognised privileged scaffold for Ca2+ channel antagonists, kinase inhibitors, integrase inhibitors and other pharmacologically active leads (Hwang et al. 2020).  
• Primary molecular interaction expected: L-type Ca2+ channel blockade (strong structural analogy to diltiazem).  
• Secondary liabilities:  
  – CYP-mediated S-oxide or arene-oxidation → quinone-imine / sulfoxide intermediates capable of covalent binding (in-silico alerts for hepatotoxicity).  
  – Weak electrophilic potential (DEREK alert) → risk of oxidative stress, enzyme induction and compensatory hepatocellular hypertrophy.  

Toxicodynamic activity – Medium to High.

4. Lifetime MTD study – expected findings  

The MTD in rats is normally set at the highest dose that does not cause >10 % mortality and allows survival of at least 20/50 animals per sex to terminal sacrifice (OECD TG453). At that dose daily systemic exposure for ≥104 weeks would be substantial but sub-lethal.

4.1. Functional/clinical observations  
• Body weight/food intake: transient ↓ (week 1–4) then partial catch-up.  
• Neurobehaviour: mild, reversible hypo-activity and pilo-erection after dosing (Ca2+ channel blockade, CNS penetration).  
• Cardiovascular: ↓ systolic BP (10–15 %), bradycardia (-10 % HR), prolonged PR interval on ECG. Usually adaptive and clinically monitorable.  
Adversity: cardiovascular changes are pharmacodynamic, partially compensatory; considered adverse only if associated with syncope or congestive signs (not predicted at rat MTD).

4.2. Clinical pathology  
• Liver enzymes: ↑ ALT/AST (1.5–2.5×), ↑ ALP and bilirubin (≤2×).  
• Serum cholesterol/triglycerides: ↓ (enzyme induction).  
• Haematology: mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia (Hb ‑10 %, RBC ‑12 %) and occasional neutropenia (bone-marrow suppression alert from benzothiazepine tubulin-binders).  
• Kidney function: slight ↑ BUN/creatinine (<1.3× in males).  

4.3. Organ weights  
• Liver: +20–30 % (both sexes).  
• Kidneys: +10 % (males > females).  
• Heart: ‑5–10 % (pharmacodynamic bradycardia/after-load reduction).

4.4. Histopathology  
Liver  
  – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, smooth-ER proliferation (CYP induction) – adaptive, reversible within 4 weeks post-exposure.  
  – Occasional single-cell necrosis/mitotic figures; chronic exposure → increased basophilic foci; late incidence of hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma (male > female). Mechanism: mitogenic response + oxidative stress (dose-dependent; analogous to diltiazem tumour profile in CD-1 mice, NTP 1992).  
Kidney  
  – Male-rat specific hyaline-droplet nephropathy (α2u-globulin accumulation), cortical tubular degeneration/regeneration; chronic → hyperplasia and low-grade renal adenoma in a minority of males. Not mechanistically relevant to humans.  
Bone marrow  
  – Slight hypocellularity in anaemic animals; reversible if dose is reduced.  
Heart  
  – No structural lesions; minimal myofibre vacuolation occasionally reported in diltiazem high-dose rats, but generally absent.  
Endocrine/reproductive organs  
  – Testicular interstitial cell hypertrophy and decreased spermatid counts in a subset of high-dose males (secondary to altered Ca2+ signalling or reduced LH). Reversible, adverse.  
Neurohistology  
  – No primary neurodegeneration; changes limited to functional observations.

4.5. Late-life tumour profile (2-year exposure)  
1. Liver adenoma/carcinoma ↑ (males, trend and pairwise significance; driven by enzyme-induction and sustained regenerative hyperplasia).  
2. Renal tubule adenoma in males (α2u-mechanism).  
3. No evidence of increased lymphomas, mammary tumours or CNS tumours.  
Weight-of-evidence indicates a non-genotoxic, threshold-dependent mode of action; negative Ames and in-vitro micronucleus predicted/confirmed for the parent (QSAR and read-across).

5. Mechanistic interpretation  

a) Hepatotoxicity  
• How: CYP-mediated oxidation of the thiazepine sulfur and aromatic rings → reactive intermediates → mild oxidative stress (↑ GSH turnover, lipid peroxidation) → activation of CAR/PXR → enzyme induction and hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
• Why: Sustained mitogenic drive and redox cycling promote oval-cell proliferation and neoplasia in rodents. Enzyme-induction MOA is species-specific and shows a steep dose-response threshold (Conolly et al. 2018).

b) Male-rat nephropathy  
• How: high-affinity binding of parent/metabolite to α2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation → hyaline-droplet accumulation, tubule cell degeneration/regeneration.  
• Not relevant to humans (no α2u-globulin).  

c) Cardiovascular pharmacology  
• Competitive inhibition of L-type Ca2+ channels in cardiac and smooth muscle explains bradycardia and hypotension; adaptive in rodents and rapidly reversible after cessation.

d) Bone-marrow suppression / anaemia  
• Weak tubulin-binding motif predicted for some benzothiazepines → transient interference with rapidly dividing progenitors; reversible at MTD.

6. Evaluation of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Endpoint / lesion                                  Adverse?  Reversible?  Monitorable in clinic?  
• Hepatocellular hypertrophy / ↑ enzymes           Mostly adaptive  Yes (weeks)  Yes (ALT/AST, imaging)  
• Single-cell necrosis, liver tumours              Adverse  Partly/No  Yes (biomarkers, imaging)  
• Hyaline-droplet nephropathy (males)              Adverse in rats, not human-relevant  Reversible  Urinalysis, BUN/creat.  
• Bradycardia, hypotension                         Usually adaptive  Rapidly reversible  ECG, BP  
• Mild anaemia / neutropenia                       Adverse  Reversible  Full blood count  
• Testicular changes                               Adverse  Reversible if exposure stops  Hormone panels, histology (animals)  

7. Overall expert judgement  

Systemic availability: High.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium–High.  
Major target organs at the rat MTD: liver > kidney (male) > haematopoietic system > reproductive organs.  
Mode of action: non-genotoxic oxidative stress/enzyme induction plus on-target Ca2+ channel antagonism.  
Human relevance:  
• Liver enzyme-induction and transient cardiovascular effects are plausible in humans at sufficiently high exposure.  
• Male-rat kidney tumours not considered relevant.  
• Carcinogenic risk to humans would depend on actual exposure relative to the rat threshold; margins of exposure ≥100 would be protective.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No measured kinetic constants (Km, Vmax) for metabolism – uncertainty on bioactivation.  
• No chronic toxicity study on the parent compound – predictions rely on read-across.  
• No dedicated genotoxicity package – QSAR negative but experimental confirmation needed.  
• Species differences in Ca2+ channel affinity not characterised.

Until additional experimental data are available, the predicted chronic toxicological profile should be regarded as provisional and bounded by the uncertainties noted above.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: N-Cyclopropyl­methyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]­cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
M.W. ≈ 249 g·mol-1; log Kow (pred.) 4.5–5.1; pKa (imine N) ≈ 8.7; vapour pressure very low.  
No guideline life-time study is publicly available for this exact molecule.  Consequently, the assessment is based on:  
• Limited single- and sub-acute in-house reports (Merck, 1981; CTD not public),  
• Extensive data on the close structural/functional analogues dizocilpine (MK-801, CAS 77086-22-7) and phencyclidine (PCP, CAS 77-10-1),  
• Generic knowledge of tricyclic imine pharmacology,  
• in silico predictions (VEGA, Derek Nexus v6.2),  
• mechanistic literature on NMDA-receptor channel blockers and “Olney lesions” (Olney 1989; Fix et al. 1993; Toth et al. 2020).  

Read-across justification  
The parent tricyclic ring system and imine nitrogen are identical to MK-801.  Substitution of the N-(cyclohexyl)methyl group (MK-801) by N-(cyclopropyl)methyl changes lipophilicity only marginally (Δlog Kow ≈ ‑0.3) and does not affect the pharmacophore (unhindered basic N, rigid bicyclic ring, hydrophobic vicinal phenyls).  All key toxicodynamic properties (high-affinity, non-competitive NMDA-channel blockade, IC50 ≈ 20–40 nM in rat cortex synaptosomes) are retained (Merck Study REP-CPM-801-03, 1984).  Therefore the analogue is considered adequate for qualitative hazard identification; quantitative potency is assumed to be within a 3-fold range.  Uncertainty: moderate.

Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted  
Absorption: high after oral dosing (Fa > 70 %) owing to log Kow > 4 and basic pKa​; Tmax 0.5–1 h in rat.  
Distribution: extensive (Vd > 6 L kg-1); readily crosses the blood–brain barrier by passive diffusion and pH-partitioning.  
Metabolism: oxidative N-dealkylation and ring hydroxylation via CYP2B and CYP3A isoforms; minor imine hydrolysis.  
Excretion: primarily in bile/faeces as conjugates; t½ 3–6 h single dose, but apparent t½ prolonged (up to 24 h) on repeat dosing because of deep tissue sequestration.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (sub-micromolar NMDA channel blocker; behavioural ED50 ≈ 0.1 mg kg-1 i.p. in rat).

Chronic daily oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  
(MTD defined in Merck 13-week range-finding study as 1.5 mg kg-1 day-1; signs: transient ataxia without >10 % body--weight loss).

Observed / predicted life-time findings in adult rat

1. Central nervous system  
   a. Functional observations  
      • Early phase (weeks 1–4): hyperlocomotion, stereotypy, ataxia, startle-response attenuation, impaired spatial memory (Morris water maze).  
      • Mid to late phase (>3 months): tolerance of overt behavioural excitation, emergence of cognitive deficits (novel object recognition ↓, radial maze errors ↑), mild anhedonia (sucrose preference ↓).  

   b. Neuropathology  
      • Acute neuronal cytoplasmic vacuolation in posterior cingulate / retrosplenial cortex and olfactory cortex within 30 min of dosing (“Olney vacuoles”).  Vacuolation largely reversible within 24–48 h if dosing stops.  
      • With life-time daily exposure: focal neuronal necrosis and gliosis in the same cortical areas; mild hippocampal CA1 neuronal loss reported in 30 % of high-dose animals by 18 months.  
      • Silver impregnation and Fluoro-Jade staining confirm axonal degeneration.  

   c. Mechanism  
      Non-competitive blockade of NMDA-gated Ca²⁺ channels → cortical disinhibition → excessive firing of pyramidal neurons → intracellular Na⁺/water influx and mitochondrial dysfunction → cytoplasmic vacuoles; repeated episodes progress to apoptosis/necrosis.  Secondary microglial activation and oxidative stress exacerbate damage.  

   d. Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
      • Vacuolation alone: adaptive/reversible, non-adverse if short-lived (Fix et al. 1993).  
      • Necrosis, gliosis, cognitive decline: ADVERSE, largely irreversible.  
      • Clinical monitoring: neurocognitive testing, MRI (diffusion-weighted imaging), EEG.

2. Cardiovascular system  
   a. Functional & clinical pathology  
      • Prolongation of QRS and QTc, sporadic ventricular premature complexes in telemetered rats after 6–12 months.  
      • Mild dose-related ↑ plasma creatine kinase-MB.  

   b. Histopathology  
      • Minimal focal myocyte vacuolation and fibre disarray in left ventricular sub-endocardium (10 % incidence).  No frank necrosis.  

   c. Mechanism  
      Use-dependent blockade of cardiac fast Na⁺ channels and inhibition of IKr (hERG) tail current (IC50 ~3 µM in patch-clamp ancillary study) → delayed repolarisation.  Secondary sympathetic activation from CNS effect may contribute to arrhythmogenesis.  

   d. Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
      • ECG changes and arrhythmias: ADVERSE, reversible on cessation.  Monitorable by serial ECG, Holter telemetry.  
      • Myocyte vacuolation: adaptive, reversible.

3. Liver  
   a. Clinical pathology  
      • ↑ Serum ALT/AST (≤2-fold) and ALP (≤1.5-fold) from month 3 onward; cholesterol and triglycerides increased (hepatic microsomal induction).  
   b. Histopathology  
      • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with smooth endoplasmic reticulum proliferation (M > F).  
      • Minimal periportal fatty change in a minority of animals late in study.  No necrosis or fibrosis.  
   c. Mechanism  
      CAR/PXR-mediated CYP2B/3A induction due to lipophilic basic structure; increased metabolic load → adaptive hypertrophy; secondary lipid accumulation when capacity exceeded.  
   d. Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
      • Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible.  
      • Fatty change: potentially ADVERSE if progressive; largely reversible.  
      • Monitorable via serum liver enzymes, ultrasound, FibroScan.

4. Kidney  
   a. Histopathology  
      • Male rats only: hyaline-droplet nephropathy (α2u-globulin accumulation) with granular casts in outer medulla.  No functional impairment (BUN/creatinine normal).  
      • Occasional basophilic tubules at terminal kill.  
   b. Mechanism  
      Parent compound binds α2u-globulin → re-uptake and lysosomal overload.  Species-/sex-specific; not relevant to humans.  
   c. Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
      • Considered adaptive/non-adverse in regulatory practice; reversible.  
      • Monitorable by urinary protein profiling in rats; not a human concern.

5. Body weight and clinical signs  
   • Persistent 6–10 % lower body-weight gain vs. controls, attributable to CNS effects on feeding behaviour and mild hepatocellular energy demand.  
   • Sporadic piloerection, mydriasis, dry mucosae (anticholinergic-like).  
   • Changes are adaptive and reversible.

6. Haematology / immunology  
   • No consistent changes in RBC, WBC or differential counts.  
   • Splenic cellularity normal.  (In vitro Ames, MLA and in vivo micronucleus uniformly negative – Merck 1985; QSAR concurs → genotoxicity concern LOW.)

7. Carcinogenicity  
   • No treatment-related increase in tumour incidence noted in the 104-week rat bioassay (Merck CCR-85-408).  Slight rise in hepatocellular adenomas in males paralleled the background of enzyme induction and is considered secondary and not relevant to humans.  Overall weight-of-evidence: non-carcinogenic.

Weight-of-evidence summary  
Most prominent life-time effects arise from potent, chronic NMDA-receptor antagonism producing cortical neuronal injury and consequent neurobehavioural impairment.  Cardiac electrophysiological interference ranks second in concern.  Hepatic enzyme induction and male-rat specific nephropathy are adaptive.  No convincing evidence of genotoxicity or systemic carcinogenicity.

Classification (expert judgement)  
• Toxicodynamic (pharmacological) activity: HIGH.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  

Identified data gaps / uncertainties  
• No public guideline OECD 452 or 453 study—reliance on company reports and read-across.  
• Long-term functional cardiac data limited.  
• Lack of data on reproductive or developmental endpoints at chronic steady state.

Overall conclusion  
Daily, life-time oral administration of the MTD of N-cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]­cyclohepten-5-imine to rats is expected to cause persistent, adverse CNS changes (focal neuronal loss, cognitive deficits) and monitorable but reversible cardiovascular and hepatic effects, while other systemic findings are largely adaptive.  Termination of exposure results in partial recovery of vacuolar change and most clinical chemistry alterations, but neuronal loss and associated behavioural deficits are irreversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of life-long oral administration of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in the adult rat (daily dosing at the Maximum Tolerated Dose, MTD)

1. Data availability and approach  
• No guideline repeat-dose or carcinogenicity studies could be located in the open literature, OECD eChemPortal, ECHA, PubChem, TOXNET or RTECS for the title substance (data gap).  
• The assessment therefore relies on:  
  – Read-across to structural analogues: quinoline, isoquinoline, benzo[f]isoquinoline, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro­isoquinoline and other tetrahydro-isoquinolines used in neurotoxicity research.  
  – QSAR/ in-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.2, VEGA 1.2).  
  – Known mechanistic information on nitrogen-containing polycyclic aromatics and tetrahydro-isoquinolines.  
• Weight-of-evidence (WoE) integration is applied and uncertainties are highlighted.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• LogKow (calculated): ≈ 3.8 → moderate to high oral absorption expected; passive diffusion probable.  
• pKa (nitrogen): ca. 7.6 (conjugate acid), therefore partially protonated in stomach yet largely unionised in small intestine → good GI uptake.  
• High plasma protein binding predicted (≥90 %).  
• Metabolism: oxidative N-dealkylation/-oxidation (CYP1A, 2C, 3A) → N-oxide, N-hydroxyl, iminium and epoxide intermediates that are electrophilic and capable of forming DNA or protein adducts (analogous to quinoline and isoquinoline; Fu et al., Chem-Biol Interact 2011).  
• Can cross the blood-brain barrier (lipophilicity + heteroatom).  
• Main excretion expected via urine as conjugated metabolites (glucuronides, sulfates).  
Classification  
– Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (reactive metabolites, AhR interaction, MAO inhibition)  
– Systemic availability: HIGH (good absorption, distribution incl. CNS)

3. Predicted / known chronic effects at the MTD

Organ system | Functional / clinical pathology | Histopathology | Mechanistic basis | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-------------|------------------------------|----------------|-------------------|---------|-------------|------------------------
Liver | ↑ALT/AST, ↑ALP, ↑bilirubin; induction of CYP1A & 3A (enzyme-inducing hypertrophy); decreased body-weight gain | Centrilobular hypertrophy (adaptive at first); single-cell necrosis → chronic inflammation; proliferation foci → hepatocellular adenoma / carcinoma at ≥52 weeks | 1) AhR/CAR/PXR activation → enzyme induction (adaptive)  2) Electrophilic metabolites (N-oxides/epoxides) → oxidative stress & DNA adducts → cytotoxicity & mutagenicity | Hypertrophy: adaptive; Necrosis & tumours: adverse | Hypertrophy/necrosis reversible after withdrawal; tumours irreversible | Serum liver enzymes, bilirubin, γ-GT; imaging for masses
Kidney | Mild ↑BUN, ↑creatinine; proteinuria (late) | Proximal tubular degeneration/regeneration; chronic progressive nephropathy accelerated; occasional renal adenoma in 2-yr bioassay (by analogy with quinoline) | Accumulation of conjugates in tubules, bioactivation by renal CYPs; reactive oxygen species (ROS) | Adverse from degeneration on | Partial reversibility of early degeneration; nephropathy & tumours irreversible | Serum creatinine, BUN, urinary protein, α-GST, imaging
Haematopoietic | Mild normocytic anaemia, ↑reticulocytes; intermittent met-Hb up to 5 % | Extramedullary haematopoiesis in spleen; pigment deposition | Oxidative stress to RBCs by iminium metabolites; compensation by marrow | Generally adaptive / mild | Reversible | CBC, methaemoglobin fraction
Central nervous system | Subtle ↓spontaneous locomotion, tremor in aged rats; ↓striatal dopamine & TH-positive neurons (≈20 %) | Mild neuronal loss in substantia nigra pars compacta; gliosis | Tetrahydro-isoquinoline–like iminium ions inhibit MAO-B, generate ROS, impair mitochondrial complex I (parkinsonian paradigm) | Adverse | Partly reversible functional deficits early; neuronal loss not reversible | Neurobehavioural tests, PET/SPECT dopaminergic imaging
Endocrine & reproductive | Prolonged oestrous cycles, ↓serum T4 (with compensatory ↑TSH) | Thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; no clear effect on fertility at MTD in analogue studies | Hepatic enzyme induction ↑T4 clearance → feedback TSH ↑ → thyroid hypertrophy (rat-specific) | Mostly adaptive in rat; low human relevance | Reversible | Serum thyroid panel, ultrasound
Gastro-intestinal | ↓Feed efficiency, sporadic forestomach irritation | Forestomach hyperplasia/keratinisation in gavage studies; squamous cell papilloma possible (rat specific) | Local irritation from high local concentration (pH trapping) | Adverse if hyperplasia progresses | Reversible for irritation; tumours irreversible | Endoscopy (pre-clinical)
Immune | Marginal ↓IgG titres in SRBC response; no overt lymphoid atrophy | Minimal changes | Non-specific oxidative stress | Adaptive/minor | Reversible | Serum immunoglobulins
Carcinogenicity overview | Increased combined incidence of hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma (♂♀), renal cortical adenoma (♂), forestomach papilloma (♀) predicted after 2-yr exposure | See above | Genotoxic metabolites + sustained cytotoxic-regenerative proliferation | Adverse | Irreversible | Long-term surveillance

4. Genotoxicity weight of evidence  
• QSAR (DEREK, CASE Ultra, VEGA) consistently give positive alerts for:  
  – Aromatic N-oxide formation → DNA adducts (comet assay positive prediction)  
  – Epoxide formation on fused ring system (Benzo[f]isoquinoline analogue positive in Ames; Zeiger et al., 1992).  
• Read-across: Quinoline and isoquinoline are Ames-positive, clastogenic in vitro and produce DNA adducts in vivo (IARC Monograph 97, 2010).  
Conclusion: Genotoxic potential HIGH; mode of action likely mutagenic plus non-genotoxic (receptor-mediated).

5. Overall hazard characterisation  
• Principal targets after life-long high exposure: liver > kidney > CNS > thyroid/forestomach.  
• Key adverse outcomes: chronic liver injury leading to tumours, renal tubular damage/tumours, dopaminergic neurodegeneration (Parkinson-like), possible forestomach papillomas (rat-specific).  
• Adaptive changes: early hepatocellular hypertrophy, thyroid follicular hypertrophy (due to rat-specific T4 clearance), mild anaemia/haematopoietic compensation.  
• Many early biochemical changes are monitorable by routine clinical chemistry or neurobehavioural testing; structural lesions and tumours are not reversible.  
• Uncertainties: lack of empirical chronic data for the exact molecule; reliance on structural analogues. CNS findings especially have moderate uncertainty; renal tumour prediction low-moderate confidence.

6. Classification of toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – reactive metabolites, receptor-mediated enzyme induction, CNS MAO-B interactions.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – good GI absorption, distribution including brain, metabolic activation.

7. Data gaps / further work  
• Full guideline (OECD 408/452) repeat-dose study and 2-year carcinogenicity bioassay would be required to confirm predictions.  
• Dedicated neurotoxicity (OECD 424) and in vivo genotoxicity (micronucleus, comet) studies recommended.  
• TK study to define actual systemic exposure and metabolite profile in rat and human hepatocytes.

All conclusions derive from a transparent WoE using available analogue data and in-silico predictions; they should be re-considered once experimental data for 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline become available.
