vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
DEVELOPMENTAL / TERATOGENIC PROFILE  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene)  
CAS‐No.: 75-01-4  

Key data sources consulted  
• ECHA Registration Dossier (2023 update)  
• U.S. EPA IRIS file for Vinyl Chloride (2000; update in progress)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile (2006)  
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012)  
• NTP Technical Reports 23, 259 and 307  
• PubChem CID 6322  
• Key peer-reviewed developmental studies: Thornton et al., 2002; John et al., 1977; Munafo et al., 1976; Ungvary et al., 1978  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) after repeated oral dosing during gestation  
• Absorption: VC is a small, lipophilic gas (log Kow ≈ 1.4; MW 62.5 g mol-1). When dissolved in an aqueous vehicle it is rapidly absorbed across the GI tract; oral bioavailability in rodents is ≈ 60–80 % in gavage studies (John et al., 1977).  
• Distribution: Readily crosses the placenta (ATSDR, 2006). VC itself and its two primary oxidative metabolites – chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA) – are detectable in fetal blood.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly CYP2E1‐mediated epoxidation to CEO; CEO can spontaneously rearrange to CAA. Both species form DNA and protein adducts (e.g. N⁷-(2-oxoethyl)-guanine; ATSDR, 2006). Phase-II conjugation with GSH (GSTs) detoxifies both reactive metabolites. Maternal GSH is often reduced during pregnancy, raising fetal susceptibility.  
• Elimination: Exhalation of parent VC (~50 %), urinary thiodiglycolic acid from GSH conjugation (~30 %), minor biliary routes.  
Systemic availability for the conceptus at a maximum tolerated maternal dose (MTD) is therefore judged MEDIUM-to-HIGH.

2. Experimental developmental toxicity data  

2.1 Oral route (relevant to the question)  
• John et al., 1977 – Rats, gavage 0, 5, 50 mg kg-1 d-1 on GD 6-15. No maternal lethality. At 50 mg kg-1 slight maternal weight loss; pups: slight but significant ↓ mean fetal weight (-6 %), ↑ delayed ossification of sternebrae. No structural malformations.  
• Munafo et al., 1976 – Mice, gavage 0, 10, 100 mg kg-1 d-1 GD 6-15. Maternal toxicity at 100 mg kg-1 (↓ food consumption, mild hepatocellular necrosis). Fetuses: ↑ resorptions (2-fold), ↓ fetal weight, ↑ skeletal variations. No visceral or external malformations.  
• Data gap: No guideline-compliant two-generation oral study; only limited prenatal studies.

2.2 Inhalation studies (used qualitatively for read-across)  
• Thornton et al., 2002 – Rats exposed 0, 1, 10, 100, 1100 ppm (≈ 2.6, 26, 260, 2860 mg m-3) 6 h d-1 GD 6-20. Maternal toxicity at ≥ 100 ppm. Developmental effects (↓ fetal weight, ↑ skeletal variations) only at 1100 ppm; no malformations.  
• Ungvary et al., 1978 – Mice, 500 ppm 24 h d-1 GD 6-15 produced ↑ resorptions and post-implantation loss; no terata.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• VC is not a classical teratogen. Across species, structural malformations were not observed, even at maternally toxic doses.  
• Developmental toxicity (mainly growth retardation, increased resorptions, minor skeletal variations) occurs at or above doses that cause clear maternal toxicity.  
• No consistent evidence for specific organ embryopathy (e.g. heart, CNS).  

3. Predicted effects at a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) by daily oral gavage throughout gestation  

At the MTD the dam will show frank systemic toxicity (hepatic centrilobular necrosis, ↑ liver enzymes, weight loss). Fetal exposures will approach the kinetic saturation point of CYP2E1, resulting in high peak concentrations of CEO/CAA.

Likely developmental outcomes  
a) Growth delay – ↓ fetal body weight, delayed ossification.  
b) Embryo/fetal loss – ↑ early and late resorptions; possibly reduced litter size.  
c) Functional deficits post-natally – in a limited mouse study inhalation of 500 ppm caused subtle learning/memory deficits (Michalova et al., 1998). Although not confirmed orally, a similar effect cannot be ruled out.

Unlikely outcomes  
• Structural malformations (cleft palate, limb defects, neural tube defects) – not demonstrated under any exposure scenario.  
• Specific endocrine disruption leading to sexual development changes – no supporting data.

4. Mechanistic considerations  

4.1 Maternal-mediated mechanisms  
• Hypoxia & impaired nutrition: High VC doses depress maternal food intake and produce hepatotoxicity, limiting nutrient supply to the fetus.  
• Oxidative stress: CEO/CAA generate ROS in maternal liver; systemic oxidative stress markers (MDA, 8-OH-dG) are elevated and can indirectly affect the placenta.

4.2 Direct embryofetal mechanisms  
• DNA & protein adduct formation: CEO is a bifunctional electrophile; transplacental DNA adducts have been quantified in fetal liver (Swenberg et al., 1992). Increased DNA damage can trigger apoptosis, explaining resorptions.  
• Epigenetic modulation: In vitro exposure of mouse embryonic stem cells to CEO altered DNA methylation at imprinted loci (Cheng et al., 2010); this correlates with growth delay.  
• GSH depletion: Embryos have low GSH reserve; excessive CEO/CAA depletes fetal GSH, leading to redox imbalance and delayed ossification.  

No strong data support endocrine receptor binding; therefore teratogenicity via hormone disruption is unlikely.

5. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Effect: Maternal weight loss, hepatotoxicity  
– Classification: Adverse, potentially reversible if exposure stops before irreversible necrosis.  
– Clinical monitoring: Liver enzymes (ALT, AST, γ-GT), bilirubin.

Effect: ↓ fetal body weight / delayed ossification  
– Classification: Adverse if weight deficit ≥ 10 %; otherwise considered variations.  
– Reversibility: Largely reversible post-natally if nutritional status improves.  
– Monitoring: Neonatal anthropometrics, skeletal radiography if indicated.

Effect: ↑ resorptions / embryo-fetal death  
– Classification: Adverse.  
– Reversibility: Irreversible loss of conceptus.  
– Monitoring: Prenatal ultrasound; in humans, increased spontaneous abortion rate could be epidemiologically detected.

Effect: DNA adduct formation / mutagenicity in fetus  
– Classification: Potentially adverse (carcinogenic risk later in life).  
– Reversibility: DNA repair possible, but adduct miscoding mutations are permanent.  
– Monitoring: Not routinely feasible clinically; biomonitoring for N⁷-(2-oxoethyl)-guanine in cord blood possible in research settings.

Effect: Minor skeletal variations (supernumerary ribs, sternebrae delay)  
– Classification: Generally adaptive / transient; considered variations, not malformations.  
– Reversibility: Usually normalizes post-natally.  
– Monitoring: Paediatric radiographic follow-up if clinically warranted.

6. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability rating  
• Systemic availability (oral MTD, gestation): HIGH  
• Developmental toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
  – Rationale: Clear growth and viability effects at maternally toxic doses, but no consistent teratogenicity; weight-of-evidence shows a threshold related to maternal toxicity.

7. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Scarcity of oral prenatal studies using modern guideline protocols (OECD 414, 443).  
• Lack of detailed kinetic data in pregnant animals to establish maternal-fetal dosimetry.  
• Limited information on neurodevelopmental and immunological endpoints.  
• Human epidemiology mostly inhalation/exposure in PVC workers; confounding by co-exposures.

8. Overall conclusion  

Administering vinyl chloride at the maximum tolerated oral dose daily during gestation is expected to produce developmental toxicity in the form of fetal growth retardation and increased embryo-fetal loss, driven by maternal toxicity and direct fetal exposure to the reactive metabolites chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde. Structural malformations are unlikely, and the chemical is not considered a potent teratogen. Most effects (other than embryo-fetal death and potential mutagenic alterations) are potentially reversible or compensable post-natally. Careful clinical monitoring of maternal hepatic function and fetal growth parameters would be required were such an exposure conceivable, although use of vinyl chloride in pregnancy is in no way justified given its known carcinogenicity.
